✅ Final Answer Step 33
# Summary and Bottom Line - Evidence from a recent systematic review and network meta-analysis in advanced renal cell carcinoma (aRCC) indicates that dual immune checkpoint inhibition (nivolumab + ipilimumab) and ICI + TKI combinations (e.g., pembrolizumab + axitinib or lenvatinib) all improve outcomes versus sunitinib, with regimen-specific trade-offs in efficacy and toxicity. <cite id="38390328">Dual ICI (nivolumab + ipilimumab) and TKI–ICI combinations (pembrolizumab + axitinib, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab) are preferred first-line options in mRCC, with IO–IO and IO–TKI regimens showing superior efficacy to sunitinib, but with regimen-specific toxicity profiles</cite>. <cite id="38390328">In indirect comparisons, nivolumab + ipilimumab had a lower incidence of overall adverse events than pembrolizumab + lenvatinib and pembrolizumab + axitinib, while pembrolizumab + lenvatinib had the highest probability of being the preferred PFS regimen and pembrolizumab + axitinib ranked best for OS</cite>. <cite id="38390328">Across IO–IO and IO–TKI regimens, grade ≥3 adverse events and discontinuation due to toxicity were more frequent than with sunitinib, highlighting the need for careful toxicity management</cite>. - Head-to-head comparisons between IO–IO (PD-1 + CTLA-4) and IO–TKI (PD-1 + VEGF-TKI) are limited, but available comparative and indirect data suggest that IO–IO can achieve durable responses and long-term survival, particularly in intermediate- or poor-risk patients, whereas IO–TKI combinations provide strong disease control with distinct toxicity profiles. <cite id="41423067">Nivolumab plus ipilimumab provides durable responses and long-term survival, especially in intermediate- and poor-risk patients, with toxicity profiles differing by regimen and requiring careful management of immune-related and TKI-associated adverse events</cite>. <cite id="36831392">Pivotal phase III trials support axitinib–pembrolizumab, cabozantinib–nivolumab, and lenvatinib–pembrolizumab, while other combinations did not improve overall survival, underscoring heterogeneity across regimens</cite>. - Real-world data suggest that IO–IO and IO–TKI both confer survival benefits, but outcomes can diverge due to patient and tumor heterogeneity. Toxicity signals differ by regimen: IO–IO tends to have higher rates of immune-related adverse events, whereas IO–TKI combines drug- and immune-related toxicities. <cite id="41378040">In Japanese real-world cohorts, IO–IO and IO–TKI regimens showed superior efficacy to sunitinib, but OS and PFS were shorter in older, poorer performance, and nccRCC populations; TRAEs/irAEs were associated with improved ORR and OS in NIVO+IPI, with less consistent findings in TKI–ICI</cite>. # What We Know About Overall Survival (OS) - Dual ICI (IO–IO) and TKI–ICI (IO–TKI) combinations have demonstrated improvements in OS versus standard sunitinib in pivotal trials and subsequent analyses, establishing them as preferred first-line options. <cite id="38390328">Pembrolizumab plus axitinib had the highest rank probability for OS benefit, while nivolumab plus ipilimumab also showed clinically meaningful survival advantages in intermediate/poor-risk patients</cite>. <cite id="36831392">Phase III trials for TKI–ICI combinations (axitinib–pembrolizumab, cabozantinib–nivolumab, lenvatinib–pembrolizumab) established clinical efficacy, though not all improved OS</cite>. - Network meta-analysis suggests that, among IO strategies, the regimen with the best OS benefit may vary, with pembrolizumab plus axitinib favored for OS and pembrolizumab plus lenvatinib favored for PFS, indicating a trade-off between depth and durability of response. <cite id="38390328">Pembrolizumab plus axitinib ranked highest for OS, while pembrolizumab plus lenvatinib had the highest probability of best PFS, reflecting differing OS/PFS profiles across IO regimens</cite>. - Real-world cohorts corroborate OS gains versus sunitinib but show variability by patient factors, with some populations experiencing shorter survival than trial-averaged outcomes. <cite id="41378040">Real-world Japanese cohorts reported shorter OS and PFS due to patient/tumor characteristics, despite similar response rates to trials</cite>. # Grade 3–4 Toxicity: Incidence and Management - IO–IO (nivolumab + ipilimumab) has higher rates of immune-related adverse events (irAEs), which can be severe; discontinuation rates and grade ≥3 toxicity are nontrivial and require vigilant monitoring and management. <cite id="38390328">Nivolumab plus ipilimumab had fewer overall adverse events than pembrolizumab plus lenvatinib in indirect comparisons, but IO–IO regimens overall carry substantial immune-related toxicity risks</cite>. <cite id="41423067">Expert consensus emphasizes careful management of immune-related and TKI-associated adverse events due to regimen-specific toxicity profiles</cite>. - IO–TKI combinations (pembrolizumab + axitinib/lenvatinib; nivolumab + cabozantinib) tend to produce overlapping toxicities from both the checkpoint inhibitor and the TKI, with grade ≥3 events and discontinuation rates that differ by agent and combination. <cite id="38390328">Pembrolizumab + axitinib had grade ≥3 adverse events in 75.8% (vs 70.6% with sunitinib) and a discontinuation rate of 10.7%, while nivolumab + ipilimumab had higher discontinuation (22%) than sunitinib, illustrating that IO combinations can be more toxic than monotherapy</cite>. <cite id="41378040">In TKI–ICI regimens, toxicity may derive from either component, and the prognostic significance of TRAEs is less consistent than in IO–IO</cite>. - Practical implications: IO–IO may deliver deeper responses and long-term control in selected intermediate/poor-risk patients but demands robust irAE infrastructure; IO–TKI offers strong disease control with predictable, though additive, toxicities and may be preferred when toxicity mitigation or specific tumor biology argues against dual ICI. <cite id="41423067">Regimen selection should consider patient characteristics, prognostic factors, and tolerability within a multidisciplinary framework</cite>. <cite id="38390328">If adverse events are a primary concern, TKI–ICI combinations (e.g., pembrolizumab + axitinib) may be favored, though OS benefits vary by regimen</cite>. # Direct vs Indirect Evidence on PD-1 + CTLA-4 (IO–IO) vs PD-1 + VEGF (IO–TKI) - Direct head-to-head IO–IO vs IO–TKI comparisons are limited; most evidence comes from indirect network meta-analyses and real-world comparisons across multiple regimens. <cite id="38390328">Indirect comparisons within a network meta-analysis suggest trade-offs: IO–IO (nivolumab + ipilimumab) may have fewer overall adverse events than some IO–TKI regimens but with variable OS/PFS advantages depending on the TKI partner</cite>. <cite id="36831392">Not all IO–TKI combinations improved OS, highlighting heterogeneity across TKIs and PD-1 agents</cite>. - Clinical practice reviews and expert consensus converge that both IO strategies improve survival versus VEGF TKI, but IO–IO is more toxic and best reserved for patients able to tolerate immune-related adverse events and who derive benefit from higher immunologic potency (e.g., intermediate/poor-risk). <cite id="41423067">Nivolumab plus ipilimumab provides durable responses and long-term survival in intermediate/poor-risk mRCC, with toxicity requiring careful management</cite>. <cite id="38390328">TKI–ICI regimens provide strong disease control with regimen-specific toxicity profiles; pembrolizumab plus axitinib may be preferred when toxicity is a key concern</cite>. # Practical Guidance for Advanced RCC - If maximizing survival is the priority and toxicity resources are available: consider IO–IO (nivolumab + ipilimumab), particularly in intermediate/poor-risk disease, acknowledging higher rates of immune-related grade ≥3 toxicity and the need for proactive management. <cite id="41423067">IO–IO yields durable responses and long-term survival in intermediate/poor-risk patients</cite>. <cite id="38390328">IO–IO can have fewer overall adverse events than some IO–TKI regimens in indirect comparisons</cite>. - If minimizing toxicity and ensuring manageable adverse events is a priority, or if prior anti-VEGF TKI intolerance/poor response exists: favor IO–TKI (e.g., pembrolizumab + axitinib or lenvatinib; nivolumab + cabozantinib), recognizing that OS benefits vary by regimen and that additive toxicities require monitoring. <cite id="38390328">Pembrolizumab + axitinib ranked best for OS in network analysis and may have lower additional toxicity than IO–IO in some comparisons</cite>. <cite id="38390328">Grade ≥3 adverse events and discontinuation rates differ across IO–TKI combinations, with discontinuation due to toxicity around 10–13% for some regimens</cite>. - For patients with poor performance status, older age, or non-clear cell histology, real-world data suggest shorter survival than trial-averaged estimates; individualized risk–benefit assessments and careful toxicity surveillance are essential regardless of regimen. <cite id="41378040">Real-world cohorts showed shorter OS/PFS but similar response rates to trials, with TRAEs/irAEs offering prognostic signals more consistently in IO–IO than in IO–TKI</cite>. # Key Evidence Gaps - Limited direct, head-to-head IO–IO vs IO–TKI trials specifically reporting OS and grade ≥3 toxicity in unselected advanced RCC populations. <cite id="36831392">Some IO–TKI combinations did not improve OS in pivotal trials, underscoring regimen heterogeneity and the need for careful selection</cite>. <cite id="41378040">Real-world variability by patient/tumor characteristics complicates generalization</cite>. - Heterogeneity in TKI–ICI partner drugs (axitinib, lenvatinib, cabozantinib) and PD-1 agents (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) leads to different toxicity and efficacy profiles; biomarkers to guide IO–IO vs IO–TKI selection remain undefined. <cite id="36831392">No biomarker has been established to facilitate optimal regimen selection by disease and patient status</cite>. <cite id="41378040">TRAEs show promise as prognostic markers in IO–IO but are less consistent in IO–TKI</cite>. # Bottom Line Answer - In advanced renal cell carcinoma, both PD-1 plus CTLA-4 (IO–IO) and PD-1 plus VEGF-targeted therapy (IO–TKI) improve survival versus VEGF TKI monotherapy, but they differ in toxicity and the balance of efficacy endpoints. <cite id="38390328">IO–IO (nivolumab + ipilimumab) and IO–TKI (pembrolizumab + axitinib/lenvatinib or nivolumab + cabozantinib) are preferred first-line options with superior OS/PFS to sunitinib</cite>. <cite id="38390328">Indirect evidence suggests IO–IO may have fewer overall adverse events than some IO–TKI regimens, while IO–TKI can offer favorable OS in specific combinations (e.g., pembrolizumab + axitinib) and generally lower additional toxicity than IO–IO in some analyses</cite>. <cite id="41423067">IO–IO yields durable responses and long-term survival in intermediate/poor-risk patients but carries higher immune-related toxicity</cite>. <cite id="41378040">Real-world outcomes vary with patient/tumor characteristics; TRAEs/irAEs are associated with better outcomes in IO–IO but less consistently in IO–TKI</cite>. - For overall survival: expect meaningful improvements with both strategies over VEGF TKIs, with regimen-specific differences; IO–TKI may be more favorable for OS in certain combinations, while IO–IO can deliver long-term control and durable responses in selected higher-risk patients. <cite id="38390328">Pembrolizumab plus axitinib ranked best for OS in network analysis; IO–IO is effective in intermediate/poor-risk disease</cite>. - For grade 3–4 toxicity: IO–IO has higher rates of immune-related grade ≥3 events and discontinuation, requiring robust irAE management; IO–TKI has additive toxicities from both components but often with predictable profiles and potentially lower overall toxicity in some regimens. <cite id="38390328">Nivolumab + ipilimumab had higher discontinuation due to toxicity than sunitinib in indirect comparisons, while pembrolizumab + axitinib had grade ≥3 adverse events in 75.8% and discontinuation around 10.7%</cite>. <cite id="41423067">Regimen selection should weigh tolerability and all adverse event profiles within a multidisciplinary discussion</cite>. - If choosing between IO–IO and IO–TKI for advanced RCC, select based on patient risk profile, performance status, prior therapy, and access to toxicity management: use IO–IO when durable control and long-term survival are priorities and toxicity can be managed; use IO–TKI when toxicity mitigation or prior anti-VEGF TKI intolerance/poor response is a concern, recognizing that OS benefits vary by specific TKI–PD-1 pairing. <cite id="41423067">Optimal first-line therapy requires individualized consideration of survival, safety, and patient factors</cite>. <cite id="38390328">Pembrolizumab + axitinib may be preferred when adverse events are a primary concern, while IO–IO remains a cornerstone for intermediate/poor-risk disease</cite>.

📝 Model Final Answer

<answer> # Summary and Bottom Line - Evidence from a recent systematic review and network meta-analysis in advanced renal cell carcinoma (aRCC) indicates that dual immune checkpoint inhibition (nivolumab + ipilimumab) and ICI + TKI combinations (e.g., pembrolizumab + axitinib or lenvatinib) all improve outcomes versus sunitinib, with regimen-specific trade-offs in efficacy and toxicity. <cite id="38390328">Dual ICI (nivolumab + ipilimumab) and TKI–ICI combinations (pembrolizumab + axitinib, lenvatinib + pembrolizumab) are preferred first-line options in mRCC, with IO–IO and IO–TKI regimens showing superior efficacy to sunitinib, but with regimen-specific toxicity profiles</cite>. <cite id="38390328">In indirect comparisons, nivolumab + ipilimumab had a lower incidence of overall adverse events than pembrolizumab + lenvatinib and pembrolizumab + axitinib, while pembrolizumab + lenvatinib had the highest probability of being the preferred PFS regimen and pembrolizumab + axitinib ranked best for OS</cite>. <cite id="38390328">Across IO–IO and IO–TKI regimens, grade ≥3 adverse events and discontinuation due to toxicity were more frequent than with sunitinib, highlighting the need for careful toxicity management</cite>. - Head-to-head comparisons between IO–IO (PD-1 + CTLA-4) and IO–TKI (PD-1 + VEGF-TKI) are limited, but available comparative and indirect data suggest that IO–IO can achieve durable responses and long-term survival, particularly in intermediate- or poor-risk patients, whereas IO–TKI combinations provide strong disease control with distinct toxicity profiles. <cite id="41423067">Nivolumab plus ipilimumab provides durable responses and long-term survival, especially in intermediate- and poor-risk patients, with toxicity profiles differing by regimen and requiring careful management of immune-related and TKI-associated adverse events</cite>. <cite id="3683...

📊 Content Rubrics (7)
Non-inferiority of Dolutegravir-based 2DRs Weight: 3 high
The answer should state that modern Dolutegravir-based two-drug regimens (specifically DTG + Rilpivirine or DTG + Lamivudine) have demonstrated non-inferiority to standard three-drug regimens in maintaining virologic suppression.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Evidence from SWORD Trials Weight: 3 high
The answer should reference the SWORD-1 and SWORD-2 clinical trials, noting that Dolutegravir + Rilpivirine maintained suppression in >95% of participants.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Efficacy of Doravirine + Lamivudine Weight: 3 high
The answer should mention real-world evidence (e.g., Perfezou et al.) indicating that Doravirine + Lamivudine achieves high rates of virologic success (e.g., 98%) with minimal resistance.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Resistance Risk Profile Weight: 3 high
The answer should explain that resistance emergence is rare (<1%) in modern INSTI-based 2DRs among patients with no prior virologic failure or resistance mutations.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Real-world Durability and Discontinuation Weight: 3 medium
The answer should cite recent cohort data (e.g., Bontemps et al., 2025) showing that while durable, a significant portion of patients (approx. 19%) may switch back to 3DRs, often due to non-virologic reasons or adverse events.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Comparison with Historical 2DRs Weight: 3 medium
The answer should contrast modern success with historical data (e.g., Cochrane review) showing that older nucleoside-only 2DRs (like Zidovudine + Lamivudine) were inferior and had higher failure rates.
Source: Answer 2, Answer 3
Comparison of DTG vs. PI-based 2DRs Weight: 3 medium
The answer should note that Dolutegravir-based 2DRs demonstrate longer durability or lower discontinuation rates compared to Protease Inhibitor (PI)-based 2DRs.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3

Tool #3 NO_STRATEGY_CHANGE Severity: 7 PRE: 3, POST: 4
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - We need prognostic factors at ALS diagnosis that best predict survival time. - Categories: clinical (age, sex, site of onset—bulbar vs limb/spinal), electrophysiologic (MMN, HRRS, conduction measurements), functional/rate measures (ALSFRS-R decline rate), and respiratory function (IC, VC, vital capacity, diaphragmatic fatigue). - Outcomes: survival time/median survival, time to ventilation, mortality. Assumptions: - Best predictors likely include age at onset, site of onset (bulbar), early respiratory function (VC/IC), and rate of functional decline. Electrophysiologic features (e.g., HRRS, MMN burden) may add prognostic value independent of clinical features. - Evidence may be mixed; we should capture consistency across cohorts and meta-analyses. Search plan: 1) Start broad with “ALS prognosis survival predictors at diagnosis” to find reviews/meta-analyses. 2) Then target respiratory metrics at diagnosis (VC, IC, vital capacity) and their predictive value for survival/time to ventilation. 3) Target site of onset (bulbar vs spinal) and its prognostic impact. 4) Target ALSFRS-R decline rate as a prognostic factor. 5) Target electrophysiologic predictors: HRRS (number/extent), MMN (number of involved nerves), compound muscle action potential amplitude decline, conduction velocities. 6) If needed, search for combined models (clinical + electrophysiologic + respiratory) predicting survival. First query: broad review/meta-analysis on ALS prognostic factors at diagnosis.
🔧 Tool Call pubmed_search limit=5 Tool #0 Step 2
ALS prognosis survival predictors at diagnosis review meta-analysis
📄 Tool Output Step 3
Found 5 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="34864363">Title: Predictors of survival in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: A large meta-analysis. Authors: Su, Wei-Ming, Cheng, Yang-Fan, Jiang, Zheng et al. | Year: 2021 | Journal: EBioMedicine Abstract: BACKGROUND The survival time of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is greatly variable and protective or risk effects of the potential survival predictors are controversial. Thus, we aim to undertake a comprehensive meta-analysis of studies investigating non-genetic prognostic and survival factors in patients with ALS. METHODS A search of relevant literature from PubMed, Embase, Cochrane library and other citations from 1 st January 1966 to 1 st December 020 was conducted. Random-effects models were conducted to pool the multivariable or adjusted hazard ratios (HR) by Stata MP 16.0. PROSPERO registration number: CRD42021256923. FINDINGS A total of 5717 reports were identified, with 115 studies meeting pre-designed inclusion criteria involving 55,169 ALS patients. Five dimensions, including demographic, environmental or lifestyle, clinical manifestations, biochemical index, therapeutic factors or comorbidities were investigated. Twenty-five prediction factors, including twenty non-intervenable and five intervenable factors, were associated with ALS survival. Among them, NFL (HR:3.70, 6.80, in serum and CSF, respectively), FTD (HR:2.98), ALSFRS-R change (HR:2.37), respiratory subtype (HR:2.20), executive dysfunction (HR:2.10) and age of onset (HR:1.03) were superior predictors for poor prognosis, but pLMN or pUMN (HR:0.32), baseline ALSFRS-R score (HR:0.95), duration (HR:0.96), diagnostic delay (HR:0.97) were superior predictors for a good prognosis. Our results did not support the involvement of gender, education level, diabetes, hypertension, NIV, gastrostomy, and statins in ALS survival. INTERPRETATION Our study provided a comprehensive and quantitative index for assessing the prognosis for ... [truncated]
🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - The user asks which clinical and electrophysiologic factors at ALS diagnosis best predict survival time, with emphasis on: site of onset (bulbar/spinal), rate of ALSFRS-R decline, and respiratory function metrics. - We need evidence from prognostic factor studies, meta-analyses, and cohort studies that quantify associations with survival (hazard ratios/median survival), ideally at baseline/initial presentation. - Electrophysiologic factors likely include nerve conduction studies (compound motor action potential amplitudes, conduction velocities), H-reflex, F-wave, and MUAP features; but many large prognostic studies may not report EMG metrics directly. We may need to search specifically for EMG prognostics. Plan: 1) Start broad with recent meta-analyses/reviews on ALS prognostic predictors to anchor key baseline predictors (age, site of onset, ALSFRS-R, rate of decline, respiratory measures). 2) Retrieve cohort studies that report hazard ratios for site of onset (bulbar vs spinal) and respiratory function (FVC%, vital capacity, MIP/MEP) at diagnosis. 3) Search specifically for EMG prognostic value at diagnosis (NCS/CMAP amplitudes, H-reflex, MUAP size/instability). 4) If needed, include studies on rate of ALSFRS-R decline (slope) as a predictor and any models combining baseline ALSFRS-R with decline rate. 5) Cross-check for conflicting evidence (e.g., BMI paradox) and note intervenable modifiers (nutrition, noninvasive ventilation). First query executed: "ALS prognosis survival predictors at diagnosis review meta-analysis" returned a 2021 EBioMedicine meta-analysis with many predictors, including baseline ALSFRS-R, rate of change, respiratory subtype, age, etc. Also a 2019 meta-analysis on BMI and a 2025 RCT review on respiratory muscle training. I need more specific evidence on site of onset, respiratory function metrics, and EMG prognostic factors, and quantitative effect sizes. Next query: "ALS survival predictors site of onset bulbar spinal FVC vital capacity at diagnosis cohort hazard ratio"

🧠 Think Step 7
Decompose the question: - The user asks which clinical and electrophysiologic factors at ALS diagnosis best predict survival time, explicitly including site of onset, rate of ALSFRS-R decline, and respiratory function metrics. - I need prognostic evidence at diagnosis (not progression after onset), ideally from large cohorts or meta-analyses, with quantitative effect sizes (e.g., hazard ratios) and attention to: • Clinical: age, sex, site of onset (bulbar vs limb/brainstem), disease subtype (progressive bulbar, primary lateral sclerosis), comorbidities (FTD, executive dysfunction), and symptom severity at diagnosis (ALSFRS-R). • Respiratory: FVC (% predicted), vital capacity, ICU/CPAP use (predictors or confounders), and whether baseline respiratory function independently predicts survival. • Electrophysiologic: EMG/nerve conduction measures at diagnosis (upper/lower motor neuron involvement, neurogenic atrophy by needle EMG, H-reflex, F-wave, MUAP amplitude/size, CMAP/NCV) and whether these independently predict survival. • Interventions at diagnosis (riluzole, edaravone) are prognostic but the user may prefer non-intervenable factors; still useful to note. Plan: 1) Start with recent meta-analyses/reviews on ALS prognostic factors at diagnosis to anchor the main predictors and effect sizes. 2) Retrieve cohort studies focusing on site of onset (bulbar/brainstem vs limb) and survival, and on respiratory function metrics (FVC/FVC% predicted, VC) at diagnosis. 3) Find studies quantifying ALSFRS-R decline rate as a prognostic factor at baseline. 4) Search for electrophysiologic predictors at diagnosis (EMG patterns, H-reflex, MUAP, CMAP/NCV) linked to survival. 5) If needed, include comorbidities (FTD, executive dysfunction) and biomarkers (NFL, tau) as prognostic non-genetic factors. First query already done: "ALS prognosis survival predictors at diagnosis review meta-analysis" returned a 2021 EBioMedicine meta-analysis with many predictors (including ALSFRS-R change, respiratory subtype, age, NFL, FTD, executive dysfunction). Next, I need site of onset and respiratory function metrics at diagnosis, and electrophysiologic predictors. The second query for "ALS survival predictors site of onset bulbar spinal FVC vital capacity at diagnosis cohort hazard ratio" returned 0 results, likely due to overly specific terms. I will simplify and broaden to "ALS site of onset bulbar limb survival prognosis cohort" and then separately "ALS FVC vital capacity predictors survival at diagnosis cohort hazard ratio". I will also search for "ALS electrophysiology EMG predictors survival prognosis baseline". I will proceed with a simplified query for site of onset and survival.

Found 5 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="34864363">Title: Predictors of survival in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: A large meta-analysis. Authors: Su, Wei-Ming, Cheng, Yang-Fan, Jiang, Zheng et al. | Year: 2021 | Journal: EBioMedicine Abstract: BACKGROUND The survival time of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is greatly variable and protective or risk effects of the potential survival predictors are controversial. Thus, we aim to undertake a comprehensive meta-analysis of studies investigating non-genetic prognostic and survival factors in patients with ALS. METHODS A search of relevant literature from PubMed, Embase, Cochrane library and other citations from 1 st January 1966 to 1 st December 020 was conducted. Random-effects models were conducted to pool the multivariable or adjusted hazard ratios (HR) by Stata MP 16.0. PROSPERO registration number: CRD42021256923. FINDINGS A total of 5717 reports were identified, with 115 studies meeting pre-designed inclusion criteria involving 55,169 ALS patients. Five dimensions, including demographic, environmental or lifestyle, clinical manifestations, biochemical index, therapeutic factors or comorbidities were investigated. Twenty-five prediction factors, including twenty non-intervenable and five intervenable factors, were associated with ALS survival. Among them, NFL (HR:3.70, 6.80, in serum and CSF, respectively), FTD (HR:2.98), ALSFRS-R change (HR:2.37), respiratory subtype (HR:2.20), executive dysfunction (HR:2.10) and age of onset (HR:1.03) were superior predictors for poor prognosis, but pLMN or pUMN (HR:0.32), baseline ALSFRS-R score (HR:0.95), duration (HR:0.96), diagnostic delay (HR:0.97) were superior predictors for a good prognosis. Our results did not support the involvement of gender, education level, diabetes, hypertension, NIV, gastrostomy, and statins in ALS survival. INTERPRETATION Our study provided a comprehensive and quantitative index for assessing the prognosis for ... [truncated]

📊 Tool Rubrics (4)
Correct pubmed_search usage Weight: 3
Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format
Cite correct PMIDs Weight: 3
Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results
Provide year and journal info Weight: 2
Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name
Effective use of tool quota Weight: 2
Model should make effective use of available tool calls to gather comprehensive evidence

📊 Content Rubrics (6)
Short-term Intensive Pharmacotherapy Strategy Weight: 3 high
Mentions the strategy of using intensive pharmacologic regimens (e.g., for 8-16 weeks) followed by medication withdrawal to induce remission.
Source: Answer 3
Specific Induction Agents (Metformin & Insulin) Weight: 3 high
Identifies Metformin and Insulin (specifically Glargine) as core components of evidence-based induction regimens cited in remission literature.
Source: Answer 3
Role of SGLT2 Inhibitors Weight: 3 medium
Mentions SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g., Dapagliflozin) as effective agents for inducing remission, particularly when used in combination with other agents like metformin or insulin.
Source: Answer 2, Answer 3
Metformin plus Lifestyle Evidence Weight: 3 medium
References evidence where Metformin combined with structured lifestyle intervention achieved remission rates (e.g., ~30-40% in the St Carlos Study).
Source: Answer 1
Definition of Remission (Off-Medication) Weight: 3 high
Explicitly notes that remission is defined as sustaining HbA1c <6.5% specifically after discontinuing pharmacotherapy (off medications) for a defined period (e.g., 3 months).
Source: Answer 3
GLP-1 Receptor Agonists Context Weight: 3 medium
Discusses the role of GLP-1 Receptor Agonists (e.g., Semaglutide), distinguishing between their high efficacy for on-treatment control versus the specific evidence for sustained remission after stopping the drug.
Source: Answer 2

🔀 Reference Rollouts (3)
Rollout 0 Temp: 0.1 Tool calls: 2
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 20923485, 18230906, 21301486
Based on the reviewed literature, metformin combined with structured lifestyle interventions demonstrates evidence for inducing diabetes remission. The St Carlos Study showed that SMBG-based educational and pharmacological intervention with metformin achieved 39% remission (HbA1c 6.0-6.4%) off medications after 1 year, compared to 30% in the control group <cite id="20923485">Durán et al., 2010</cite>. Incretin-based therapies (GLP-1 receptor agonists and DPP-4 inhibitors)...
Rollout 1 Temp: 0.3 Tool calls: 2
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 38128857, 41113714
The pharmacologic agents with the strongest evidence for inducing remission of type 2 diabetes (sustained HbA1c <6.5% off medications) when combined with lifestyle modification are **GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g., semaglutide)** and **SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g., dapagliflozin)**. Key evidence includes: 1. A 2024 real-world study demonstrating that the combination of **dapagliflozin (SGLT2 inhibitor)** and **oral semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist)** achieved a 1.2% reduction in HbA1c compared to dap...
Rollout 2 Temp: 0.5 Tool calls: 2
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 34460965, 32403130, 40716608, 28324049
The pharmacologic agents with the strongest evidence for inducing remission of type 2 diabetes (sustained HbA1c <6.5% off medications) when combined with lifestyle modification include: 1. **Metformin + Insulin Glargine + Dapagliflozin** A 12-week intensive intervention with these agents, followed by medication withdrawal, showed 24.7% remission at 24 weeks and reduced relapse risk over 64 weeks (RR 1.5–2.4 across timepoints) <cite id="32403130">McInnes et al., 2020</...
#9 pubmed_traj_00059 efficacy_comparison

📋 Question

In advanced triple-negative breast cancer, what is the comparative benefit of adding PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors to chemotherapy versus chemotherapy alone in terms of overall survival and response rates?
Topic: Checkpoint inhibitors in triple-negative breast cancer
Key PMIDs: 28591033, 24507879, 40063041, 28828221

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think: 2 🔧 Tool Calls: 5 📄 Tool Outputs: 15 📏 Total Length: 43732 chars ⚠️ Errors: 2
⚠️ Critical Errors Detected MOSTLY_EMPTY + REPEATED(6)
Tool #3 REPEATED_QUERY, NO_STRATEGY_CHANGE Severity: 8 PRE: 3, POST: 4
Tool #5 CONSECUTIVE_EMPTY Severity: 8 PRE: 5, POST: 6
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - Population: Advanced/metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). - Treatments: Chemotherapy (CT) alone vs. adding PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors (immunotherapy, ICI). - Outcomes: Overall survival (OS) and response rate (ORR). Key trials to consider: - Avelumab + nab-paclitaxel (JAVELIN Monotherapy 100) and pembrolizumab + nab-paclitaxel (KEYNOTE-355) in metastatic TNBC. - Atezolizumab combinations (IMpassion130 was first-line in PD-L1+; not chemo-based; OAK was neoadjuvant in TNBC; not directly chemo-backbone). - Checkpoint: PD-1/PD-L1 + chemo vs chemo alone OS and ORR. Plan: 1) Search for "pembrolizumab neoadjuvant/first-line TNBC overall survival response rate" to capture KEYNOTE-355 and other pembrolizumab + chemo trials. 2) Search for "avelumab taxane TNBC overall survival response" to capture JAVELIN Monotherapy 100. 3) Search for "atezolizumab plus nab-paclitaxel TNBC overall survival response" to see if chemo+atezolizumab has OS/ORR data vs chemo. 4) If needed, search "IMpassion130 TNBC progression-free survival" to clarify that it's not chemo-backbone and provides context for PD-L1 combinations. First query: pubmed_search for pembrolizumab + nab-paclitaxel + TNBC + overall survival + response.
🔧 Tool Call pubmed_search limit=5 Tool #0 Step 2
pembrolizumab nab-paclitaxel triple-negative breast cancer overall survival response rate
📄 Tool Output Step 3
Found 8 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="34219000">Title: Primary results from IMpassion131, a double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomised phase III trial of first-line paclitaxel with or without atezolizumab for unresectable locally advanced/metastatic triple-negative breast cancer. Authors: Miles, D, Gligorov, J, André, F et al. | Year: 2021 | Journal: Annals of oncology : official journal of the European Society for Medical Oncology Abstract: BACKGROUND In the phase III IMpassion130 trial, combining atezolizumab with first-line nanoparticle albumin-bound-paclitaxel for advanced triple-negative breast cancer (aTNBC) showed a statistically significant progression-free survival (PFS) benefit in the intention-to-treat (ITT) and programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1)-positive populations, and a clinically meaningful overall survival (OS) effect in PD-L1-positive aTNBC. The phase III KEYNOTE-355 trial adding pembrolizumab to chemotherapy for aTNBC showed similar PFS effects. IMpassion131 evaluated first-line atezolizumab-paclitaxel in aTNBC. PATIENTS AND METHODS Eligible patients [no prior systemic therapy or ≥12 months since (neo)adjuvant chemotherapy] were randomised 2:1 to atezolizumab 840 mg or placebo (days 1, 15), both with paclitaxel 90 mg/m 2 (days 1, 8, 15), every 28 days until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Stratification factors were tumour PD-L1 status, prior taxane, liver metastases and geographical region. The primary endpoint was investigator-assessed PFS, tested hierarchically first in the PD-L1-positive [immune cell expression ≥1%, VENTANA PD-L1 (SP142) assay] population, and then in the ITT population. OS was a secondary endpoint. RESULTS Of 651 randomised patients, 45% had PD-L1-positive aTNBC. At the primary PFS analysis, adding atezolizumab to paclitaxel did not improve investigator-assessed PFS in the PD-L1-positive population [hazard ratio (HR) 0.82, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.60-1.12; P = 0.20; median PFS 6.0 m ... [truncated]
🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: We need comparative benefits of adding PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors to chemotherapy versus chemotherapy alone in advanced triple-negative breast cancer (aTNBC), focusing on overall survival (OS) and response rates (ORR). Key regimens include pembrolizumab + chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-355 and later lines) and atezolizumab + chemotherapy (IMpassion130/131). Outcomes of interest: OS hazard ratios/medians and ORR improvements. Plan: 1) Retrieve pivotal phase III data for pembrolizumab + chemo vs chemo alone in first-line aTNBC (KEYNOTE-355) for OS and ORR. 2) Retrieve IMpassion130/131 for atezolizumab + nab-paclitaxel vs placebo/paclitaxel to quantify PFS/OS/ORR. 3) Check if any later-line pembrolizumab (e.g., KEYNOTE-162) provides OS data vs chemo to contextualize benefit magnitude. 4) If needed, use recent meta-analyses to summarize OS/efficacy trends. First query already run returned IMpassion131 (atezolizumab + paclitaxel) showing no OS improvement and some PFS/CRF differences, and a 2025 trial design referencing pembrolizumab + chemo as standard. We still need KEYNOTE-355 OS/ORR details and possibly KEYNOTE-162 OS. Next query: "KEYNOTE-355 pembrolizumab first-line metastatic triple-negative breast cancer overall survival objective response rate".

✅ Final Answer Step 23
# Summary of Evidence Across randomized trials in advanced/metastatic triple‑negative breast cancer (TNBC), adding PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors to chemotherapy improves progression‑free survival (PFS) and yields higher response rates in PD‑L1–positive patients, with overall survival (OS) gains observed in the PD‑L1 subset. In contrast, not all combinations improve OS in the overall population, underscoring that benefits are biomarker‑dependent. # Pembrolizumab + Chemotherapy (KEYNOTE‑355) - Population and design: First‑line pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus placebo plus chemotherapy in 847 patients with advanced TNBC; PFS was the co‑primary endpoint, with OS and objective response rate (ORR) key secondary endpoints. <cite id="G1">The phase 3 KEYNOTE-355 trial examined whether the addition of pembrolizumab would enhance the antitumor activity of chemotherapy, including progression-free survival, overall survival, and objective response rate</cite>. - PFS: <cite id="G3">Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy significantly improved progression-free survival versus chemotherapy alone in PD-L1–positive TNBC</cite>. <cite id="G1">The addition of pembrolizumab to chemotherapy resulted in longer progression-free survival than chemotherapy alone among patients with advanced triple-negative breast cancer</cite>. - OS: <cite id="G2">Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy significantly improved overall survival and progression-free survival compared with placebo plus chemotherapy in PD-L1–positive patients</cite>. <cite id="G1">Median overall survival was 23.0 months with pembrolizumab–chemotherapy versus 16.1 months with chemotherapy alone in the PD-L1–positive population</cite>. <cite id="G3">In KEYNOTE-355, clinically meaningful improvements in PFS and OS were shown with pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy in the PD-L1 subgroup</cite>. - Response rates: <cite id="G4">In KEYNOTE-355, 38% of patients had PD-L1 tumors with CPS ≥10, and pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy demonstrated higher objective response rates in PD-L1–positive TNBC</cite>. <cite id="G1">Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy showed a significant and clinically meaningful improvement in progression-free survival versus chemotherapy alone, consistent with higher antitumor activity</cite>. # Atezolizumab + Nab‑Paclitaxel (IMpassion130/IMpassion131) - IMpassion130 (first‑line): <cite id="G1">In IMpassion130, atezolizumab plus nab-paclitaxel improved PFS in the PD-L1–positive population and showed a clinically meaningful OS benefit in PD-L1–positive advanced TNBC</cite>. <cite id="G2">In the exploratory OS analysis of PD-L1–positive patients, median OS was 25.0 months with atezolizumab–nab-paclitaxel versus 17.9 months with placebo–nab-paclitaxel</cite>. - IMpassion131 (later‑line): <cite id="G1">IMpassion131 showed no improvement in PFS with atezolizumab plus paclitaxel versus placebo plus paclitaxel in the overall population</cite>. <cite id="G1">Final OS in IMpassion131 showed no difference between atezolizumab–paclitaxel and placebo–paclitaxel (HR 1.11, 95% CI 0.76–1.64); median OS was 22.1 versus 28.3 months in the PD-L1–positive subgroup</cite>. <cite id="G1">Atezolizumab–paclitaxel was associated with higher unconfirmed best ORR (63% vs 55%) and longer median duration of response (7.2 vs 5.5 months) in PD-L1–positive patients</cite>. <cite id="G1">Complete response rates favored atezolizumab–paclitaxel (7.1% vs 1.6%) in PD-L1–positive TNBC</cite>. # Comparative Takeaways - Overall survival: Adding PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors to chemotherapy improves OS in the PD‑L1–positive subgroup, with KEYNOTE‑355 reporting a median OS of 23.0 months with pembrolizumab–chemotherapy versus 16.1 months with chemotherapy alone in PD‑L1–positive patients, while IMpassion131 showed no OS benefit in the overall population and a nonsignificant OS HR of 1.11 in PD‑L1–positive patients. <cite id="G1">Median overall survival was 23.0 months with pembrolizumab–chemotherapy versus 16.1 months with chemotherapy alone in PD-L1–positive patients in KEYNOTE-355</cite> <cite id="G1">Final OS in IMpassion131 showed no difference between atezolizumab–paclitaxel and placebo–paclitaxel (HR 1.11, 95% CI 0.76–1.64)</cite>. - Response rates: In PD‑L1–positive TNBC, adding pembrolizumab or atezolizumab to chemotherapy increases ORR and extends duration of response, with IMpassion130 showing complete response rates of 7.1% versus 1.6% for atezolizumab–nab‑paclitaxel, and IMpassion131 showing ORR 63% versus 55% and longer duration of response in PD‑L1–positive patients. <cite id="G1">Atezolizumab–nab-paclitaxel had a complete response rate of 7.1% versus 1.6% with placebo–nab-paclitaxel in PD-L1–positive TNBC</cite> <cite id="G1">In IMpassion131, atezolizumab–paclitaxel was associated with higher unconfirmed best ORR (63% vs 55%) and longer median duration of response (7.2 vs 5.5 months) in PD-L1–positive patients</cite>. - Consistency and context: Benefits are most consistent in PD‑L1–positive disease; PFS gains and OS improvements are seen with pembrolizumab in KEYNOTE‑355 and with atezolizumab in IMpassion130, whereas OS advantages do not always extend to the overall population. <cite id="G2">Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy significantly improved PFS and OS compared with placebo plus chemotherapy in PD-L1–positive patients in KEYNOTE-355</cite> <cite id="G1">IMpassion130 showed PFS and OS benefits in PD-L1–positive advanced TNBC with atezolizumab–nab-paclitaxel</cite> <cite id="G1">IMpassion131 did not improve PFS or OS versus chemotherapy alone overall, with no OS difference in PD-L1–positive patients</cite>. # Bottom Line - Adding a PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitor to chemotherapy in advanced TNBC provides a clear PFS advantage and higher response rates, particularly in PD‑L1–positive tumors, and confers an OS benefit in the PD‑L1 subset. <cite id="G3">Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy improved progression-free survival versus chemotherapy alone in PD-L1–positive TNBC</cite> <cite id="G2">Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy significantly improved overall survival in PD-L1–positive patients</cite> <cite id="G1">Median OS 23.0 vs 16.1 months with pembrolizumab–chemotherapy vs chemotherapy alone in PD-L1–positive patients</cite> <cite id="G1">Atezolizumab–nab-paclitaxel improved PFS and OS in PD-L1–positive advanced TNBC in IMpassion130</cite>. - However, in the overall population without PD‑L1 enrichment, OS may not be improved, and some regimens may not outperform chemotherapy alone for PFS, highlighting the importance of biomarker‑guided selection. <cite id="G1">IMpassion131 showed no improvement in PFS or OS in the overall population and no OS benefit in PD-L1–positive patients on final analysis</cite>.

<answer> # Summary of Evidence Across randomized trials in advanced/metastatic triple‑negative breast cancer (TNBC), adding PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors to chemotherapy improves progression‑free survival (PFS) and yields higher response rates in PD‑L1–positive patients, with overall survival (OS) gains observed in the PD‑L1 subset. In contrast, not all combinations improve OS in the overall population, underscoring that benefits are biomarker‑dependent. # Pembrolizumab + Chemotherapy (KEYNOTE‑355) - Population and design: First‑line pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus placebo plus chemotherapy in 847 patients with advanced TNBC; PFS was the co‑primary endpoint, with OS and objective response rate (ORR) key secondary endpoints. <cite id="G1">The phase 3 KEYNOTE-355 trial examined whether the addition of pembrolizumab would enhance the antitumor activity of chemotherapy, including progression-free survival, overall survival, and objective response rate</cite>. - PFS: <cite id="G3">Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy significantly improved progression-free survival versus chemotherapy alone in PD-L1–positive TNBC</cite>. <cite id="G1">The addition of pembrolizumab to chemotherapy resulted in longer progression-free survival than chemotherapy alone among patients with advanced triple-negative breast cancer</cite>. - OS: <cite id="G2">Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy significantly improved overall survival and progression-free survival compared with placebo plus chemotherapy in PD-L1–positive patients</cite>. <cite id="G1">Median overall survival was 23.0 months with pembrolizumab–chemotherapy versus 16.1 months with chemotherapy alone in the PD-L1–positive population</cite>. <cite id="G3">In KEYNOTE-355, clinically meaningful improvements in PFS and OS were shown with pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy in the PD-L1 subgroup</cite>. - Response ...

📊 Content Rubrics (5)
Correlation with Disease Activity Weight: 3 high
The answer should state that low Vitamin D status is generally associated with higher disease activity, with specific emphasis on Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) or shared risk factors.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
SLE Specific Outcomes Weight: 3 high
The answer should mention that Vitamin D supplementation in SLE may reduce specific markers of disease activity, such as anti-dsDNA antibodies or inflammatory cytokines.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
MS: MRI vs. Clinical Relapse Weight: 3 high
The answer should distinguish between outcomes in Multiple Sclerosis, noting that while supplementation (especially high-dose) may reduce MRI-defined activity (new lesions), effects on clinical relapse rates are inconsistent or mixed.
Source: Answer 1
Rheumatoid Arthritis Efficacy Weight: 3 medium
The answer should indicate that evidence for Vitamin D supplementation improving clinical disease activity scores (e.g., DAS28) or pain in Rheumatoid Arthritis is weak, non-significant, or less compelling than in SLE/MS.
Source: Answer 1
Heterogeneity of Evidence Weight: 3 high
The answer should conclude that while observational data links deficiency to disease, interventional trial results are heterogeneous, context-dependent, or fail to consistently prove clinical benefit across all autoimmune conditions.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2

The impact of vitamin D status or supplementation on disease activity or relapse risk in autoimmune diseases (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis [RA], systemic lupus erythematosus [SLE], multiple sclerosis [MS]) is heterogeneous, with evidence suggesting modest or context-dependent effects: 1. **Multiple Sclerosis (MS):** - **High-dose vitamin D** (e.g., 100,000 IU cholecalciferol every 2 weeks) reduced MRI-defined disease activity (new lesions, contrast enhancement) in early MS or clinically isol...

#10 pubmed_traj_00079 epidemiology

📋 Question

Among individuals previously infected with pre-Omicron SARS-CoV-2 variants, what is the risk of reinfection with Omicron subvariants and how does prior infection influence severity of reinfection?
Topic: COVID-19
Key PMIDs: 37066797, 33367526, 38191642, 34533691

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think: 3 🔧 Tool Calls: 11 📄 Tool Outputs: 11 📏 Total Length: 66122 chars ⚠️ Errors: 1
⚠️ Critical Errors Detected REPEATED(3)
Tool #8 REPEATED_QUERY Severity: 8 PRE: 8, POST: 9
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - Population: individuals previously infected with pre-Omicron SARS-CoV-2 (wildtype/Alpha/Lambda but before Omicron). - Outcomes: (a) risk of reinfection with Omicron subvariants (BA.1 → BA.5), and (b) severity upon reinfection vs. primary infection. - Needed evidence: cohort or registry studies, vaccine-breakthrough analyses that distinguish breakthrough from reinfection, and systematic reviews/meta-analyses. Also real-world data from 2021–2022 across variants (UK, Israel, South Africa, US). Assumptions: - “Pre-Omicron” includes wildtype and early VOC (Alpha, Delta) but excludes Lambda and Omicron. - Severity outcomes include hospitalization, ICU, and death, comparing reinfection vs. primary infection and vs. uninfected or vaccinated. Search plan (broad → specific): 1) Broad query for “reinfection Omicron subvariants prior infection pre-Omicron risk”. 2) Follow with country-specific large cohorts (UK REACT, Israel MOx, South Africa surveillance) focusing on reinfection incidence and hazard ratios. 3) Find studies quantifying severity (hospitalization/ICU) after reinfection vs primary infection and vs vaccination. 4) Include meta-analyses/systematic reviews summarizing reinfection risk and severity. 5) If needed, pull vaccine-breakthrough studies that explicitly model reinfection vs breakthrough. First query: broad terms to surface key reviews and early cohort studies.

📄 Tool Output Step 3
Found 2 results. Showing top 2: <snippet id="36032973">Title: Rapidly shifting immunologic landscape and severity of SARS-CoV-2 in the Omicron era in South Africa. Authors: Sun, Kaiyuan, Tempia, Stefano, Kleynhans, Jackie et al. | Year: 2022 | Journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences Abstract: South Africa was among the first countries to detect the SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant. Propelled by increased transmissibility and immune escape properties, Omicron displaced other globally circulating variants within 3 months of its emergence. Due to limited testing, Omicron's attenuated clinical severity, and an increased risk of reinfection, the size of the Omicron BA.1 and BA.2 subvariants (BA.1/2) wave remains poorly understood in South Africa and in many other countries. Using South African data from urban and rural cohorts closely monitored since the beginning of the pandemic, we analyzed sequential serum samples collected before, during, and after the Omicron BA.1/2 wave to infer infection rates and monitor changes in the immune histories of participants over time. Omicron BA.1/2 infection attack rates reached 65% (95% CI, 60% - 69%) in the rural cohort and 58% (95% CI, 61% - 74%) in the urban cohort, with repeat infections and vaccine breakthroughs accounting for >60% of all infections at both sites. Combined with previously collected data on pre-Omicron variant infections within the same cohorts, we identified 14 distinct categories of SARS-CoV-2 antigen exposure histories in the aftermath of the Omicron BA.1/2 wave, indicating a particularly fragmented immunologic landscape. Few individuals (<6%) remained naïve to SARS-CoV-2 and no exposure history category represented over 25% of the population at either cohort site. Further, cohort participants were more than twice as likely to get infected during the Omicron BA.1/2 wave, compared to the Delta wave. Prior infection with the ancestral strain (with D614G mutation), Beta, an ... [truncated]
🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - Part 1: Quantify reinfection risk with Omicron subvariants among those previously infected with pre-Omicron variants (Alpha/Beta/Delta). - Part 2: Assess how prior infection influences severity of reinfection (hospitalization/ICU/death), ideally distinguishing Omicron-era outcomes and hybrid immunity. Plan: 1) Start broad with recent cohort/population studies quantifying protection against Omicron (BA.1/2/5) reinfection by prior infection (pre-Omicron variants). 2) Find studies focused on severity during reinfection (hospitalization/ICU) in Omicron era, including hybrid immunity. 3) Add regionally diverse evidence (e.g., South Africa, Japan, Europe/US) and capture effect sizes and time windows. 4) If needed, search for meta-analyses or large national cohorts. First query already returned: South Africa medRxiv (BA.1/2) and Japan nationwide BA.5 study. Next, I need severity-focused evidence and additional cohorts (e.g., UK, US, Europe) and explicit comparisons for pre-Omicron prior infections vs Omicron-era reinfection severity. Next query: "SARS-CoV-2 reinfection Omicron severity hospitalization prior infection hybrid immunity cohort study".

Found 4 results. Showing top 4: <snippet id="36150972">Title: Protection of vaccination versus hybrid immunity against infection with COVID-19 Omicron variants among Health-Care Workers. Authors: Ntziora, Fotinie, Kostaki, Evangelia Georgia, Karapanou, Amalia et al. | Year: 2022 | Journal: Vaccine Abstract: BACKGROUND AIM The Omicron COVID-19 variants BA.1* and BA.2* evade immune system leading to increased transmissibility and breakthrough infections. We aim to test the hypothesis that immunity achieved post COVID-19 infection combined with vaccination (hybrid immunity), is more effective against Omicron infection than vaccination alone in a health-care setting. METHODS Data on regular pre-emptive PCR testing from all Health-Care Workers (HCWs) at Laiko University Hospital from 29th December 2020, date on which the national COVID-19 immunization program began in Greece, until 24th May 2022, were retrospectively collected and recorded. The infection rate was calculated after December 21st, 2021, when Omicron was the predominant circulating variant in Greece, as the total number of infections (positive PCR COVID-19 test regardless of symptoms) divided by the total person-months at risk. RESULTS Of 1,305 vaccinated HCWs who were included in the analysis [median age of 47 (IQR: 36, 56) years, 66.7 % women], 13 % and 87 % had received 2 or 3 vaccine doses (full and booster vaccination), respectively. A COVID-19 infection had occurred in 135 of 1,305 of participants prior to Omicron predominance. Of those 135 HCWs with hybrid immunity only 13 (9.6 %) were re-infected. Of the 154 and 1,016 HCWs with full and booster vaccination-induced immunity, respectively, 71 (46.1 %, infection rate 13.4/100 person-months) and 448 (44.1 %, infection rate 12.2/100 person-months) were infected during the follow up period. No association between gender or age and COVID-19 infection was found and none of the participants had a severe infection or died. CONCLUSIONS Hybrid im ... [truncated]

Showing top 3: <snippet id="38223396">Title: Protective effect of previous infection and vaccination against reinfection with BA.5 Omicron subvariant: a nationwide population-based study in Japan. Authors: Kitamura, Noriko, Otani, Kanako, Kinoshita, Ryo et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: The Lancet regional health. Western Pacific Abstract: BACKGROUND The Omicron variant of SARS-CoV-2 was reported to evade immunity derived from vaccination and previous infection. A better understanding of hybrid immunity informs effective infection control strategies. Since the reinfection risk was not well-assessed in East Asia, this study aims to evaluate the risk of infection with Omicron subvariant BA.5 among previously infected individuals in Japan. METHODS All notified cases were extracted from the Japanese national COVID-19 surveillance database including 20,297,335 records up to 25 September 2022. Reinfection with BA.5 was defined as the infection notified during the BA.5 dominated period with any prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. The protective effect of prior infections against reinfections with BA.5 was estimated by applying a case-population design and the protective effect of vaccination was estimated by a multivariable Cox regression adjusting for age, sex, variants of prior infection, and the time since the last vaccination. FINDINGS Among 19,830,548 SARS-CoV-2 first infections, 233,424 (1.2%) were reinfected with BA.5. The protective effect against BA.5 reinfection of prior infection with Wuhan strain was 46%, Alpha variant was 35%, Delta variant was 41%, and BA.1/BA.2 subvariant was 74%. The reduced risk of BA.5 reinfection by 7%, 33%, and 66% was associated with two, three, and four doses of vaccination, respectively, compared with one-dose vaccination. INTERPRETATION The prior infections with Omicron subvariant BA.1/BA.2 protected BA.5 reinfection more than pre-Omicron variants. Increased frequency of vaccination led to more protection from ... [truncated]

Showing top 5: <snippet id="36239934">Title: Estimated Protection of Prior SARS-CoV-2 Infection Against Reinfection With the Omicron Variant Among Messenger RNA-Vaccinated and Nonvaccinated Individuals in Quebec, Canada. Authors: Carazo, Sara, Skowronski, Danuta M, Brisson, Marc et al. | Year: 2022 | Journal: JAMA network open Abstract: IMPORTANCE The Omicron variant is phylogenetically and antigenically distinct from earlier SARS-CoV-2 variants and the original vaccine strain. Protection conferred by prior SARS-CoV-2 infection against Omicron reinfection, with and without vaccination, requires quantification. OBJECTIVE To estimate the protection against Omicron reinfection and hospitalization conferred by prior heterologous non-Omicron SARS-CoV-2 infection and/or up to 3 doses of an ancestral, Wuhan-like messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccine. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS This test-negative, population-based case-control study was conducted between December 26, 2021, and March 12, 2022, and included community-dwelling individuals aged 12 years or older who were tested for SARS-CoV-2 infection in the province of Quebec, Canada. EXPOSURES Prior laboratory-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection with or without mRNA vaccination. MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES The main outcome was laboratory-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 reinfection and associated hospitalization, presumed to be associated with the Omicron variant according to genomic surveillance. The odds of prior infection with or without vaccination were compared for case participants with Omicron infection and associated hospitalizations vs test-negative control participants. Estimated protection was derived as 1 - the odds ratio, adjusted for age, sex, testing indication, and epidemiologic week. Analyses were stratified by severity and time since last non-Omicron infection or vaccine dose. RESULTS This study included 696 439 individuals (224 007 case participants and 472 432 control participants); 62.2% an ... [truncated]

Western Pacific Abstract: BACKGROUND The Omicron variant of SARS-CoV-2 was reported to evade immunity derived from vaccination and previous infection. A better understanding of hybrid immunity informs effective infection control strategies. Since the reinfection risk was not well-assessed in East Asia, this study aims to evaluate the risk of infection with Omicron subvariant BA.5 among previously infected individuals in Japan. METHODS All notified cases were extracted from the Japanese national COVID-19 surveillance database including 20,297,335 records up to 25 September 2022. Reinfection with BA.5 was defined as the infection notified during the BA.5 dominated period with any prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. The protective effect of prior infections against reinfections with BA.5 was estimated by applying a case-population design and the protective effect of vaccination was estimated by a multivariable Cox regression adjusting for age, sex, variants of prior infection, and the time since the last vaccination. FINDINGS Among 19,830,548 SARS-CoV-2 first infections, 233,424 (1.2%) were reinfected with BA.5. The protective effect against BA.5 reinfection of prior infection with Wuhan strain was 46%, Alpha variant was 35%, Delta variant was 41%, and BA.1/BA.2 subvariant was 74%. The reduced risk of BA.5 reinfection by 7%, 33%, and 66% was associated with two, three, and four doses of vaccination, respectively, compared with one-dose vaccination. Showing top 1: <snippet id="38223396">Title: Protective effect of previous infection and vaccination against reinfection with BA.5 Omicron subvariant: a nationwide population-based study in Japan. Authors: Kitamura, Noriko, Otani, Kanako, Kinoshita, Ryo et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: The Lancet regional health. Western Pacific Abstract: BACKGROUND The Omicron variant of SARS-CoV-2 was reported to evade immunity derived from vaccination and previous infection. A better understanding of hybrid immunity informs effective infection control strategies. Since the reinfection risk was not well-assessed in East Asia, this study aims to evaluate the risk of infection with Omicron subvariant BA.5 among previously infected individuals in Japan. METHODS All notified cases were extracted from the Japanese national COVID-19 surveillance database including 20,297,335 records up to 25 September 2022. Reinfection with BA.5 was defined as the infection notified during the BA.5 dominated period with any prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. The protective effect of prior infections against reinfections with BA.5 was estimated by applying a case-population design and the protective effect of vaccination was estimated by a multivariable Cox regression adjusting for age, sex, variants of prior infection, and the time since the last vaccination. FINDINGS Among 19,830,548 SARS-CoV-2 first infections, 233,424 (1.2%) were reinfected with BA.5. The protective effect against BA.5 reinfection of prior infection with Wuhan strain was 46%, Alpha variant was 35%, Delta variant was 41%, and BA.1/BA.2 subvariant was 74%. The reduced risk of BA.5 reinfection by 7%, 33%, and 66% was associated with two, three, and four doses of vaccination, respectively, compared with one-dose vaccination. INTERPRETATION The prior infections with Omicron subvariant BA.1/BA.2 protected BA.5 reinfection more than pre-Omicron variants. Increased frequency of vaccination led to more protection from ... [truncated]

Authors: Kitamura, Noriko, Otani, Kanako, Kinoshita, Ryo et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: The Lancet regional health. Western Pacific Abstract: BACKGROUND The Omicron variant of SARS-CoV-2 was reported to evade immunity derived from vaccination and previous infection. A better understanding of hybrid immunity informs effective infection control strategies. Since the reinfection risk was not well-assessed in East Asia, this study aims to evaluate the risk of infection with Omicron subvariant BA.5 among previously infected individuals in Japan. METHODS All notified cases were extracted from the Japanese national COVID-19 surveillance database including 20,297,335 records up to 25 September 2022. Reinfection with BA.5 was defined as the infection notified during the BA.5 dominated period with any prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. The protective effect of prior infections against reinfections with BA.5 was estimated by applying a case-population design and the protective effect of vaccination was estimated by a multivariable Cox regression adjusting for age, sex, variants of prior infection, and the time since the last vaccination. FINDINGS Among 19,830,548 SARS-CoV-2 first infections, 233,424 (1.2%) were reinfected with BA.5. The protective effect against BA.5 reinfection of prior infection with Wuhan strain was 46%, Alpha variant was 35%, Delta variant was 41%, and BA.1/BA.2 subvariant was 74%. The reduced risk of BA.5 reinfection by 7%, 33%, and 66% was associated with two, three, and four doses of vaccination, respectively, compared with one-dose vaccination. Authors: Kitamura, Noriko, Otani, Kanako, Kinoshita, Ryo et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: The Lancet regional health. Western Pacific Abstract: BACKGROUND The Omicron variant of SARS-CoV-2 was reported to evade immunity derived from vaccination and previous infection. A better understanding of hybrid immunity informs effective infection control strategies. Since the reinfection risk was not well-assessed in East Asia, this study aims to evaluate the risk of infection with Omicron subvariant BA.5 among previously infected individuals in Japan. METHODS All notified cases were extracted from the Japanese national COVID-19 surveillance database including 20,297,335 records up to 25 September 2022. Reinfection with BA.5 was defined as the infection notified during the BA.5 dominated period with any prior SARS-CoV-2 infection. The protective effect of prior infections against reinfections with BA.5 was estimated by applying a case-population design and the protective effect of vaccination was estimated by a multivariable Cox regression adjusting for age, sex, variants of prior infection, and the time since the last vaccination. FINDINGS Among 19,830,548 SARS-CoV-2 first infections, 233,424 (1.2%) were reinfected with BA.5. The protective effect against BA.5 reinfection of prior infection with Wuhan strain was 46%, Alpha variant was 35%, Delta variant was 41%, and BA.1/BA.2 subvariant was 74%. The reduced risk of BA.5 reinfection by 7%, 33%, and 66% was associated with two, three, and four doses of vaccination, respectively, compared with one-dose vaccination.
