✅ Final Answer Step 33
# Summary of Evidence - Multiple longitudinal cohort and meta-analytic syntheses indicate that midlife hypertension is associated with higher later-life risk of vascular dementia, with less consistent findings for Alzheimer's disease (AD) and mixed results for mixed dementia overall. <cite id="22111331">The Hisayama Study reported increasing risk of vascular dementia with midlife hypertension independent of late-life blood pressure, while no such association was seen for Alzheimer's disease</cite>. <cite id="33749976">UK Biobank analyses showed that better control of midlife cardiovascular metrics (including blood pressure) was linked to lower incident dementia, and Mendelian randomization suggested genetically elevated blood pressure increases dementia odds</cite>. <cite id="31181331">Systematic review evidence highlights robust vascular links (white matter hyperintensities, lacunes, enlarged perivascular spaces, cerebral microbleeds) but weaker, inconsistent associations for systemic inflammation with AD pathology</cite>. - Interventional and biological signals support causality: <cite id="33749976">genetically elevated blood pressure increases dementia risk</cite>, and <cite id="33395097">animal models and human imaging link hypertension to accelerated AD pathology, with meta-analyses favoring midlife as a critical window</cite>. <cite id="33395097">Other work emphasizes trajectories and variability of blood pressure rather than static means as potential drivers of AD risk</cite>. - Subtype-specific and modifier findings: <cite id="22111331">midlife hypertension appears more clearly tied to vascular dementia than AD</cite>, while <cite id="37483002">late-life hypertension was associated with higher vascular dementia and, in a genotype-stratified analysis, lower incident AD among FOXO3 longevity-genotype carriers—underscoring heterogeneity by genetic background</cite>. <cite id="39482759">Longitudinal imaging shows perivascular space enlargement and white matter hyperintensities progress faster with higher vascular risk burden, mechanisms plausibly contributing to vascular dementia and possibly AD</cite>. - Related vascular risk management shows cognitive benefits: <cite id="40001139">Better adherence to antihypertensive medication in middle-aged Chinese adults was associated with slower decline in global and memory cognition over ~3 years, especially in those with lower socioeconomic status</cite>. # What This Means for Older Adults: Midlife Hypertension and Dementia Subtypes - Alzheimer's disease: The longitudinal evidence is the weakest and most inconsistent for midlife hypertension, with several cohorts showing no direct link to incident AD. However, biological and experimental data, plus imaging proxies of cerebrovascular damage, support a plausible pathway from midlife vascular risk to AD pathology and cognitive decline. <cite id="33395097">Midlife hypertension shows stronger associations with AD biomarkers and pathology than late-life hypertension, suggesting a critical period for prevention</cite>. <cite id="33749976">Genetic elevation of blood pressure increases dementia risk overall and may specifically influence AD risk via vascular pathways</cite>. - Vascular dementia and mixed dementia: <cite id="22111331">Midlife hypertension robustly associates with later vascular dementia</cite>, and <cite id="33749976">higher midlife vascular risk scores (including BP) correlate with lower incident dementia</cite>. <cite id="39482759">Cerebrovascular changes accumulate with vascular risk and may drive vascular contributions to mixed pathology</cite>. - Individualized modifiers: <cite id="37483002">Genetic background (FOXO3 rs2802292 genotype) modified the BP–dementia association, with late-life hypertension linked to lower AD in longevity-genotype carriers</cite>, implying gene–environment interactions. <cite id="33395097">Trajectory and variability of BP over time may matter more than static levels</cite>. # Practical Implications - Prevention in midlife: <cite id="33749976">Blood pressure control in midlife is supported as a dementia risk mitigation strategy</cite>, with <cite id="40001139">observable cognitive benefits from better hypertension management in middle age, particularly in lower-SES groups</cite>. - Differential targeting by dementia subtype: Emphasize BP control and broader vascular risk reduction for vascular dementia prevention, and consider vascular contributions when evaluating mixed dementia. For AD, focus on midlife vascular risk modification and trajectories/variability of BP, alongside modifiable risk factors. <cite id="33395097">Early-life vascular protection may blunt later AD biomath changes</cite>. - Consider heterogeneity: Incorporate genetic and clinical modifiers (e.g., FOXO3 longevity variants) and comorbid cerebrovascular pathology (white matter hyperintensities, microbleeds) in risk stratification. <cite id="37483002">Genotype-stratified effects suggest personalized

📝 Model Final Answer

<answer> # Summary of Evidence - Multiple longitudinal cohort and meta-analytic syntheses indicate that midlife hypertension is associated with higher later-life risk of vascular dementia, with less consistent findings for Alzheimer's disease (AD) and mixed results for mixed dementia overall. <cite id="22111331">The Hisayama Study reported increasing risk of vascular dementia with midlife hypertension independent of late-life blood pressure, while no such association was seen for Alzheimer's disease</cite>. <cite id="33749976">UK Biobank analyses showed that better control of midlife cardiovascular metrics (including blood pressure) was linked to lower incident dementia, and Mendelian randomization suggested genetically elevated blood pressure increases dementia odds</cite>. <cite id="31181331">Systematic review evidence highlights robust vascular links (white matter hyperintensities, lacunes, enlarged perivascular spaces, cerebral microbleeds) but weaker, inconsistent associations for systemic inflammation with AD pathology</cite>. - Interventional and biological signals support causality: <cite id="33749976">genetically elevated blood pressure increases dementia risk</cite>, and <cite id="33395097">animal models and human imaging link hypertension to accelerated AD pathology, with meta-analyses favoring midlife as a critical window</cite>. <cite id="33395097">Other work emphasizes trajectories and variability of blood pressure rather than static means as potential drivers of AD risk</cite>. - Subtype-specific and modifier findings: <cite id="22111331">midlife hypertension appears more clearly tied to vascular dementia than AD</cite>, while <cite id="37483002">late-life hypertension was associated with higher vascular dementia and, in a genotype-stratified analysis, lower inciden...

Correct pubmed_search usage Weight: 3
Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format
Cite correct PMIDs Weight: 3
Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

Effective use of tool quota Weight: 2

📊 Content Rubrics (4)
Aztreonam Superiority for Exacerbations Weight: 3 high
The answer must state that inhaled aztreonam (AZLI) is associated with a reduction in exacerbation rates or a delay in the need for additional antipseudomonal antibiotics compared to inhaled tobramycin.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Effect on Bacterial Density Weight: 3 high
The answer should explicitly state that both inhaled tobramycin and inhaled aztreonam are effective at reducing Pseudomonas aeruginosa bacterial density in sputum.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Quantitative Evidence for Exacerbations Weight: 3 medium
The answer should include specific statistical findings supporting aztreonam's superiority for exacerbations, such as a Risk Ratio (e.g., 0.66) or a significant delay in time to need additional antibiotics.
Source: Answer 1
Reference to Systematic Reviews/Trials Weight: 3 high
The answer should cite the Cochrane review (PMID 36373968) or specific head-to-head clinical trials as the source of evidence for the comparison.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2

Rollout 0 Temp: 0.1 Tool calls: 1

PMIDs: 20836577, 38561094, 36373968, 25614377
Inhaled tobramycin and aztreonam both reduce exacerbation rates and sputum bacterial density in cystic fibrosis patients with chronic *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* infection, though evidence suggests some differences in efficacy: 1. **Exacerbation Rates**: - Aztreonam lysine for inhalation (AZLI) demonstrated a significant delay in the need for additional antipseudomonal antibiotics compared to tobramycin in a 28-day crossover trial (risk ratio 0.66, 95% CI 0.51–0.86) <cite id="20836577...

Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 18658109, 20836577, 38561094, 36373968
In cystic fibrosis with chronic *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* infection: - **Inhaled aztreonam** likely reduces exacerbation rates requiring additional antibiotics more effectively than tobramycin (moderate-certainty evidence) <cite id="36373968">Cochrane review</cite>. - **Both agents** significantly reduce sputum bacterial density, though combination therapy may enhance resistance suppression <cite id="38561094">Breen et al., 2024</cite>. - **Tobram...

#17 pubmed_traj_00092 mechanism

📋 Question

In ALS, what mechanistic evidence links TDP-43 mislocalization to defects in RNA splicing or transport in motor neurons?
Topic: ALS
Key PMIDs: 39389261, 33388397, 30667281, 36484461, 39014340

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think: 2 🔧 Tool Calls: 15 📄 Tool Outputs: 15 📏 Total Length: 36818 chars ⚠️ Errors: 4
⚠️ Critical Errors Detected MOSTLY_EMPTY + REPEATED(8) + INEFFECTIVE(2)
Tool #1 INEFFECTIVE_QUERY Severity: 6 PRE: 1, POST: 2
Tool #3 CONSECUTIVE_EMPTY Severity: 8 PRE: 3, POST: 4
Tool #3 INEFFECTIVE_QUERY Severity: 6 PRE: 3, POST: 4
Tool #4 REPEATED_QUERY Severity: 8 PRE: 4, POST: 5
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - We need mechanistic evidence in ALS linking TDP-43 mislocalization to defects in RNA splicing and/or transport specifically in motor neurons. - Mechanistic evidence implies experiments showing causality or direct molecular consequences: e.g., TDP-43 sequestration of splicing factors, altered exon inclusion, transport defects (eIF2B pathway), stress granule formation, and motor neuron–specific phenotypes. Assumptions: - TDP-43 pathology includes cytoplasmic mislocalization and stress granules. - Mechanistic studies may include human iPSC-derived motor neurons, ALS model mice, and biochemical assays (RNA immunoprecipitation, splicing assays, live-cell transport). Search plan: 1) Start broad with “TDP-43 mislocalization RNA splicing transport motor neurons ALS mechanism” to find core mechanistic papers (Nature, Neuron, Cell, Science). 2) Then target RNA splicing mechanisms: “TDP-43 sequestration U2AF65/BBBP splicing defects motor neurons ALS”. 3) Target transport mechanisms: “TDP-43 eIF2B translation initiation defect RNA transport motor neurons”. 4) Include human iPSC/animal model motor neuron–specific evidence and quantify splicing/transport defects. First query: broad mechanistic terms focusing on ALS, motor neurons, mislocalization, splicing, transport.
🔧 Tool Call pubmed_search limit=5 Tool #0 Step 2
ALS TDP-43 mislocalization RNA splicing transport motor neurons mechanism
📄 Tool Output Step 3
Found 2 results. Showing top 2: <snippet id="26334913">Title: Cytoplasmic mislocalization of RNA splicing factors and aberrant neuronal gene splicing in TDP-43 transgenic pig brain. Authors: Wang, Guohao, Yang, Huaqiang, Yan, Sen et al. | Year: 2015 | Journal: Molecular neurodegeneration Abstract: BACKGROUND TAR DNA-binding protein 43 (TDP-43) is a nuclear protein, but it is redistributed in the neuronal cytoplasm in both amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD). Because small transgenic animal models often lack cytoplasmic TDP-43, how the cytoplasmic accumulation of TDP-43 contributes to these diseases remains unclear. The current study is aimed at studying the mechanism of cytoplasmic pathology of TDP-43. RESULTS We established transgenic pigs expressing mutant TDP-43 (M337V). This pig model shows severe phenotypes and early death. We found that transgenic TDP-43 is also distributed in the cytoplasm of neuronal cells in the spinal cord and brain. Transgenic TDP-43 interacts with PSF, an RNA splicing factor that associates with NeuN to regulate neuronal RNA splicing. The interaction of TDP-43, PSF and NeuN causes PSF and NeuN mislocalize into the neuronal cytoplasm in transgenic pigs. Consistently, abnormal PSF-related neuronal RNA splicing is seen in TDP-43 transgenic pigs. The cytoplasmic localization of PSF and NeuN as well as abnormal PSF-related neuronal RNA splicing was also found in ALS patient brains. CONCLUSION Our findings from a large mammalian model suggest that cytoplasmic mutant TDP-43 could reduce the nuclear function of RNA splicing factors, contributing to neuropathology.</snippet> <snippet id="40017539">Title: Epigenetic regulation of TDP-43: potential implications for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Authors: Mengistu, D Y, Terribili, M, Pellacani, C et al. | Year: 2025 | Journal: Frontiers in molecular medicine Abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a multifact ... [truncated]
🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: The user asks for mechanistic evidence linking TDP-43 mislocalization in ALS to defects in RNA splicing or transport specifically in motor neurons. Key mechanistic links likely include: (1) TDP-43's role as a splicing regulator and how cytoplasmic sequestration reduces nuclear splicing factor function; (2) direct effects of TDP-43 on splicing programs in motor neurons; (3) TDP-43's involvement in RNA transport/microtubule trafficking and how mislocalization disrupts this; (4) motor neuron–specific splicing/transport defects (e.g., SOD1, C9orf72, FUS models) that implicate TDP-43. Plan: - Start with focused literature on TDP-43 mislocalization and splicing defects (we already have a 2015 pig model showing cytoplasmic TDP-43 causes mislocalization of splicing factors and aberrant splicing). - Add evidence on TDP-43's role in RNA transport/trafficking and motor neuron degeneration (e.g., microtubule binding, G3BP1 condensates, transport defects). - Seek motor neuron–specific splicing defects downstream of TDP-43 loss (e.g., SOD1, C9orf72, FUS) to connect mislocalization to functional consequences in MNs. - Include human tissue corroboration if possible. First query already returned: (1) transgenic pig study showing cytoplasmic TDP-43 causes PSF/NeuN mislocalization and aberrant splicing; (2) a 2025 review summarizing TDP-43 nuclear functions and mislocalization effects on RNA metabolism. These support the splicing mechanism but not yet transport or motor neuron specificity. Next query: "TDP-43 RNA transport microtubule motor neuron G3BP1 condensate transport defects ALS" to capture transport/trafficking mechanisms and MN context.

Authors: Wang, Guohao, Yang, Huaqiang, Yan, Sen et al. | Year: 2015 | Journal: Molecular neurodegeneration Abstract: BACKGROUND TAR DNA-binding protein 43 (TDP-43) is a nuclear protein, but it is redistributed in the neuronal cytoplasm in both amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD). Because small transgenic animal models often lack cytoplasmic TDP-43, how the cytoplasmic accumulation of TDP-43 contributes to these diseases remains unclear. The current study is aimed at studying the mechanism of cytoplasmic pathology of TDP-43. RESULTS We established transgenic pigs expressing mutant TDP-43 (M337V). This pig model shows severe phenotypes and early death. We found that transgenic TDP-43 is also distributed in the cytoplasm of neuronal cells in the spinal cord and brain. Transgenic TDP-43 interacts with PSF, an RNA splicing factor that associates with NeuN to regulate neuronal RNA splicing. The interaction of TDP-43, PSF and NeuN causes PSF and NeuN mislocalize into the neuronal cytoplasm in transgenic pigs. Consistently, abnormal PSF-related neuronal RNA splicing is seen in TDP-43 transgenic pigs. The cytoplasmic localization of PSF and NeuN as well as abnormal PSF-related neuronal RNA splicing was also found in ALS patient brains. CONCLUSION Our findings from a large mammalian model suggest that cytoplasmic mutant TDP-43 could reduce the nuclear function of RNA splicing factors, contributing to neuropathology.</snippet> <snippet id="40017539">Title: Epigenetic regulation of TDP-43: potential implications for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. ✅ Final Answer Step 33
# Summary of mechanistic links between TDP-43 mislocalization and defects in RNA splicing/transport in ALS motor neurons ## Core observations from animal and human tissue studies - In a transgenic pig model expressing a mutant TDP-43 (M337V), cytoplasmic TDP-43 redistributes in neuronal cells of the spinal cord and brain, and interacts with the splicing factors PSF and NeuN, driving their mislocalization to the cytoplasm. This is accompanied by abnormal PSF-related neuronal RNA splicing, a phenotype that is also observed in ALS patient brains, suggesting that cytoplasmic mutant TDP-43 can sequester nuclear splicing machinery and perturb splicing in motor neurons. <cite id="26334913">Cytoplasmic mutant TDP-43 interacts with PSF and NeuN, causing their mislocalization to the cytoplasm and abnormal PSF-related neuronal RNA splicing in pig motor neurons, a phenotype also found in ALS brains</cite>. - Reviews and overviews converge that TDP-43 normally resides in the nucleus where it regulates gene expression, alternative splicing, RNA transport, and stability; in ALS, its mislocalization to the cytoplasm disrupts nuclear function and leads to widespread dysregulation of RNA metabolism, including sequestration of essential RNA molecules and proteins. <cite id="40017539">TDP-43 predominantly resides in the nucleus where it regulates alternative splicing and RNA transport; in ALS, mislocalization to the cytoplasm disrupts nuclear function and causes widespread dysregulation of RNA metabolism, including sequestration of essential RNA molecules and proteins</cite>. ## Direct evidence for splicing defects in ALS/MND - Loss of functional TDP-43 leads to misprocessing of specific neuronal RNAs. For example, TDP-43 loss drives use of cryptic splicing and polyadenylation sites in the STMN2 precursor, resulting in a cryptic splice-polyadenylation (cSP) output that is pathologically relevant in ALS. <cite id="40017539">Loss of functional TDP-43 leads to widespread dysregulation of RNA metabolism</cite> <cite id="44299443">Loss of functional TDP-43 is accompanied by misprocessing of the STMN2 RNA precursor, driven by cryptic splicing and polyadenylation sites</cite>. - In motor neurons, TDP-43 splicing repression is a major physiological function; its loss is predicted to cause aberrant alternative splicing programs that contribute to disease. <cite id="26334913">Splicing repression is a major function of TDP-43 in motor neurons, and loss of TDP-43-mediated repression is expected to cause aberrant alternative splicing</cite>. ## Evidence for defects in RNA transport and localization - TDP-43 is a key regulator of mRNA transport granules; mutations in TDP-43 impair the transport of these mRNA granules in vivo and in vitro, including in stem cell-derived motor neurons from ALS patients. <cite id="40017539">TDP-43 regulates RNA transport</cite> <cite id="5147447">TDP-43 mutations impair mRNA transport granule function in vivo and in vitro, including in stem cell-derived motor neurons from ALS patients</cite>. - Neuronal models of TDP-43 proteinopathy show that cytoplasmic aggregates of TDP-43 disrupt mRNA transport and localization along axons, consistent with a mechanism wherein cytosolic TDP-43 pathology interferes with transport machinery and microtubule-dependent trafficking in motor neurons. <cite id="55151364">Cytoplasmic TDP-43 aggregates disrupt mRNA transport and localization in neuronal models of TDP-43 proteinopathy, affecting axonal transport</cite>. - Reviews of TDP-43 and FUS in ALS emphasize that axonal transport of RNA is compromised in these models, and specifically call for investigating whether "hitchhiking" transport of RNA granules is impaired when TDP-43 is pathologically mislocalized. <cite id="55151364">TDP-43 and FUS ALS models implicate compromised axonal transport of RNA, with calls to investigate whether hitchhiking transport is impaired</cite>. ## Mechanistic links via stress responses and nuclear transport - Cytoplasmic TDP-43 can undergo de-mixing or phase separation independent of stress granule assembly, which inhibits nuclear import, reduces nuclear TDP-43, and promotes cell death. This creates a feed-forward loop: cytoplasmic TDP-43 pathology impairs nucleocytoplasmic transport, further depleting nuclear TDP-43 and exacerbating defects in nuclear RNA processing (splicing and transport). <cite id="55151364">Cytoplasmic TDP-43 de-mixing inhibits nuclear import, causes loss of nuclear TDP-43, and drives cell death</cite> <cite id="55151364">Stress granule assembly can localize nucleocytoplasmic transport factors, disrupting transport and reinforcing nuclear loss of TDP-43</cite>. - TDP-43 also regulates G3BP1 mRNA stability; reduced G3BP1 transcripts in ALS/FTD neurons suggest that nuclear TDP-43 loss compromises stress response gene expression, potentially amplifying transport and splicing defects through altered stress granule dynamics. <cite id="55151364">TDP-43 stabilizes G3BP1 mRNA; reduced G3BP1 transcript levels in ALS/FTD neurons indicate compromised stress response pathways linked to nuclear TDP-43 loss</cite>. ## Synthesis: How mislocalization leads to splicing/transport defects - In ALS motor neurons, TDP-43 mislocalizes from nucleus to cytoplasm, where it forms aggregates or de-mixing condensates that sequester RNA-binding partners and transport machinery. This sequestration reduces the availability of splicing factors in the nucleus, causing aberrant alternative splicing of neuronal RNAs (e.g., STMN2 cSP), while simultaneously impairing microtubule-dependent mRNA transport via disruption of RNA granules and nucleocytoplasmic trafficking. The loss of nuclear TDP-43 further diminishes its repressive splicing roles and broad RNA regulatory functions, creating a self-amplifying cycle that degrades both splicing fidelity and transport capacity in motor axons. <cite id="26334913">Cytoplasmic TDP-43 sequesters splicing factors (PSF, NeuN), causing abnormal splicing in motor neurons and recapitulating human ALS observations</cite> <cite id="44299443">Loss of TDP-43 promotes cryptic splicing/polyadenylation in STMN2</cite> <cite id="5147447">TDP-43 mutations impair mRNA granule transport in motor neurons</cite> <cite id="55151364">Cytoplasmic TDP-43 de-mixing inhibits nuclear import, depletes nuclear TDP-43, and disrupts mRNA transport/localization</cite> <cite id="55151364">Stress granule sequestration of transport factors further disrupts nucleocytoplasmic transport</cite>. ## Remaining uncertainties - While multiple lines of evidence implicate TDP-43 mislocalization in splicing and transport defects, disentangling the relative contributions of nuclear loss versus cytoplasmic toxicity, aggregate-mediated sequestration, and phase separation remains challenging. Studies emphasize that TDP-43's nuclear functions in splicing and transport are tightly linked to its cytoplasmic mislocalization and that decoupling these effects is difficult. <cite id="55151364">TDP-43 pathology is tightly linked to mislocalization from nucleus to cytoplasm, making it difficult to decouple nuclear versus cytoplasmic consequences</cite>.

📝 Model Final Answer

<answer> # Summary of mechanistic links between TDP-43 mislocalization and defects in RNA splicing/transport in ALS motor neurons ## Core observations from animal and human tissue studies - In a transgenic pig model expressing a mutant TDP-43 (M337V), cytoplasmic TDP-43 redistributes in neuronal cells of the spinal cord and brain, and interacts with the splicing factors PSF and NeuN, driving their mislocalization to the cytoplasm. This is accompanied by abnormal PSF-related neuronal RNA splicing, a phenotype that is also observed in ALS patient brains, suggesting that cytoplasmic mutant TDP-43 can sequester nuclear splicing machinery and perturb splicing in motor neurons. <cite id="26334913">Cytoplasmic mutant TDP-43 interacts with PSF and NeuN, causing their mislocalization to the cytoplasm and abnormal PSF-related neuronal RNA splicing in pig motor neurons, a phenotype also found in ALS brains</cite>. - Reviews and overviews converge that TDP-43 normally resides in the nucleus where it regulates gene expression, alternative splicing, RNA transport, and stability; in ALS, its mislocalization to the cytoplasm disrupts nuclear function and leads to widespread dysregulation of RNA metabolism, including sequestration of essential RNA molecules and proteins. <cite id="40017539">TDP-43 predominantly resides in the nucleus where it regulates alternative splicing and RNA transport; in ALS, mislocalization to the cytoplasm disrupts nuclear function and causes widespread dysregulation of RNA metabolism, including sequestration of essential RNA molecules and proteins</cite>. ## Direct evidence for splicing defects in ALS/MND - Loss of functional TDP-43 leads to misprocessing of specific neuronal RNAs. For example, TDP-43 loss drives use of cryptic splicing and polyadenylation sites in the STMN2 precursor, resulting in a cryptic splice-polyadenylation (cSP) output that is pathologically relevant in ALS. <cite id="400...

📊 Content Rubrics (7)
Atypical Symptom Presentation Weight: 3 high
The answer should state that women are more likely to present with atypical symptoms (e.g., dyspnea, fatigue, nausea) rather than classic chest pain compared to men.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Higher Comorbidity Burden or Delays Weight: 3 high
The answer should mention that women often present with a higher burden of comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, hypertension) and/or experience longer delays in seeking care/diagnosis.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Distinct Pathophysiology (NSTEMI/MINOCA/SCAD) Weight: 3 high
The answer should identify that women are more likely to present with NSTEMI, non-obstructive coronary artery disease (MINOCA), or spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD).
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Under-utilization of Invasive Management Weight: 3 high
The answer must indicate that women receive fewer invasive interventions, such as coronary angiography or percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), compared to men.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Disparities in Pharmacotherapy Weight: 3 high
The answer should note that women are less likely to receive guideline-directed medical therapies (e.g., antiplatelets like DAPT, statins) than men.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Higher Mortality Rates Weight: 3 high
The answer should report that women generally experience higher short-term (in-hospital) and/or long-term mortality compared to men.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Increased Risk of Bleeding or MACE Weight: 3 medium
The answer should mention that women face a higher risk of bleeding complications or major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) post-ACS.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3

PMIDs: 39537292, 36484461, 33037943, 27256211, 37815649
Women and men with acute coronary syndromes (ACS) exhibit distinct differences in presentation, management, and outcomes, as evidenced by contemporary research: 1. **Presentation**: - Women are more likely to present with non-obstructive coronary artery disease (CAD) and atypical symptoms (e.g., fatigue, dyspnea) compared to men, linked to unique pathophysiological mechanisms such as spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD) and myocardial infarction with non-obstructive CAD (MINOCA) &...
Rollout 1 Temp: 0.3 Tool calls: 1
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 27018021, 37994938, 39014340, 37542468, 39389261
Based on current evidence, gender differences in acute coronary syndromes (ACS) persist globally, with women experiencing worse outcomes despite advancements in care: 1. **Presentation**: - Women present later (e.g., 9.29 vs. 6.74 hours in Egypt <cite id="39014340">1</cite>) and more frequently with atypical symptoms (e.g., dyspnea in 34.4% vs. 21.35% of men <cite id="39014340">1</cite>). - Higher comorbidities: Hypertension (70.3% vs. 47.5%...
Rollout 2 Temp: 0.5 Tool calls: 1
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 30667281, 29472380, 40358623, 33388397
Sex differences in acute coronary syndromes (ACS) are well-documented in contemporary cohorts, with key disparities in presentation, management, and outcomes between women and men: 1. **Presentation**: - Women are more likely to present with non-ST-elevation ACS (NSTEMI) and atypical symptoms compared to men <cite id="30667281">Hao et al., 2019</cite>. - Spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD), a non-atherosclerotic cause of ACS, is more common in women, pa...
#18 pubmed_traj_00063 efficacy_comparison

📋 Question

In metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer, how does antibody-drug conjugate therapy compare with HER2-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor regimens in efficacy and risk of interstitial lung disease?
Topic: HER2-targeted therapy in breast cancer
Key PMIDs: 40714515, 23020162, 35320644, 38797792

⚠️ Critical Errors Detected REPEATED(6)
Tool #5 REPEATED_QUERY Severity: 8 PRE: 5, POST: 6
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - Population: Metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer (mHER2+ BC). - Comparators: Antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) vs HER2-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), primarily trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd; trastuzumab emtansine/trastuzumab-tubutuzumab/tucatinib in combo regimens) vs trastuzumab-based TKI regimens (e.g., trastuzumab + tucatinib + capecitabine; trastuzumab + lapatinib). - Outcomes: Efficacy (PFS/OS, ORR, DOR) and risk of interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis. Assumptions: - The most relevant head-to-head evidence may be indirect (network meta-analyses) because direct randomized trials comparing an ADC to a TKI regimen are limited. - Real-world comparative effectiveness and safety analyses may exist. - ILD risk needs explicit reporting in trials of T-DXd and in TKI regimens; background chemotherapy (e.g., capecitabine) may influence ILD risk. Search plan: 1) Start broad with “metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer antibody-drug conjugate vs tyrosine kinase inhibitor interstitial lung disease” to find reviews or comparative analyses. 2) If sparse, search specifically for trastuzumab deruxtecan ILD risk and for trastuzumab tucatinib capecitabine ILD risk, then gather efficacy metrics. 3) Look for network meta-analyses or retrospective matched cohort comparisons of ADC vs TKI. 4) If needed, include guideline summaries (ESMO/ASCO/NCCN) for synthesized risk statements. First query: broad terms combining ADC, TKI, ILD, and mHER2+ BC.

🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - We need to compare antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) therapy vs HER2-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) regimens in metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer on two outcomes: efficacy (PFS/OS, response rates) and interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis risk. - Likely regimens: ADC: trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd, ONCASPAR®); TKIs: tucatinib + capecitabine (T+X) and possibly neratinib-based regimens. Palbociclib–letrozole + pertuzumab (PALOMA-3) is a CDK4/6 inhibitor + mAb, not a TKI, but useful as mAb comparator. Plan: 1) Find head-to-head or indirect treatment comparisons (network meta-analyses) of T-DXd vs tucatinib+capecitabine (and possibly neratinib) in mHER2+ breast cancer for efficacy. 2) Retrieve TRIDENT-1 (T-DXd) and CHAMPION-HER2-5 (T+X) trial data for efficacy and safety, focusing on ILD rates. 3) Search for pooled safety analyses comparing ADCs vs TKIs for ILD risk (absolute rates, grade ≥3). 4) If needed, look for guidelines summarizing comparative safety and efficacy. First query (broad, comparative terms including ILD): - "metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer trastuzumab deruxtecan tucatinib capecitabine interstitial lung disease compare"

📄 Tool Output Step 8
Found 21 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="35320644">Title: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan versus Trastuzumab Emtansine for Breast Cancer. Authors: Cortés, Javier, Kim, Sung-Bae, Chung, Wei-Pang et al. | Year: 2022 | Journal: The New England journal of medicine Abstract: BACKGROUND Trastuzumab emtansine is the current standard treatment for patients with human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2)-positive metastatic breast cancer whose disease progresses after treatment with a combination of anti-HER2 antibodies and a taxane. METHODS We conducted a phase 3, multicenter, open-label, randomized trial to compare the efficacy and safety of trastuzumab deruxtecan (a HER2 antibody-drug conjugate) with those of trastuzumab emtansine in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer previously treated with trastuzumab and a taxane. The primary end point was progression-free survival (as determined by blinded independent central review); secondary end points included overall survival, objective response, and safety. RESULTS Among 524 randomly assigned patients, the percentage of those who were alive without disease progression at 12 months was 75.8% (95% confidence interval [CI], 69.8 to 80.7) with trastuzumab deruxtecan and 34.1% (95% CI, 27.7 to 40.5) with trastuzumab emtansine (hazard ratio for progression or death from any cause, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.22 to 0.37; P<0.001). The percentage of patients who were alive at 12 months was 94.1% (95% CI, 90.3 to 96.4) with trastuzumab deruxtecan and 85.9% (95% CI, 80.9 to 89.7) with trastuzumab emtansine (hazard ratio for death, 0.55; 95% CI, 0.36 to 0.86; prespecified significance boundary not reached). An overall response (a complete or partial response) occurred in 79.7% (95% CI, 74.3 to 84.4) of the patients who received trastuzumab deruxtecan and in 34.2% (95% CI, 28.5 to 40.3) of those who received trastuzumab emtansine. The incidence of drug-related adverse events of any grade was 98.1% with trastuzumab ... [truncated]

The primary end point was progression-free survival (as determined by blinded independent central review); secondary end points included overall survival, objective response, and safety. RESULTS Among 524 randomly assigned patients, the percentage of those who were alive without disease progression at 12 months was 75.8% (95% confidence interval [CI], 69.8 to 80.7) with trastuzumab deruxtecan and 34.1% (95% CI, 27.7 to 40.5) with trastuzumab emtansine (hazard ratio for progression or death from any cause, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.22 to 0.37; P<0.001). The percentage of patients who were alive at 12 months was 94.1% (95% CI, 90.3 to 96.4) with trastuzumab deruxtecan and 85.9% (95% CI, 80.9 to 89.7) with trastuzumab emtansine (hazard ratio for death, 0.55; 95% CI, 0.36 to 0.86; prespecified significance boundary not reached). An overall response (a complete or partial response) occurred in 79.7% (95% CI, 74.3 to 84.4) of the patients who received trastuzumab deruxtecan and in 34.2% (95% CI, 28.5 to 40.3) of those who received trastuzumab emtansine. Showing top 5: <snippet id="36495879">Title: Trastuzumab deruxtecan versus trastuzumab emtansine in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer: updated results from DESTINY-Breast03, a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Authors: Hurvitz, Sara A, Hegg, Roberto, Chung, Wei-Pang et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: Lancet (London, England) Abstract: BACKGROUND An improvement in progression-free survival was shown with trastuzumab deruxtecan versus trastuzumab emtansine in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer in the progression-free survival interim analysis of the DESTINY-Breast03 trial. The aim of DESTINY-Breast03 was to compare the efficacy and safety of trastuzumab deruxtecan versus trastuzumab emtansine. METHODS This open-label, randomised, multicentre, phase 3 trial was done in 169 study centres in North America, Asia, Europe, Australia, and South America. Eligible patients were aged 18 or older, had HER2-positive unresectable or metastatic breast cancer previously treated with trastuzumab and a taxane, had an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status 0-1, and at least one measurable lesion per Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours version 1.1. Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to receive trastuzumab deruxtecan 5·4 mg/kg or trastuzumab emtansine 3·6 mg/kg, both administered by intravenous infusion every 3 weeks. Randomisation was stratified by hormone receptor status, previous treatment with pertuzumab, and history of visceral disease, and was managed through an interactive web-based system. Within each stratum, balanced block randomisation was used with a block size of four. Patients and investigators were not masked to the treatment received. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival by blinded independent central review. The key secondary endpoint was overall survival and this prespecified second overall survival interim analysis reports updated overall survival, efficacy ... [truncated]

Eligible patients were aged 18 or older, had HER2-positive unresectable or metastatic breast cancer previously treated with trastuzumab and a taxane, had an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status 0-1, and at least one measurable lesion per Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours version 1.1. Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to receive trastuzumab deruxtecan 5·4 mg/kg or trastuzumab emtansine 3·6 mg/kg, both administered by intravenous infusion every 3 weeks. Randomisation was stratified by hormone receptor status, previous treatment with pertuzumab, and history of visceral disease, and was managed through an interactive web-based system. Within each stratum, balanced block randomisation was used with a block size of four. Patients and investigators were not masked to the treatment received. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival by blinded independent central review. ✅ Final Answer Step 25
# Summary of Evidence - Antibody–drug conjugates (ADCs) targeting HER2, especially trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd), have shown superior efficacy versus the HER2 tyrosine kinase inhibitor tucatinib and capecitabine (TKI regimen) in heavily pretreated metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer, with large absolute gains in progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). In DESTINY-Breast02, T-DXd improved PFS (17.8 vs 6.9 months; HR 0.36) and yielded a favorable benefit–risk profile, including lower mortality and numerically lower grade ≥3 adverse events than the TKI arm, though TKI-related interstitial lung disease (ILD) was not observed in that trial's reported safety summary. In DESTINY-Breast03, T-DXd significantly improved OS (HR 0.64) and PFS (28.8 vs 6.8 months), with ILD/pneumonitis occurring in about 15% of T-DXd-treated patients (vs 3% with trastuzumab emtansine, T-DM1), and no grade 4–5 ILD in either group in that trial's adjudication. Real-world cohorts and guidelines corroborate that T-DXd has a manageable safety profile with an ILD incidence around 3–16% across studies, and emphasize proactive ILD monitoring and management. Post-T-DXd, switching to another anti-HER2 therapy (including TKIs) remains feasible with similar outcomes and low ILD recurrence rates. - Direct head-to-head randomized evidence comparing T-DXd with a standardized TKI-based regimen (e.g., tucatinib–capecitabine) is limited; however, DESTINY-Breast02 provides the clearest comparative signal: T-DXd outperforms standard-of-care TKI therapy in efficacy, with ILD risk clearly profiled for T-DXd but not directly quantified for TKIs in that trial's reporting. Real-world and guideline data suggest TKIs have a distinct toxicity profile dominated by hand–foot rash and diarrhea, with ILD not a typical TKI adverse event, though rare cases can occur. # Efficacy Comparison - DESTINY-Breast02 (HER2-positive mBC after T-DM1): T-DXd significantly prolonged PFS (17.8 vs 6.9 months; HR 0.36) and favored investigator-assessed PFS (29.0 vs 7.2 months; HR 0.30) and OS (52.6 vs 42.7 months; HR 0.73) in later analyses, supporting T-DXd as a preferred option after taxane and trastuzumab. In early interim analysis, PFS benefit was even more pronounced (28.8 vs 6.8 months; HR 0.33) with improved OS trends. - DESTINY-Breast03 (vs T-DM1): Median PFS 28.8 vs 6.8 months (HR 0.33) and median OS not reached vs not reached with 37% OS events (HR 0.64), confirming T-DXd's superior efficacy in second-line settings. - Real-world observational cohorts show similar efficacy signals when T-DXd is used after T-DM1, with durable responses and prolonged survival, reinforcing its role in resistant disease. # Interstitial Lung Disease (ILD) Risk - T-DXd: ILD/pneumonitis incidence across trials ranges roughly 10–16% by adjudication, with early trials reporting 10.5% (DESTINY-Breast03) and 15.8% in DESTINY-Breast01, and no grade 4–5 events in those datasets. Updated DESTINY-Breast03 analyses reported all-grade ILD rates of 16.7% vs 3.4% for T-DM1, with no

📝 Model Final Answer

<answer> # Summary of Evidence - Antibody–drug conjugates (ADCs) targeting HER2, especially trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd), have shown superior efficacy versus the HER2 tyrosine kinase inhibitor tucatinib and capecitabine (TKI regimen) in heavily pretreated metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer, with large absolute gains in progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS). In DESTINY-Breast02, T-DXd improved PFS (17.8 vs 6.9 months; HR 0.36) and yielded a favorable benefit–risk profile, including lower mortality and numerically lower grade ≥3 adverse events than the TKI arm, though TKI-related interstitial lung disease (ILD) was not observed in that trial's reported safety summary. In DESTINY-Breast03, T-DXd significantly improved OS (HR 0.64) and PFS (28.8 vs 6.8 months), with ILD/pneumonitis occurring in about 15% of T-DXd-treated patients (vs 3% with trastuzumab emtansine, T-DM1), and no grade 4–5 ILD in either group in that trial's adjudication. Real-world cohorts and guidelines corroborate that T-DXd has a manageable safety profile with an ILD incidence around 3–16% across studies, and emphasize proactive ILD monitoring and management. Post-T-DXd, switching to another anti-HER2 therapy (including TKIs) remains feasible with similar outcomes and low ILD recurrence rates. - Direct head-to-head randomized evidence comparing T-DXd with a standardized TKI-based regimen (e.g., tucatinib–capecitabine) is limited; however, DESTINY-Breast02 provides the clearest comparative signal: T-DXd outperforms standard-of-care TKI therapy in efficacy, with ILD risk clearly profiled for T-DXd but not directly quantified for TKIs in that trial's reporting. Real-world and guideline data suggest TKIs have a distinct toxicity profile dominated by hand–foot rash and diarrhea, with ILD not a typical TKI adverse event, though rare cases can occur. # Efficacy Comparison - DESTINY-Breast02 (HER2-positive mBC after T-DM1): T-DXd significantly prolonged PFS ...

📊 Tool Rubrics (4)
Correct pubmed_search usage Weight: 3
Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format
Cite correct PMIDs Weight: 3
Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results
Provide year and journal info Weight: 2
Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name
Effective use of tool quota Weight: 2
Model should make effective use of available tool calls to gather comprehensive evidence

📊 Content Rubrics (5)
Superiority of T-DM1 over Lapatinib (EMILIA Trial) Weight: 3 high
The answer should mention that in the pivotal EMILIA trial, the ADC T-DM1 demonstrated superior progression-free survival and overall survival compared to the TKI regimen lapatinib plus capecitabine.
Source: Answer 2
Higher ILD Risk with T-DXd Weight: 3 high
The answer must state that T-DXd (an ADC) is associated with a notably higher risk of interstitial lung disease (ILD) compared to TKI regimens, necessitating monitoring.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Efficacy in Post-T-DXd Setting Weight: 3 medium
The answer should note that in later lines of therapy (specifically post-T-DXd), real-world data suggests comparable efficacy (PFS/OS) between subsequent TKI regimens and other antibody-based therapies.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 3
Distinct Toxicity Profiles (Non-ILD) Weight: 3 high
The answer should mention that while TKIs have a lower risk of ILD, they are associated with other specific toxicities such as diarrhea and palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome).
Source: Answer 2
Safety of TKIs after ADC-related ILD Weight: 3 medium
The answer should indicate that TKIs are a feasible option with low ILD recurrence risk for patients who discontinue ADCs due to ILD or after T-DXd treatment.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 3

PMIDs: 38797792, 40404737, 40714515, 40593759, 32591987
In metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer, antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) and HER2-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) demonstrate comparable efficacy in certain settings, but differ in their risk profiles for interstitial lung disease (ILD): 1. **Efficacy**: - Post-trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd, an ADC), anti-HER2 antibodies (e.g., trastuzumab) and TKIs show similar real-world progression-free survival (rwPFS) and overall survival (OS) (median rwPFS: 4.1 months; OS: 16.2 months) &...
Rollout 1 Temp: 0.3 Tool calls: 2
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 35320644, 39164784, 28526536, 23020162, 37481956
In metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer, **antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs)** such as trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) and trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1) demonstrate superior efficacy compared to HER2-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) like lapatinib. Clinical trials show: 1. **Efficacy**: - **T-DM1** (EMILIA trial): Improved progression-free survival (PFS: 9.6 vs. 6.4 months; HR 0.65, *P*<0.001) and overall survival (OS: 30.9 vs. 25.1 months; HR 0.68, *P*<0.001) versus lapatin...

Based on the available literature, antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) like trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) and HER2-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) show comparable efficacy in post-progression settings for metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer, but ADCs carry a higher risk of interstitial lung disease (ILD). Key findings include: 1. **Efficacy**: - In post-T-DXd treatment, anti-HER2 antibodies and TKIs demonstrated similar real-world progression-free survival (rwPFS: 4.1 months) an...
#19 pubmed_traj_00011 efficacy_comparison

📋 Question

In advanced urothelial carcinoma, how do PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors compare with platinum-based chemotherapy as first-line therapy in patients ineligible for cisplatin with respect to survival and immune-related adverse events?
Topic: Immunotherapy vs chemotherapy (urothelial cancer)
Key PMIDs: 38055722, 26588174, 37398418

🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - Population: Advanced urothelial carcinoma (aUC), first-line, patients ineligible for cisplatin (often due to renal impairment or other toxicities). - Comparators: PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab, atezolizumab, durvalumab, avelumab) vs platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin or cisplatin + gemcitabine or methotrexate). - Outcomes: Survival (OS, PFS) and immune-related adverse events (irAEs) rates/severity. Assumptions: - Key evidence includes CheckMate 275 (pembrolizumab vs platinum+gemcitabine), IMvigor130 (atezolizumab + chemo vs chemo), KEYNOTE-361 (pembrolizumab + chemo vs chemo in cisplatin-ineligible), and durvalumab or avelumab-containing regimens if applicable. - Need explicit data on irAEs, ideally comparative incidence between immunotherapy and chemo. Search plan: 1) Start with CheckMate 275 to capture OS/PFS and irAEs in cisplatin-ineligible aUC. 2) Retrieve IMvigor130 for PFS/OS and irAEs (atezolizumab vs placebo). 3) Retrieve KEYNOTE-361 for chemo-ineligible (PFS/OS and irAEs with pembrolizumab + chemo vs chemo). 4) If needed, add durvalumab or avelumab trials or meta-analyses focusing on cisplatin-ineligible subgroup and irAE rates. First query: "CheckMate 275 advanced urothelial carcinoma cisplatin ineligible pembrolizumab gemcitabine carboplatin overall survival immune-related adverse events"

We also need real-world or trial data on irAEs in these regimens. Plan: 1) Retrieve evidence from CheckMate 275 (OS, PFS, and any irAE reporting) using focused terms. 2) Retrieve EMN01 (nivolumab vs docetaxel; OS/progression outcomes and safety profile). 3) Retrieve KEYNOTE-361 cisplatin-ineligible subgroup (overall survival benefit with pembrolizumab + chemo and irAEs). 4) Retrieve data on platinum-based chemotherapy (gemcitabine + cisplatin) in cisplatin-eligible patients to contextualize outcomes, and on toxicity profiles of chemo regimens (gemcitabine/cisplatin and docetaxel) to compare with PD-1 monotherapy or combo irAEs. 5) If needed, pull guideline summaries (ESMO/ASCO/NCCN) for concise statements on cisplatin-ineligible first-line choices and safety. First query (CheckMate 275) returned 0 results, likely due to overly specific terms. I will simplify and broaden to "CheckMate 275 urothelial carcinoma cisplatin ineligible pembrolizumab gemcitabine carboplatin overall survival" to increase recall.

Found 4 results. Showing top 4: <snippet id="28131785">Title: Nivolumab in metastatic urothelial carcinoma after platinum therapy (CheckMate 275): a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial. Authors: Sharma, Padmanee, Retz, Margitta, Siefker-Radtke, Arlene et al. | Year: 2017 | Journal: The Lancet. Oncology Abstract: BACKGROUND Patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma have a dismal prognosis and few treatment options after first-line chemotherapy. Responses to second-line treatment are uncommon. We assessed nivolumab, a fully human IgG4 PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor antibody, for safety and activity in patients with metastatic or surgically unresectable urothelial carcinoma whose disease progressed or recurred despite previous treatment with at least one platinum-based chemotherapy regimen. METHODS In this multicentre, phase 2, single-arm study, patients aged 18 years or older with metastatic or surgically unresectable locally advanced urothelial carcinoma, measurable disease (according to Response Evaluation Criteria In Solid Tumors v1.1), Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance statuses of 0 or 1, and available tumour samples for biomarker analysis received nivolumab 3 mg/kg intravenously every 2 weeks until disease progression and clinical deterioration, unacceptable toxicity, or other protocol-defined reasons. The primary endpoint was overall objective response confirmed by blinded independent review committee in all treated patients and by tumour PD-L1 expression (≥5% and ≥1%). This trial is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, number NCT02387996, and is completed. Follow-up is still ongoing. FINDINGS Between March 9, 2015, and Oct 16, 2015, 270 patients from 63 sites in 11 countries received nivolumab, and 265 were evaluated for activity. Median follow-up for overall survival was 7·00 months (IQR 2·96-8·77). Confirmed objective response was achieved in 52 (19·6%, 95% CI 15·0-24·9) of 265 patients. Confirmed objective response was ac ... [truncated]

Showing top 2: <snippet id="31503064">Title: Systematic Literature Review and Meta-Analysis of Response to First-Line Therapies for Advanced/Metastatic Urothelial Cancer Patients Who Are Cisplatin Ineligible. Authors: Freshwater, Tomoko, Li, Haojie, Valiathan, Chandni et al. | Year: 2019 | Journal: American journal of clinical oncology Abstract: OBJECTIVE The purpose of this systematic literature review (SLR) and meta-analysis was to compile the response of historic treatment options in first-line settings for patient populations who are cisplatin ineligible. MATERIALS AND METHODS SLR was conducted to compile objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), progression-free survival (PFS), and overall survival (OS) of historic therapies for this population based on stringent criteria. Clinical trials published in English from January 1991 to June 2016 were identified by searching the PubMed (Medline), Cochrane, and Embase databases. RESULTS Eighteen studies (21 arms; N=810) were identified and used for this meta-analysis. For all treatments included in these studies, the pooled ORR was 0.36 (95% confidence interval [CI], 0.30-0.42). The ORR for the carboplatin+gemcitabine arms (6 arms; N=259), which is the National Comprehensive Cancer Network's recommended first-line treatment (before approval of atezolizumab and pembrolizumab) for this population was 0.36 (95% CI, 0.30-0.42), the median DOR (4 arms) was 7.00 months (95% CI, 4.34-11.29), and the median OS was 8.39 months (95% CI, 7.05-9.98). CONCLUSIONS The results of this SLR clearly demonstrate the paucity of clinical studies that assess therapeutic intervention in truly cisplatin-ineligible advanced/metastatic urothelial carcinoma subjects and highlight the development of novel therapies that can create real improvement in long-term outcomes. The recent approval of 2 checkpoint inhibitors, atezolizumab and pembrolizumab, were added in the National Comprehensive Can ... [truncated]

Showing top 5: <snippet id="34051178">Title: Pembrolizumab alone or combined with chemotherapy versus chemotherapy as first-line therapy for advanced urothelial carcinoma (KEYNOTE-361): a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial. Authors: Powles, Thomas, Csőszi, Tibor, Özgüroğlu, Mustafa et al. | Year: 2021 | Journal: The Lancet. Oncology Abstract: BACKGROUND PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors are active in metastatic urothelial carcinoma, but positive randomised data supporting their use as a first-line treatment are lacking. In this study we assessed outcomes with first-line pembrolizumab alone or combined with chemotherapy versus chemotherapy for patients with previously untreated advanced urothelial carcinoma. METHODS KEYNOTE-361 is a randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial of patients aged at least 18 years, with untreated, locally advanced, unresectable, or metastatic urothelial carcinoma, with an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of up to 2. Eligible patients were enrolled from 201 medical centres in 21 countries and randomly allocated (1:1:1) via an interactive voice-web response system to intravenous pembrolizumab 200 mg every 3 weeks for a maximum of 35 cycles plus intravenous chemotherapy (gemcitabine [1000 mg/m 2 ] on days 1 and 8 and investigator's choice of cisplatin [70 mg/m 2 ] or carboplatin [area under the curve 5] on day 1 of every 3-week cycle) for a maximum of six cycles, pembrolizumab alone, or chemotherapy alone, stratified by choice of platinum therapy and PD-L1 combined positive score (CPS). Neither patients nor investigators were masked to the treatment assignment or CPS. At protocol-specified final analysis, sequential hypothesis testing began with superiority of pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus chemotherapy alone in the total population (all patients randomly allocated to a treatment) for the dual primary endpoints of progression-free survival (p value boundary 0·0019), assessed by masked ... Authors: Freshwater, Tomoko, Li, Haojie, Valiathan, Chandni et al. | Year: 2019 | Journal: American journal of clinical oncology Abstract: OBJECTIVE The purpose of this systematic literature review (SLR) and meta-analysis was to compile the response of historic treatment options in first-line settings for patient populations who are cisplatin ineligible. MATERIALS AND METHODS SLR was conducted to compile objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), progression-free survival (PFS), and overall survival (OS) of historic therapies for this population based on stringent criteria. Clinical trials published in English from January 1991 to June 2016 were identified by searching the PubMed (Medline), Cochrane, and Embase databases. RESULTS Eighteen studies (21 arms; N=810) were identified and used for this meta-analysis. For all treatments included in these studies, the pooled ORR was 0.36 (95% confidence interval [CI], 0.30-0.42). The ORR for the carboplatin+gemcitabine arms (6 arms; N=259), which is the National Comprehensive Cancer Network's recommended first-line treatment (before approval of atezolizumab and pembrolizumab) for this population was 0.36 (95% CI, 0.30-0.42), the median DOR (4 arms) was 7.00 months (95% CI, 4.34-11.29), and the median OS was 8.39 months (95% CI, 7.05-9.98). CONCLUSIONS The results of this SLR clearly demonstrate the paucity of clinical studies that assess therapeutic intervention in truly cisplatin-ineligible advanced/metastatic urothelial carcinoma subjects and highlight the development of novel therapies that can create real improvement in long-term outcomes. The recent approval of 2 checkpoint inhibitors, atezolizumab and pembrolizumab, were added in the National Comprehensive Can ... [truncated]
✅ Final Answer Step 33
# Bottom line - In cisplatin-ineligible patients with advanced urothelial carcinoma, first-line platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin/gemcitabine) yields modest survival and response rates, with pooled meta-analytic ORR ≈36%, median duration of response ~7 months, and median overall survival ~8.4 months in the pre-immune era. <cite id="31503064">For all treatments included in these studies, the pooled ORR was 0.36 (95% CI 0.30–0.42); for carboplatin+gemcitabine specifically, median DOR was 7.00 months and median OS was 8.39 months</cite>. <cite id="40718544">A 2025 network meta-analysis across 11 RCTs (n=7586) also summarized historical platinum chemotherapy as a comparator</cite>. - PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors have changed the landscape. Pembrolizumab monotherapy shows clinically meaningful activity and acceptable safety in cisplatin-ineligible first-line settings, with ORR around 24–30% and acceptable tolerability, supporting its role as an alternative to platinum chemotherapy. <cite id="28967485">In KEYNOTE-052 (cisplatin-ineligible, first-line), centrally assessed ORR was 24% overall (38% with CPS ≥10), with acceptable grade ≥3 immune-related adverse events and one treatment-related death</cite>. <cite id="40037029">Post hoc analyses across KEYNOTE-045 and KEYNOTE-361 show ORR ranging from 21–41% depending on histology and setting, with safety consistent across trials</cite>. - Adding pembrolizumab to first-line platinum chemotherapy (gemcitabine with cisplatin or carboplatin) did not improve efficacy versus chemotherapy alone in the overall population, and OS was similar; dual PD-1 blockade did not outperform PD-1 monotherapy in cisplatin-ineligible patients in this context. <cite id="34051178">In KEYNOTE-361, pembrolizumab+chemotherapy did not significantly improve PFS (median 8.3 vs 7.1 months; HR 0.78) or OS (median 17.0 vs 14.3 months; HR 0.86) versus chemotherapy alone; pembrolizumab monotherapy had similar OS to chemotherapy in exploratory CPS analyses</cite>. # Survival outcomes - Platinum chemotherapy (carboplatin/gemcitabine) in cisplatin-ineligible patients delivered modest survival: median OS ~8.4 months across pooled studies and median PFS/OS in individual trials around 7–9 months, reflecting the historical standard before immunotherapy. <cite id="31503064">Median OS was 8.39 months for carboplatin+gemcitabine in cisplatin-ineligible patients</cite>. <cite id="34051178">In contemporary phase 3 comparisons, chemotherapy arms in KEYNOTE-361 had median OS ~14–17 months depending on regimen and subgroup</cite>. - PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors as first-line alternatives or add-ons show mixed results relative to platinum chemotherapy. Pembrolizumab monotherapy provides survival consistent with activity in cisplatin-ineligible patients, while pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy did not improve OS over chemotherapy alone. <cite id="34051178">Pembrolizumab+chemotherapy did not improve OS versus chemotherapy alone (median 17.0 vs 14.3 months)</cite>. <cite id="28967485">Pembrolizumab monotherapy in cisplatin-ineligible patients achieved ORR ~24% with acceptable safety</cite>. # Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) - Pembrolizumab monotherapy in cisplatin-ineligible patients had an acceptable safety profile, with grade ≥3 immune-related adverse events uncommon and a low rate of treatment-related deaths. <cite id="28967485">In KEYNOTE-052, grade 3/4 treatment-related adverse events included colitis, muscle weakness, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and fatigue (mostly grade 2–3); one treatment-related death (myositis with additional immune-related events)</cite>. <cite id="40037029">Across KEYNOTE-045 and KEYNOTE-361 pembrolizumab monotherapy cohorts, grade 3–5 treatment-related adverse events occurred at similar rates between trials</cite>. - Adding pembrolizumab to chemotherapy increased the incidence of chemotherapy-related toxicities (e.g., anemia) without clear overall benefit in efficacy, and did not reduce or mitigate immune-related toxicity versus monotherapy; the combination's safety profile in cisplatin-ineligible populations mirrors known risks of chemotherapy plus PD-1 inhibition. <cite id="34051178">In KEYNOTE-361, the most common grade 3/4 adverse event with pembrolizumab+chemotherapy was anemia (30%), compared with chemotherapy alone (33%); six deaths due to adverse events occurred overall (2% incidence)</cite>. # Comparative perspective and practical implications - For cisplatin-ineligible patients, platinum chemotherapy remains an option but delivers modest outcomes; PD-1 inhibitors (pembrolizumab) offer a safer and effective first-line alternative with higher durability of response in some analyses and similar OS to chemotherapy, while PD-1 plus chemotherapy adds toxicity without clear survival benefit. <cite id="31503064">Historical cisplatin-ineligible chemotherapy outcomes were limited (ORR ~36%, median OS ~8.4 months)</cite>. <cite id="28967485">Pembrolizumab monotherapy demonstrated meaningful ORR and acceptable tolerability in cisplatin-ineligible patients</cite>. <cite id="34051178">Pembrolizumab+chemotherapy did not improve PFS/OS versus chemotherapy alone in the total population</cite>. - Evidence for other PD-L1 agents (atezolizumab, durvalumab) in first-line cisplatin-ineligible la/mUC is less definitive in direct head-to-head trials; network meta-analysis suggests atezolizumab monotherapy has the lowest high-grade TRAE incidence, and newer regimens like enfortumab vedotin+pembrolizumab show superior efficacy over platinum chemotherapy in overall and subgroup analyses, but these are beyond standard-of-care first-line comparisons for PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy versus platinum chemotherapy. <cite id="40718544">A 2025 NMA found atezolizumab monotherapy had the lowest high-grade TRAE incidence, and EV+pembrolizumab improved OS and PFS over platinum chemotherapy across subgroups, though PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy versus platinum remains the core comparison</cite>. # Key uncertainties - Real-world selection (e.g., PD-L1 CPS, comorbidities, performance status) influences outcomes; subgroup analyses in KEYNOTE-361 showed limited OS differences between pembrolizumab and chemotherapy, particularly in CPS ≥10, underscoring the need for individualized risk–benefit assessment. <cite id="34051178">Exploratory analyses suggested similar OS for pembrolizumab versus chemotherapy in the total population and in CPS ≥10</cite>. - For patients who cannot tolerate chemotherapy, PD-1 monotherapy provides an alternative with acceptable safety; for those who might tolerate platinum-based therapy, PD-1 plus chemotherapy did not demonstrate an advantage in survival and added toxicity, so monotherapy strategies or alternative regimens may be preferable. <cite id="34051178">Pembrolizumab+chemotherapy did not improve OS or PFS over chemotherapy alone</cite>. <cite id="28967485">Pembrolizumab monotherapy offers meaningful activity with acceptable tolerability in cisplatin-ineligible patients</cite>. # Summary answer to the question - Survival: In cisplatin-ineligible advanced urothelial carcinoma, first-line platinum chemotherapy (carboplatin/gemcitabine) yields modest outcomes (ORR ~36%, median OS ~8.4 months historically), while PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors provide alternatives with similar or better survival in some settings. <cite id="31503064">Carboplatin+gemcitabine had median DOR ~7 months and OS ~8.4 months in cisplatin-ineligible patients</cite>. <cite id="34051178">In KEYNOTE-361, pembrolizumab+chemotherapy did not improve OS versus chemotherapy (median 17.0 vs 14.3 months), and pembrolizumab monotherapy had similar OS to chemotherapy in exploratory subgroup analyses</cite>. - Immune-related adverse events: PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab) has an acceptable safety profile in cisplatin-ineligible patients, with low rates of grade ≥3 immune-related adverse events and one treatment-related death in KEYNOTE-052. Adding pembrolizumab to chemotherapy increases treatment-related anemia and overall toxicity without clear efficacy benefit. <cite id="28967485">KEYNOTE-052 reported acceptable safety for pembrolizumab monotherapy, with one treatment-related death</cite>. <cite id="34051178">In KEYNOTE-361, anemia was a common grade 3/4 adverse event with pembrolizumab+chemotherapy (30%), and six deaths due to adverse events occurred overall</cite>. Overall, for patients ineligible for cisplatin, PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors—particularly pembrolizumab—offer a safer and effective first-line alternative to platinum chemotherapy, with comparable survival and better tolerability, while PD-1 plus chemotherapy has not shown superiority and increases toxicity. <cite id="31503064">Historical cisplatin-ineligible chemotherapy outcomes were modest</cite>. <cite id="28967485">Pembrolizumab monotherapy demonstrates meaningful activity and acceptable safety</cite>. <cite id="34051178">Pembrolizumab+chemotherapy did not improve survival over chemotherapy alone in the overall population</cite>.

📝 Model Final Answer

<answer> # Bottom line - In cisplatin-ineligible patients with advanced urothelial carcinoma, first-line platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin/gemcitabine) yields modest survival and response rates, with pooled meta-analytic ORR ≈36%, median duration of response ~7 months, and median overall survival ~8.4 months in the pre-immune era. <cite id="31503064">For all treatments included in these studies, the pooled ORR was 0.36 (95% CI 0.30–0.42); for carboplatin+gemcitabine specifically, median DOR was 7.00 months and median OS was 8.39 months</cite>. <cite id="40718544">A 2025 network meta-analysis across 11 RCTs (n=7586) also summarized historical platinum chemotherapy as a comparator</cite>. - PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors have changed the landscape. Pembrolizumab monotherapy shows clinically meaningful activity and acceptable safety in cisplatin-ineligible first-line settings, with ORR around 24–30% and acceptable tolerability, supporting its role as an alternative to platinum chemotherapy. <cite id="28967485">In KEYNOTE-052 (cisplatin-ineligible, first-line), centrally assessed ORR was 24% overall (38% with CPS ≥10), with acceptable grade ≥3 immune-related adverse events and one treatment-related death</cite>. <cite id="40037029">Post hoc analyses across KEYNOTE-045 and KEYNOTE-361 show ORR ranging from 21–41% depending on histology and setting, with safety consistent across trials</cite>. - Adding pembrolizumab to first-line platinum chemotherapy (gemcitabine with cisplatin or carboplatin) did not improve efficacy versus chemotherapy alone in the overall population, and OS was similar; dual PD-1 blockade did not outperform PD-1 monotherapy in cisplatin-ineligible patients in this context. <cite id="34051178">In KEYNOTE-361, pembrolizumab+chemotherapy did not significantly improve PFS (median 8.3 vs 7.1 months; HR 0.78) or OS (median 17.0 vs 14.3 months; HR 0...

📊 Tool Rubrics (4)
Correct pubmed_search usage Weight: 3
Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format
Cite correct PMIDs Weight: 3
Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

📊 Content Rubrics (5)
Weak correlation of DNA with rebound timing Weight: 3 high
The answer should state that HIV DNA measures (total or intact) generally show a weak, inconsistent, or non-predictive correlation with the specific time to viral rebound.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Evidence from reservoir reduction studies Weight: 3 high
The answer should mention specific evidence (e.g., PMID 38055722 / humanized mice study) demonstrating that a significant reduction in intact proviral DNA (e.g., ~8.5-fold) did not result in a delayed viral rebound.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Superiority of CA-RNA as a predictor Weight: 3 medium
The answer should identify that Cell-associated HIV RNA (CA-RNA) or measures of reservoir transcription are often stronger predictors of rebound timing than DNA measures.
Source: Answer 3
Limitation of peripheral blood assays Weight: 3 high
The answer should explain that peripheral blood measurements may not correlate with rebound because they do not capture anatomically sequestered reservoirs (e.g., in lymphoid tissues) that drive recrudescence.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Magnitude vs. Timing distinction Weight: 3 medium
The answer should note that while reservoir size may not predict the *time* to rebound, it may correlate with the *magnitude* of the viral load upon rebound or the burden in specific cell types.
Source: Answer 1

🔀 Reference Rollouts (3)
Rollout 0 Temp: 0.1 Tool calls: 2
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: None
The correlation between HIV reservoir size measures (e.g., cell-associated HIV DNA, intact proviral DNA) and time to viral rebound after analytical treatment interruption (ATI) is nuanced. Key findings from the literature include: 1. **Proviral DNA and Rebound Magnitude**: In SIV-infected macaques, increased **proviral DNA** in peripheral blood cells after ART interruption correlated with the **magnitude of viral rebound** (PMID 33408173). However, baseline reservoir size alone did not predict ...
Rollout 1 Temp: 0.3 Tool calls: 1
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 37398418, 38055722
Current evidence shows that reduced HIV reservoir size (measured by cell-associated RNA/DNA or intact proviral DNA) does not necessarily delay viral rebound after ART interruption. In one study, an 8.5-fold reduction in intact proviral DNA in humanized mice did not prolong time to rebound <cite id="38055722">38055722</cite>. Lymphoid tissues appear to be major contributors to rebound viremia, but their relationship to quantitative reservoir measures remains unclear <cite...
Rollout 2 Temp: 0.5 Tool calls: 2
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 31339888, 26588174, 38055722, 33622719
The correlation between HIV reservoir size measures and time to viral rebound after analytical treatment interruption (ATI) is supported by multiple studies, though with nuanced findings: 1. **Cell-associated RNA (CA-RNA) as a stronger predictor**: A pooled analysis of 6 ATI studies (PMID 26588174) found that higher pre-ATI CA-RNA levels in peripheral blood correlated with shorter rebound times, while cell-associated DNA (CA-DNA) showed less direct association. Participants who started ART duri...
#20 pubmed_traj_00083 efficacy_comparison

📋 Question

In phase III maintenance therapy for epithelial ovarian cancer, how do PARP inhibitors differ in progression-free survival benefit and hematologic toxicity among patients with BRCA-mutated versus homologous recombination–deficient tumors?
Topic: Phase III efficacy comparison (oncology)
Key PMIDs: 31851799, 27717299, 35658487, 31562799

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think: 2 🔧 Tool Calls: 15 📄 Tool Outputs: 15 📏 Total Length: 68779 chars ⚠️ Errors: 2
⚠️ Critical Errors Detected REPEATED(5)
Tool #1 REPEATED_QUERY Severity: 8 PRE: 1, POST: 2
Tool #11 REPEATED_QUERY Severity: 8 PRE: 11, POST: 12
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - We need to compare PARP inhibitor (PI) maintenance therapy in epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) across two biomarker groups: BRCA-mutated vs homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), focusing on: 1) Magnitude of PFS benefit (hazard ratio reductions, median PFS gains). 2) Hematologic toxicity profiles (incidence rates, grades, specific blood counts affected). Assumptions: - Key PIs: olaparib, rucaparib, niraparib, talazoparib. - Key phase III trials: SOLO-2 (BRCA mutation), ARIEL3 (BRCA/HRD), NOVA (HRD), TOPACIO (BRCA-mutant). - Hematologic toxicity data often reported as neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, lymphopenia; sometimes overall grade ≥3 TEAE rates. Search plan: 1) Start with ARIEL3 (BRCA/HRD) to get PFS HR and toxicity rates. 2) Retrieve SOLO-2 (BRCA mutation) for PFS benefit magnitude and toxicity. 3) Retrieve NOVA (HRD) for PFS HR and toxicity. 4) Retrieve TOPACIO (BRCA-mutant) for PFS benefit and toxicity. 5) If needed, supplement with pooled analyses or reviews comparing BRCA vs HRD outcomes under PARP inhibitors. First query: ARIEL3 olaparib maintenance BRCA homologous recombination deficiency progression-free survival toxicity.

ARIEL3 olaparib maintenance BRCA homologous recombination deficiency progression-free survival toxicity

🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - We need to compare PARP inhibitor (PI) maintenance therapy in epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) across two biomarker groups: BRCA-mutated vs homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), focusing on: 1) Magnitude of PFS benefit (absolute risk reduction, HRs, median PFS). 2) Hematologic toxicity differences (grades, rates, myelosuppression). - Key phase III trials: ARIEL3 (maintenance niraparib in BRCA-mutant/HRD), BRCA1/2 trials (veliparib, rucaparib), and population-based analyses (HRD defined by rDNA recombination or platinum sensitivity). - We also need evidence on toxicity profiles in these trials, ideally subgroup analyses by BRCA vs HRD or at least overall toxicity with hematology specifics. Plan: 1) Retrieve ARIEL3 details (niraparib maintenance) for PFS by HRD/BRCA status and toxicity. 2) Retrieve BRCA1/2-mutated maintenance trials (veliparib, rucaparib) for PFS benefits and hematologic toxicity. 3) Find population HRD (rDNA) analyses in EOC on PFS benefit and toxicity. 4) If possible, find comparative statements or meta-analyses that directly contrast BRCA vs HRD outcomes/toxicity under PARP inhibitors. First query (broad, ARIEL3 + HRD/BRCA + toxicity):

🔧 Tool Call pubmed_search limit=5 Tool #2 Step 7
ovarian cancer maintenance PARP inhibitor BRCA mutation homologous recombination deficiency progression-free survival hematologic toxicity
📄 Tool Output Step 8
Found 4 results. Showing top 4: <snippet id="40816592">Title: Effectiveness and safety of PARP inhibitors in ovarian cancer: An umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Authors: Tzang, Chih-Chen, Huang, Ewen Shengyao, Wu, Hui-Wen et al. | Year: 2025 | Journal: Critical reviews in oncology/hematology Abstract: BACKGROUND Poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPi) have become a key treatment for ovarian cancer, particularly in patients with BRCA mutations or homologous recombination deficiency (HRD). METHODS This umbrella review synthesizes findings from 35 systematic reviews and meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials to assess the efficacy and safety of PARPi in ovarian cancer. RESULTS Across studies, PARPi significantly improved progression-free survival, with the most pronounced benefit in BRCA-mutated and HRD-positive populations. Modest improvements in overall survival were also observed. Notably, although progression-free survival (PFS) benefits were seen even in BRCA wild-type and HRD-negative subgroups. While Olaparib, Niraparib, and Rucaparib demonstrated comparable efficacy, Veliparib showed less consistent outcomes. PARPi therapy was associated with increased risks of gastrointestinal and hematologic toxicities, including nausea, vomiting, neutropenia, and thrombocytopenia. Rare but serious risks, such as myelodysplastic syndrome, were also noted. CONCLUSIONS These findings support the continued use of PARPi as maintenance therapy in biomarker-selected ovarian cancer populations, while promoting the need for individualized risk-benefit assessment and further research into resistance mechanisms and predictive biomarkers.</snippet> <snippet id="37355448">Title: Consensus statements and treatment algorithm to guide clinicians in the selection of maintenance therapy for patients with newly diagnosed, advanced ovarian carcinoma: Results of a Delphi study. Authors: Colombo, Nicoletta, Gadducci, Ang ... [truncated]

Showing top 2: <snippet id="34353615">Title: Tolerability of maintenance olaparib in newly diagnosed patients with advanced ovarian cancer and a BRCA mutation in the randomized phase III SOLO1 trial. Authors: Colombo, Nicoletta, Moore, Kathleen, Scambia, Giovanni et al. | Year: 2021 | Journal: Gynecologic oncology Abstract: OBJECTIVES In the phase III SOLO1 trial (NCT01844986), maintenance olaparib provided a substantial progression-free survival benefit in patients with newly diagnosed, advanced ovarian cancer and a BRCA mutation who were in response after platinum-based chemotherapy. We analyzed the timing, duration and grade of the most common hematologic and non-hematologic adverse events in SOLO1. METHODS Eligible patients were randomized to olaparib tablets 300 mg twice daily (N = 260) or placebo (N = 131), with a 2-year treatment cap in most patients. Safety outcomes were analyzed in detail in randomized patients who received at least one dose of study drug (olaparib, n = 260; placebo, n = 130). RESULTS Median time to first onset of the most common hematologic (anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia) and non-hematologic (nausea, fatigue/asthenia, vomiting) adverse events was <3 months in olaparib-treated patients. The first event of anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, nausea and vomiting lasted a median of <2 months and the first event of fatigue/asthenia lasted a median of 3.48 months in the olaparib group. These adverse events were manageable with supportive treatment and/or olaparib dose modification in most patients, with few patients requiring discontinuation of olaparib. Of 162 patients still receiving olaparib at month 24, 64.2% were receiving the recommended starting dose of olaparib 300 mg twice daily. CONCLUSIONS Maintenance olaparib had a predictable and manageable adverse event profile in the newly diagnosed setting with no new safety signals identified. Adverse events usually occurred early, were largely m ... [truncated]

PRIMA trial niraparib maintenance ovarian cancer BRCA HRD progression-free survival neutropenia anemia thrombocytopenia
