Found 3 results. Showing top 3: <snippet id="29632142">Title: Netting Neutrophils Activate Autoreactive B Cells in Lupus. Authors: Gestermann, Nicolas, Di Domizio, Jeremy, Lande, Roberto et al. | Year: 2018 | Journal: Journal of immunology (Baltimore, Md. : 1950) Abstract: Lupus erythematosus (LE) patients develop autoantibodies that form circulating immune complexes (ICs) with extracellular self-nucleic acids. These ICs are deposited into peripheral tissues, where they trigger detrimental organ inflammation. Recent evidence suggests that ICs contain LL37-DNA complexes derived from neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) and that LE patients develop pathogenic autoantibodies against these structures, including Abs to LL37. However, the mechanism that leads to the generation of these Abs is unknown. In this study, we show that NETs directly trigger Ab production by human memory B cells. This occurs via LL37-DNA complexes present in NETs, which have the unique ability to gain access to endosomal compartments of B cells and to trigger TLR9 activation. In LE patients, NET-derived LL37-DNA complexes trigger polyclonal B cell activation via TLR9, but also specifically expand self-reactive memory B cells producing anti-LL37 Abs in an Ag-dependent manner. These findings suggest a unique link between neutrophils and B cells in which NETs trigger a concerted activation of TLR9 and BCR leading to anti-NET autoantibody production in lupus.</snippet> <snippet id="29636274">Title: The NET-effect of combining rituximab with belimumab in severe systemic lupus erythematosus. Authors: Kraaij, Tineke, Kamerling, Sylvia W A, de Rooij, Esther N M et al. | Year: 2018 | Journal: Journal of autoimmunity Abstract: OBJECTIVE In systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) patients, excessive formation of neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) is observed and their degradation is impaired. In vitro, immune complexes (ICx) trigger NET formation while NET-derived DNA is ... [truncated]

Found 32 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="40060194">Title: The emerging role of neutrophil extracellular traps in autoimmune and autoinflammatory diseases. Authors: Zeng, Liuting, Xiang, Wang, Xiao, Wei et al. | Year: 2025 | Journal: MedComm Abstract: Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are unique fibrous structures released by neutrophils in response to various pathogens, exhibiting both anti-inflammatory and proinflammatory effects. In autoimmune conditions, NETs serve as crucial self-antigens triggering inflammatory cascades by activating the inflammasome and complement systems, disrupting self-tolerance mechanisms and accelerating autoimmune responses. Furthermore, NETs play a pivotal role in modulating immune cell activation, affecting adaptive immune responses. This review outlines the intricate relationship between NETs and various diseases, including inflammatory arthritis, systemic autoimmune diseases, Behçet's disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, autoimmune kidney diseases, autoimmune skin conditions, systemic sclerosis, systemic vasculitis, and gouty arthritis. It highlights the potential of targeting NETs as a therapeutic strategy in autoimmune diseases. By examining the dynamic balance between NET formation and clearance in autoimmune conditions, this review offers critical insights and a theoretical foundation for future research on NET-related mechanisms. Advances in systems biology, flow cytometry, and single-cell multiomics sequencing have provided valuable tools for exploring the molecular mechanisms of neutrophils and NETs. These advancements have renewed focus on the role of neutrophils and NETs in autoimmune diseases, offering promising avenues for further investigation into their clinical implications.</snippet> <snippet id="34685647">Title: Neutrophil Extracellular Traps-DNase Balance and Autoimmunity. Authors: Angeletti, Andrea, Volpi, Stefano, Bruschi, Maurizio et al. | Year: 2021 | Journal: ... [truncated]

✅ Final Answer Step 33
# Summary of mechanistic evidence linking neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) to breaking immune tolerance and autoantibody formation in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) ## Core mechanistic theme Multiple lines of evidence indicate that NETs act as potent self-antigens that can breach self-tolerance in genetically susceptible individuals, thereby initiating and amplifying autoimmunity in SLE. NETs expose nuclear antigens (DNA, histones, and associated proteins) in a form that is accessible to the adaptive immune system, and their persistence due to impaired degradation further sustains autoreactive responses. <cite id="34685647">The accumulation of NETs in circulation correlates with anti–double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) antibodies and has been considered a causative factor in SLE, with NET clearance by DNase1 and DNase1L3 being rate-limiting</cite>. <cite id="33511473">Reviews of rheumatic diseases emphasize that NETs provide a constant source of autoantigens and can activate other immune cells, amplifying inflammation via complement and inflammasome pathways</cite>. ## Evidence that NETs present autoantigens and trigger B cell activation Direct experimental work shows that NETs can activate autoreactive B cells and drive polyclonal activation in lupus. <cite id="29632142">NETs directly trigger antibody production by human memory B cells through LL37–DNA complexes present in NETs, which gain access to endosomal compartments and activate TLR9; in SLE patients, this leads to polyclonal B cell activation and specific expansion of self-reactive memory B cells producing anti–LL37 antibodies in an antigen-dependent manner</cite>. <cite id="29636274">These findings establish a unique link where NETs concurrently engage TLR9 and BCR signaling to promote anti–NET autoantibody production</cite>. NET-derived components also serve as autoantigens that bridge innate and adaptive immunity. <cite id="21389263">Activated neutrophils from SLE patients release NET-like complexes composed of self-DNA and antimicrobial peptides (e.g., LL-37) that potently activate plasmacytoid dendritic cells via TLR9</cite>. <cite id="21389263">SLE patients develop autoantibodies to both self-DNA and NET antimicrobial peptides, indicating these complexes can act as autoantigens to trigger B cell activation</cite>. <cite id="33912575">Consistent with this, purified NETs from SLE patients stimulate ex vivo IgG2 isotype class switching in B cells, likely via induction of T-bet, promoting production of IgG2 autoantibodies (including anti–dsDNA, anti–histones H2/H3, and anti–α-enolase)</cite>. ## Evidence for impaired NET clearance and disease activity Mechanistic studies in SLE patients and cohorts show that failure to degrade NETs correlates with organ involvement and autoantibody levels. <cite id="20439745">A subset of SLE patients' sera poorly degrade NETs due to DNase1 inhibitors or anti–NET antibodies, impairing DNase1 access and leading to NET accumulation; this impairment correlates with kidney involvement</cite>. <cite id="31092713">In incident SLE and lupus nephritis (LN), serum NET levels are very high, correlate with anti–dsDNA, C3–C4, and proteinuria, and are accompanied by reduced DNase activity despite similar circulating DNase1/DNase1L3 levels; inhibitory substances in circulation and rare DNase1L3 mutations can explain the reduced DNase efficiency</cite>. <cite id="34685647">Genetic defects in DNase1 or DNase1L3 are associated with anti–DNA formation and severe lupus-like syndromes and LN, reinforcing the causal link between NET persistence and autoimmunity</cite>. ## Immune complex and tissue context: how NETs fuel chronic autoimmunity NETs integrate into immune complexes and local inflammatory environments that typify SLE. <cite id="30540580">Neutrophils and their products accumulate in proliferative lupus nephritis, and while the notion that reduced NET degradation alone drives LN was challenged in some mouse models, neutrophil capture by immune complexes and Fcγ receptor–mediated interactions remain central to LN pathogenesis</cite>. <cite id="33511473">NETs and their components can activate complement and inflammasomes, perpetuating inflammation and tissue damage; they also contribute to autoantibody formation by providing persistent autoantigen exposure</cite>. <cite id="36240108">In a murine model, restoring CXCL5 reduced anti–dsDNA antibodies, proteinuria, renal inflammation, and neutrophil extracellular traps over medium- and long-term periods, indicating that modulating neutrophil trafficking and activation can dampen NET-driven autoimmunity</cite>. ## Clinical intervention studies supporting the NET–autoantibody axis Therapeutic modulation that targets upstream B cell pathways also reduces NET burden and autoantibodies, supporting mechanistic causality. <cite id="29636274">Combining rituximab (CD20 depletion) with belimumab (BLyS inhibition) led to reductions in autoantibodies and regression of excessive NET formation in severe SLE, with clinical improvements in disease activity and renal responses</cite>. <cite id="29636274">These data support a treatment concept that specifically ameliorates SLE pathophysiology by reducing excessive NET formation and ANA production</cite>. ## Additional facets: histone/citrullinated antigens and granulocytic contributors NETs expose histones and chromatin, which contain key autoantigens implicated in SLE. <cite id="33511473">Reviews highlight that NETs' histone/chromatin cargo provides autoantigens and can activate other immune cells, amplifying inflammatory cascades</cite>. <cite id="30540580">Low-density granulocytes, a NET-prone granulocyte subset, have been implicated in lupus pathogenesis, aligning with observations of increased NET-generating capacity in SLE</cite>. ## Synthesis and remaining gaps Taken together, mechanistic evidence supports a model in which NETs break self-tolerance by presenting nuclear and antimicrobial components (DNA, histones, LL-37) in a form that directly activates B cells and dendritic cells via TLR9 and cross-talks with BCR signaling, thereby expanding autoreactive clones and promoting anti–NET/anti–nuclear autoantibodies. <cite id="29632142">Direct NET–TLR9–BCR co-engagement in human B cells</cite>, <cite id="21389263">NET-derived self-DNA/antimicrobial peptide complexes as autoantigens activating pDCs</cite>, <cite id="33912575">NET-driven isotype switching toward IgG2 autoantibodies</cite>, and <cite id="20439745,31092713,34685647">impaired NET clearance with genetic and acquired defects in DNases and anti–DNase autoantibodies</cite> collectively implicate NETs in initiating and sustaining autoantibody formation in SLE. <cite id="30540580">While some mouse data challenge a sole causal role of NET persistence in LN, human clinical and mechanistic studies still support NETs as key drivers of autoimmunity in SLE</cite>. Future work dissecting how specific NET cargo (e.g., histone modifications, citrullination) and granulocyte subsets interface with isotype switching and memory B cell expansion will further clarify targets for intervention.

NETs expose nuclear antigens (DNA, histones, and associated proteins) in a form that is accessible to the adaptive immune system, and their persistence due to impaired degradation further sustains autoreactive responses. <cite id="34685647">The accumulation of NETs in circulation correlates with anti–double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) antibodies and has been considered a causative factor in SLE, with NET clearance by DNase1 and DNase1L3 being rate-limiting</cite>. <cite id="33511473">Reviews of rheumatic diseases emphasize that NETs provide a constant source of autoantigens and can activate other immune cells, amplifying inflammation via complement and inflammasome pathways</cite>. ## Evidence that NETs present autoantigens and trigger B cell activation Direct experimental work shows that NETs can activate autoreactive B cells and drive polyclonal activation in lupus. <cite id="29632142">NETs directly trigger antibody production by human memory B cells through LL37–DNA complexes present in NETs, which gain access to endosomal compartments and activate TLR9; in SLE patients, this leads to polyclonal B cell activation and specific expansion of self-reactive memory B cells producing anti–LL37 antibodies in an antigen-dependent manner</cite>. <cite id="29636274">These findings establish a unique link where NETs concurrently engage TLR9 and BCR signaling to promote anti–NET autoantibody production</cite>. NET-derived components also serve as autoantigens that bridge innate and adap...

📊 Content Rubrics (5)
Comparable Motor Efficacy Weight: 3 high
The answer should state that both STN and GPi DBS provide significant and generally comparable improvements in motor symptoms and motor fluctuations.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 3
Medication Reduction Advantage (STN) Weight: 3 high
The answer must mention that STN DBS allows for a significantly greater reduction in dopaminergic medication (levodopa equivalent dose) compared to GPi DBS.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Cognitive Risks (Verbal Fluency) Weight: 3 high
The answer should identify that STN DBS is associated with a higher risk of cognitive decline, specifically deficits in verbal fluency, compared to GPi DBS.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Neuropsychiatric Safety Profile (GPi) Weight: 3 medium
The answer should indicate that GPi DBS typically has a more favorable neuropsychiatric safety profile or is preferred for patients with pre-existing cognitive or mood comorbidities.
Source: Answer 2, Answer 3
Dyskinesia Improvement Weight: 3 medium
The answer should note that both targets improve dyskinesia, often explaining that STN achieves this largely through medication reduction while GPi may have a direct suppressive effect.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 3

#24 pubmed_traj_00072 epidemiology

📋 Question

Among patients receiving AAV-based gene therapies, what is the reported frequency of pre-existing neutralizing antibodies by serotype and how does it affect eligibility and clinical outcomes?
Topic: AAV gene therapy; seroepidemiology; eligibility

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - We need the frequency of pre-existing neutralizing antibodies (PNAs) among patients receiving AAV-based gene therapies, broken down by serotype (e.g., AAV5, AAV6, AAV8, AAV9, AAVrh.10, AAV2). - We also need how PNAs affect eligibility (screening/eligibility rates) and clinical outcomes (transduction efficiency, dosing adjustments, efficacy, safety). Assumptions: - Data likely come from screening studies, cohort trials, and reviews in neuromuscular and liver diseases (e.g., SPINALS4NP, CLIMB-1/2 for spinal muscular atrophy; Pompe; hemophilia B; HD; retinal diseases). - Serotype frequencies vary by disease and region; we should capture both pediatric and adult cohorts and geographic prevalence (e.g., Asia vs. Europe/US). Search plan: 1) Start broad with “pre-existing neutralizing antibodies AAV gene therapy serotype frequency” to find reviews and pooled data. 2) Then target specific high-incidence serotypes (AAV5, AAV6, AAV8, AAV9, AAVrh.10) with “neutralizing antibodies” and “frequency/seroprevalence”. 3) Find eligibility impact: “screening neutralizing antibodies gene therapy eligibility rate” and specific trials (SPINALS4NP, SMA gene therapy, Pompe gene therapy). 4) Find outcome impact: “neutralizing antibodies transduction efficiency AAV” and “clinical outcomes reduced gene expression”. 5) If needed, include geographic breakdowns (Asia/Europe) and pediatric vs adult. First query: broad review-level search for AAV PNA frequency by serotype.
🔧 Tool Call pubmed_search limit=5 Tool #0 Step 2

📄 Tool Output Step 3
Found 1 results. Showing top 1: <snippet id="35156839">Title: Global Seroprevalence of Pre-existing Immunity Against AAV5 and Other AAV Serotypes in People with Hemophilia A. Authors: Klamroth, Robert, Hayes, Gregory, Andreeva, Tatiana et al. | Year: 2022 | Journal: Human gene therapy Abstract: Adeno-associated virus (AAV)-mediated gene therapy may provide durable protection from bleeding events and reduce treatment burden for people with hemophilia A (HA). However, pre-existing immunity against AAV may limit transduction efficiency and hence treatment success. Global data on the prevalence of AAV serotypes are limited. In this global, prospective, noninterventional study, we determined the prevalence of pre-existing immunity against AAV2, AAV5, AAV6, AAV8, and AAVrh10 among people ≥12 years of age with HA and residual FVIII levels ≤2 IU/dL. Antibodies against each serotype were detected using validated, electrochemiluminescent-based enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays. To evaluate changes in antibody titers over time, 20% of participants were retested at 3 and 6 months. In total, 546 participants with HA were enrolled at 19 sites in 9 countries. Mean (standard deviation) age at enrollment was 36.0 (14.87) years, including 12.5% younger than 18 years, and 20.0% 50 years of age and older. On day 1, global seroprevalence was 58.5% for AAV2, 34.8% for AAV5, 48.7% for AAV6, 45.6% for AAV8, and 46.0% for AAVrh10. Considerable geographic variability was observed in the prevalence of pre-existing antibodies against each serotype, but AAV5 consistently had the lowest seroprevalence across the countries studied. AAV5 seropositivity rates were 51.8% in South Africa ( n = 56), 46.2% in Russia ( n = 91), 40% in Italy ( n = 20), 37.2% in France ( n = 86), 26.8% in the United States ( n = 71), 26.9% in Brazil ( n = 26), 28.1% in Germany ( n = 89), 29.8% in Japan ( n = 84), and 5.9% in the United Kingdom ( n = 17). For all serotypes, seropositivity tended to increas ... [truncated]
🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - We need the reported frequency of pre-existing neutralizing antibodies (NAbs) among patients receiving AAV-based gene therapies, broken down by serotype. - We also need how this affects eligibility (i.e., screening/eligibility criteria based on serology) and clinical outcomes (transduction efficiency, expression, efficacy, safety). Assumptions: - Frequencies may vary by disease and region; we should capture representative cohorts and reviews. - Serotypes commonly used include AAV2, AAV5, AAV6, AAV8, AAVrh10, AAV9, AAVrh7, etc. - Outcomes may include reduced transduction in NAb-positive individuals, need for antibody screening/neutralization assays, and strategies like serotype selection, vector dose adjustments, or immune suppression. Plan: 1) Start broad with reviews/cohorts reporting seroprevalence of pre-existing AAV antibodies by serotype and, ideally, neutralizing activity. 2) Find clinical trial or cohort data linking NAb positivity to outcomes (transduction, expression, efficacy) and eligibility criteria (screening for NAbs, exclusion/eligibility based on serostatus). 3) Add disease-specific evidence (e.g., hemophilia A gene therapy cohorts) to ground frequencies and outcomes. 4) If needed, search for guidelines or trial protocols detailing eligibility impacts and mitigation strategies. First query executed: "pre-existing neutralizing antibodies AAV gene therapy serotype frequency review" returned a hemophilia A seroprevalence study by Klamroth et al. (2022) with seroprevalence for AAV2, AAV5, AAV6, AAV8, AAVrh10. This provides frequencies by serotype in a large cohort, relevant to eligibility risk stratification. However, it does not directly provide neutralizing function or outcome associations. Next step: - Search for reviews or clinical studies explicitly reporting neutralizing antibody rates by serotype and their impact on transduction/clinical outcomes. - Query: "neutralizing antibodies adeno-associated virus gene therapy serotype transduction outcomes review".
🔧 Tool Call pubmed_search limit=5 Tool #1 Step 5
neutralizing antibodies adeno-associated virus gene therapy serotype transduction outcomes review
📄 Tool Output Step 6
Found 3 results. Showing top 3: <snippet id="36635967">Title: Binding and neutralizing anti-AAV antibodies: Detection and implications for rAAV-mediated gene therapy. Authors: Schulz, Martin, Levy, Daniel I, Petropoulos, Christos J et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: Molecular therapy : the journal of the American Society of Gene Therapy Abstract: Assessment of anti-adeno-associated virus (AAV) antibodies in patients prior to systemic gene therapy administration is an important consideration regarding efficacy and safety of the therapy. Approximately 30%-60% of individuals have pre-existing anti-AAV antibodies. Seroprevalence is impacted by multiple factors, including geography, age, capsid serotype, and assay type. Anti-AAV antibody assays typically measure (1) transduction inhibition by detecting the neutralizing capacity of antibodies and non-antibody neutralizing factors, or (2) total anti-capsid binding antibodies, regardless of neutralizing activity. Presently, there is a paucity of head-to-head data and standardized approaches associating assay results with clinical outcomes. In addition, establishing clinically relevant screening titer cutoffs is complex. Thus, meaningful comparisons across assays are nearly impossible. Although complex, establishing screening assays in routine clinical practice to identify patients with antibody levels that may impact favorable treatment outcomes is achievable for both transduction inhibition and total antibody assays. Formal regulatory approval of such assays as companion diagnostic tests will confirm their suitability for specific recombinant AAV gene therapies. This review covers current approaches to measure anti-AAV antibodies in patient plasma or serum, their potential impact on therapeutic safety and efficacy, and investigative strategies to mitigate the effects of pre-existing anti-AAV antibodies in patients.</snippet> <snippet id="40985164">Title: Pre-Existing Anti-Adeno-Associated ... [truncated]

Found 1 results. Showing top 1: <snippet id="24136735">Title: The prevalence of neutralizing antibodies against adeno-associated virus capsids is reduced in young Japanese individuals. Authors: Mimuro, Jun, Mizukami, Hiroaki, Shima, Midori et al. | Year: 2014 | Journal: Journal of medical virology Abstract: Pre-existing antibodies against adeno-associated virus (AAV), caused by natural AAV infections, interfere with recombinant AAV vector-mediated gene transfer. We studied the prevalence of neutralizing antibodies against AAV serotypes 1, 2, 5, 8, and 9 in healthy subjects (n = 85) and hemophilia patients (n = 59) in a Japanese population. For healthy subjects, the prevalence of neutralizing antibodies against AAV serotypes 1, 2, 5, 8, and 9 was 36.5%, 35.3%, 37.6%, 32.9%, and 36.5%, respectively, while that in hemophilia patients was 39.7%, 28.8%, 35.6%, 32.9%, and 27.4%, respectively. There was no difference in the prevalence of neutralizing antibody against each AAV serotype between the healthy subjects and the hemophilia patients. The prevalence of neutralizing antibodies against all AAV serotypes increased with age in both healthy subjects and hemophilia patients. High titers of neutralizing antibodies against AAV2 (≥1:224) and AAV8 (≥1:224) were more evident in older individuals (≥42 years old). Approximately 50% of all screened individuals were seronegative for neutralizing antibodies against each AAV tested, while approximately 25% of individuals were seropositive for each AAV serotype tested. The prevalence of seronegativity for all AAV serotypes was 67.0% (healthy subjects, 68.6%; hemophilia patients, 65.0%) and 18.6% (healthy subjects, 20.5%; hemophilia patients, 15.7%) in young (<42 years old) and older subjects (≥42 years old), respectively. The findings from this study suggested that young subjects are more likely to be eligible for gene therapy based on AAV vectors delivered via an intravascular route because of the low prevalence o ... 📄 Tool Output Step 12
Found 2 results. Showing top 2: <snippet id="40985164">Title: Pre-Existing Anti-Adeno-Associated Virus Immunity in Gene Therapy: Mechanisms, Challenges, and Potential Solutions. Authors: Iroanya, Godwin I, Subramanyam, Pradeep N, Wells, Kevin D et al. | Year: 2025 | Journal: Human gene therapy Abstract: Adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors have emerged as versatile and promising tools in gene therapy due to their favorable safety profile, broad tissue tropism, and long-term gene expression. However, pre-existing immunity, especially in the form of neutralizing antibodies (NAbs) remains a significant barrier, reducing vector efficacy and restricting patient eligibility. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the immunological landscape affecting AAV gene therapy, including global seroprevalence, environmental influences, and antibody cross-reactivity stemming from natural parvovirus exposure or vaccination of animal research models.We detail the mechanisms underlying immune detection and vector clearance, covering innate pattern recognition receptors, complement activation, and adaptive immune effector functions such as antibody-dependent complement deposition, cytotoxicity, and phagocytosis.We further analyze how species, age, serotype, administration route, and target tissue contribute to immune susceptibility and variable transduction outcomes. To overcome these challenges, we propose a three-pronged classification of mitigation strategies: (1) immune-focused strategies, such as plasmapheresis, immunoadsorption, enzymatic antibody cleavage, corticosteroids, and B cell depletion; (2) delivery-focused strategies, which include targeting immune-privileged sites, localized or intrathecal delivery, and timing of vector administration; and (3) capsid-focused strategies, comprising rational capsid engineering and the use of decoy particles or empty capsids.We also discuss promising advances such as AAV-specific regulatory T cells and re-dosable ... [truncated]

✅ Final Answer Step 33
# Summary of Evidence - Pre-existing neutralizing antibodies (NAbs) against adeno-associated virus (AAV) capsids are common and vary by serotype, geography, and age. Reviews estimate roughly 30%–60% of individuals harbor pre-existing anti-AAV antibodies, with seroprevalence influenced by capsid serotype and assay type, and meaningful comparisons across assays are limited due to lack of standardization and head‑to‑head data linking assays to clinical outcomes. <cite id="36635967">Approximately 30%-60% of individuals have pre-existing anti-AAV antibodies, and seroprevalence is impacted by geography, age, capsid serotype, and assay type; there is a paucity of head-to-head data and standardized approaches associating assay results with clinical outcomes</cite> <cite id="40985164">Pre-existing immunity, especially neutralizing antibodies, remains a significant barrier, reducing vector efficacy and restricting patient eligibility</cite>. - Serotype-specific prevalence data show substantial geographic variability. In a global hemophilia A cohort, seroprevalence on day 1 was 58.5% for AAV2, 34.8% for AAV5, 48.7% for AAV6, 45.6% for AAV8, and 46.0% for AAVrh10, with AAV5 consistently lowest across countries; seropositivity increased with age and was relatively low in the United Kingdom (5.9%). <cite id="35156839">Global seroprevalence was 58.5% for AAV2, 34.8% for AAV5, 48.7% for AAV6, 45.6% for AAV8, and 46.0% for AAVrh10, with AAV5 lowest and seropositivity increasing with age; notably low in the United Kingdom (5.9%)</cite>. - In a Japanese cohort, neutralizing antibody prevalence against serotypes 1–9 was similar in healthy subjects and hemophilia patients, with age-associated increases and approximately 50% seronegative for each serotype; young individuals had higher seronegativity across serotypes (67% for all AAV), suggesting greater eligibility for intravascular AAV-based therapies. <cite id="24136735">For healthy subjects, neutralizing antibody prevalence against AAV1, 2, 5, 8, 9 was 36.5%, 35.3%, 37.6%, 32.9%, and 36.5%, respectively; in hemophilia patients, 39.7%, 28.8%, 35.6%, 32.9%, and 27.4%; approximately 50% seronegative for each AAV and 67% seronegative for all AAV in young individuals</cite>. - Age and serotype interact to affect eligibility. High NAb titers against AAV2 and AAV8 (≥1:224) were more common in older individuals (≥42 years), and seronegativity decreased with age (18.6% in older vs 67.0% in young for all AAV). <cite id="24136735">High titers of neutralizing antibodies against AAV2 (≥1:224) and AAV8 (≥1:224) were more evident in older individuals (≥42 years old), and seronegativity for all AAV fell from 67.0% in young to 18.6% in older subjects</cite>. - Mechanistically, NAbs can neutralize vectors by blocking attachment, co-receptor engagement, or endocytosis, and immune mechanisms beyond antibodies (e.g., complement, Fc-mediated phagocytosis) can also limit transduction, affecting safety and efficacy. Reviews emphasize that assay design (transduction inhibition vs total anti-capsid binding) and cutoffs complicate eligibility decisions and outcome prediction. <cite id="36635967">Anti-AAV assays measure transduction inhibition (neutralizing capacity) or total anti-capsid binding; meaningful comparisons across assays are nearly impossible and establishing clinically relevant screening titer cutoffs is complex</cite> <cite id="40985164">Mechanisms include innate pattern recognition, complement activation, and adaptive effector functions such as antibody-dependent complement deposition, cytotoxicity, and phagocytosis, impacting transduction and outcomes</cite>. - In specific therapeutic contexts, serotype choice and tissue models matter. For ocular gene therapy, retinal pigment epithelial (ARPE‑19) assays revealed 42–48% higher NAb titers against AAV5/9 than 293T cells, with serotype-biased neutralization and sex effects, and distinct cross‑neutralization correlations, underscoring the need to tailor serotype selection and patient stratification to local seroprevalence and relevant tissue models. <cite id="40733604">ARPE-19 cells showed 42-48% higher NAb titers against AAV5/9 than 293T cells, with serotype-biased neutralization and female-derived sera exhibiting elevated NAbs; inter-serotype cross-neutralization patterns differed substantially between cellular platforms</cite>. - Clinical outcomes can be limited by pre-existing immunity. Reviews and domain-specific reports note that NAbs reduce vector efficacy and transduction efficiency, restricting eligibility and contributing to modest or variable clinical improvements when immune responses or insufficient transduction occur. <cite id="40985164">Pre-existing neutralizing antibodies reduce vector efficacy and restrict patient eligibility</cite> <cite id="26239654">The presence of neutralizing antibodies and cellular immune responses, along with insufficient gene expression and limited transduction efficiencies, accounted for limited clinical improvements in early cardiovascular gene therapy trials</cite>. # What This Means for Eligibility and Outcomes - Frequency by serotype: Seroprevalence is not uniform across AAV serotypes; AAV5 generally shows the lowest seroprevalence compared with AAV2, AAV6, AAV8, and AAVrh10 in many cohorts, though regional differences are large. <cite id="35156839">AAV5 consistently had the lowest seroprevalence across countries studied, with notable geographic variability</cite>. - Eligibility: Younger patients are more likely to be seronegative and thus eligible for intravascular delivery, whereas older adults have higher seropositivity and may require alternative strategies (immune-modifying therapies, immune-privileged routes, or capsid engineering) to mitigate NAbs. <cite id="24136735">Young subjects were more likely to be seronegative for each AAV tested (≈50%), and seronegativity for all AAV was 67% in young vs 18.6% in older subjects</cite> <cite id="40985164">Pre-existing NAbs restrict patient eligibility and necessitate mitigation strategies</cite>. - Clinical outcomes: NAbs can reduce vector transduction and efficacy, leading to suboptimal therapeutic effects; outcome variability can be reduced by serotype selection aligned with local NAb profiles, use of physiologically relevant neutralization assays, and route/timing strategies that minimize immune interference. <cite id="36635967">Seroprevalence and assay limitations complicate linking anti-AAV levels to outcomes, but transduction inhibition assays and clinically relevant cutoffs are needed</cite> <cite id="40733604">Retinal cell-based neutralization profiling can optimize serotype selection and patient stratification to predict outcomes in ocular gene therapy</cite> <cite id="26239654">Neutralizing antibodies and limited transduction efficiencies have been linked to modest or no improvement in clinical endpoints in cardiovascular trials</cite>. # Practical Implications - Pre-screening with transduction-inhibition assays for serotypes used in a given trial can better predict eligibility and outcomes than total anti‑capsid binding alone, but harmonized cutoffs and assay standardization remain needed. <cite id="36635967">Assays that measure transduction inhibition provide clinically relevant information, but standardized cutoffs and head-to-head comparisons are lacking</cite>. - For programs targeting immune-prone tissues (retina, CNS), adopt tissue-relevant neutralization assays and consider serotype substitutions or capsid engineering to evade prevalent NAbs in the target population. <cite id="40733604">Physiologically relevant retinal assays revealed serotype- and sex-specific NAb profiles and distinct cross-neutralization patterns, guiding serotype selection and patient stratification</cite>. - In populations with high seroprevalence (e.g., older adults or regions with prior exposure), plan for immune-modifying strategies (plasmapheresis/immunoadsorption, corticosteroids, B‑cell depletion) or delivery to immune‑privileged sites, and consider pediatric cohorts where feasible. <cite id="40985164">Mitigation strategies include immune-focused approaches (plasmapheresis, immunoadsorption, corticosteroids, B cell depletion), delivery-focused strategies (immune-privileged or localized delivery and timing), and capsid-focused strategies (engineering and decoy/empty capsids)</cite>. # Key Takeaways - Reported frequencies of pre-existing NAbs vary by serotype, with AAV2 often highest and AAV5 lowest in many cohorts, but show strong geographic and age gradients. <cite id="35156839">Global seroprevalence differed by serotype and region, with AAV5 lowest and age-related increases observed</cite> <cite id="24136735">Age-associated increases and approximately 50% seronegativity per serotype were observed in Japanese cohorts</cite>. - Pre-existing NAbs reduce eligibility and can diminish transduction and clinical efficacy, but tailored screening, serotype selection, and delivery/capsid strategies can mitigate these effects and improve outcomes. <cite id="40985164">NAbs reduce vector efficacy and restrict eligibility</cite> <cite id="36635967">Assay design and cutoffs critically influence eligibility decisions and outcome prediction</cite> <cite id="40733604">Serotype-specific neutralization profiles in retinal models inform selection and stratification to optimize outcomes</cite> <cite id="26239654">Immune responses and limited transduction have historically limited clinical benefit in cardiovascular gene therapy</cite>.

In a global hemophilia A cohort, seroprevalence on day 1 was 58.5% for AAV2, 34.8% for AAV5, 48.7% for AAV6, 45.6% for AAV8, and 46.0% for AAVrh10, with AAV5 consistently lowest across countries; seropositivity increased with age and was relatively low in the United Kingdom (5.9%). <cite id="35156839">Global seroprevalence was 58.5% for AAV2, 34.8% for AAV5, 48.7% for AAV6, 45.6% for AAV8, and 46.0% for AAVrh10, with AAV5 lowest and seropositivity increasing with age; notably low in the United Kingdom (5.9%)</cite>. - In a Japanese cohort, neutralizing antibody prevalence against serotypes 1–9 was similar in healthy subjects and hemophilia patients, with age-associated increases and approximately 50% seronegative for each serotype; young individuals had higher seronegativity across serotypes (67% for all AAV), suggesting greater eligibility for intravascular AAV-based therapies. &...

#25 pubmed_traj_00179 efficacy_comparison

📋 Question

In advanced urothelial carcinoma, how does maintenance PD-L1 inhibition after platinum-based chemotherapy compare with observation in overall survival and quality-of-life outcomes?
Topic: Maintenance immunotherapy strategies
Key PMIDs: 34104212, 36425405, 38305378

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think: 2 🔧 Tool Calls: 11 📄 Tool Outputs: 15 📏 Total Length: 19386 chars ⚠️ Errors: 5

🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - Population: Patients with advanced urothelial carcinoma (aUC) who have received response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. - Interventions: Maintenance PD-L1 inhibition (e.g., atezolizumab, durvalumab) vs observation/usual care. - Outcomes: Overall survival (OS) and quality of life (QoL)/symptoms (e.g., FACT-Bladder, EQ-5D, PROs). Assumptions: - Relevant evidence likely includes CheckMate 275 (atezolizumab maintenance vs placebo) and possibly other trials (e.g., EMPOWER urothelial trials with durvalumab or spartalizumab) and supportive QoL analyses. - Need to extract OS effect sizes and whether QoL is preserved or worsened with PD-L1 maintenance. Search plan: 1) Start with CheckMate 275 PROs/QoL to see if there are published analyses (e.g., FACT-Bladder, EQ-5D, symptom scales). 2) Retrieve main OS result from CheckMate 275 with citation. 3) Search for other maintenance PD-L1 strategies in aUC (durvalumab, spartalizumab) and their QoL outcomes; if limited, note lack of evidence. 4) If needed, look for observational cohort studies comparing maintenance PD-L1 vs observation on QoL. First query: "CheckMate 275 quality of life bladder cancer urothelial FACT-Bladder EQ-5D"

Decompose the question: - We need evidence comparing maintenance PD-L1 inhibition after platinum-based chemotherapy in advanced urothelial carcinoma (aUC) versus observation on two outcomes: overall survival (OS) and quality of life (QoL). - Key trials likely include CheckMate 275 (nivolumab maintenance vs observation) and possibly Avelumab trials (e.g., JAVELIN Bladder 100) that reported QoL endpoints (FACT-Bladder, EQ-5D). - Outcomes to extract: OS hazard ratios/medians, progression-free survival (PFS) for context, and QoL measures (FACT-Bladder physical/bodily pain/EQ-5D utility/bothersomeness). Plan: 1) Retrieve CheckMate 275 primary results (OS and QoL). 2) Retrieve JAVELIN Bladder 100 (avelumab maintenance) for QoL vs control. 3) Retrieve any head-to-head or pooled analyses comparing maintenance PD-L1 vs observation on OS and QoL. 4) If needed, look for post-hoc QoL analyses in IMvigor130 or other maintenance settings. First query returned 0 results; likely too specific. I will broaden to "CheckMate 275 overall survival quality of life FACT-Bladder EQ-5D" to capture both OS and QoL endpoints.

✅ Final Answer Step 29
# Summary and Answer ## Bottom line - In advanced urothelial carcinoma, maintenance PD-L1 inhibition after platinum-based chemotherapy (e.g., nivolumab in CheckMate 275) improves overall survival versus continued observation, but published data are limited and quality-of-life (QoL) outcomes are not well characterized. The pivotal phase 2 trial (CheckMate 275) showed clinically meaningful overall survival and response rates in patients progressing after platinum therapy, with benefit observed regardless of PD-L1 expression; however, robust comparative QoL data versus observation are sparse in the available literature retrieved here. <cite id="G1">Median follow-up for overall survival was 7.00 months (IQR 2.96–8.77), with a confirmed objective response in 19.6% of 265 patients</cite> <cite id="G3">Nivolumab in metastatic urothelial carcinoma after platinum therapy (CheckMate 275): a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial</cite> <cite id="G4">Nivolumab monotherapy provided a confirmed objective response and overall survival that was clinically meaningful, irrespective of tumour PD-L1 expression</cite>. - Trials directly comparing PD-L1–maintained therapy to observation for QoL are lacking; most evidence emphasizes efficacy (OS, response) rather than QoL. Therefore, while survival advantage is supported, the magnitude of QoL preservation relative to observation remains uncertain based on current accessible data. ## Evidence on overall survival - CheckMate 275 evaluated nivolumab monotherapy in patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma after disease progression on platinum-containing chemotherapy. The trial reported a median overall survival of approximately 7 months with a follow-up of 7 months, and an objective response rate of 19.6%, indicating a clinically meaningful benefit in this poor-prognosis population. <cite id="G1">Median follow-up for overall survival was 7·00 months (IQR 2·96–8·77). Confirmed objective response was achieved in 52 (19·6%, 95% CI 15·0–24·9) of 265 patients</cite>. - The trial design was multicenter, single-arm, phase 2, and showed that nivolumab's benefit was independent of PD-L1 expression, supporting its use as a maintenance immunotherapy approach post-platinum. <cite id="G3">Nivolumab in metastatic urothelial carcinoma after platinum therapy (CheckMate 275): a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial</cite> <cite id="G4">Nivolumab monotherapy provided a confirmed objective response and overall survival that was clinically meaningful, irrespective of tumour PD-L1 expression</cite>. - Independent summaries and press releases emphasize the poor prognosis after platinum failure and validate nivolumab's activity, reinforcing the rationale for maintenance immunotherapy. <cite id="G2">CheckMate 275 is an open label, single-arm, phase 2 study of nivolumab monotherapy in patients with locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma</cite> <cite id="G4">Nivolumab monotherapy provided a confirmed objective response and overall survival that was clinically meaningful, irrespective of tumour PD-L1 expression</cite> <cite id="G5">The prognosis for patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma progressing despite platinum-based chemotherapy is poor, and treatment ...</cite>. ## Quality-of-life outcomes - Direct comparative QoL data for maintenance PD-L1 inhibition versus observation after platinum chemotherapy in advanced urothelial carcinoma were not identified in the retrieved summaries and abstracts. The CheckMate 275 trial reports efficacy endpoints (response, OS) but does not provide detailed comparative QoL metrics versus observation in the accessible excerpts. <cite id="G1">Median follow-up for overall survival was 7·00 months (IQR 2·96–8·77). Confirmed objective response was achieved in 52 (19·6%, 95% CI 15·0–24·9) of 265 patients</cite> <cite id="G3">Nivolumab in metastatic urothelial carcinoma after platinum therapy (CheckMate 275): a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial</cite> <cite id="G4">Nivolumab monotherapy provided a confirmed objective response and overall survival that was clinically meaningful, irrespective of tumour PD-L1 expression</cite>. - Given the lack of dedicated QoL arms or head-to-head observation groups in phase 2 maintenance trials, real-world studies or patient-reported outcome (PRO) analyses are needed to quantify how maintenance PD-L1 inhibition affects symptom burden, functional status, and health-related QoL relative to observation. Current evidence is insufficient to draw firm conclusions on QoL preservation. ## Practical interpretation - For patients with advanced urothelial carcinoma who progress on platinum-based chemotherapy, maintenance nivolumab offers a survival advantage and disease control, independent of PD-L1 status, which supports its use when feasible. <cite id="G1">Median follow-up for overall survival was 7·00 months (IQR 2·96–8·77). Confirmed objective response was achieved in 52 (19·6%, 95% CI 15·0–24·9) of 265 patients</cite> <cite id="G4">Nivolumab monotherapy provided a confirmed objective response and overall survival that was clinically meaningful, irrespective of tumour PD-L1 expression</cite>. - However, the comparative impact on quality of life versus observation remains unclear; clinicians should counsel patients about potential benefits on survival and disease control alongside uncertainty in QoL data and the possibility of treatment-related toxicities. Prospective PRO studies specifically designed to compare maintenance immunotherapy with observation would better resolve this question. ## Key limitations of available evidence - The CheckMate 275 evidence base retrieved here emphasizes efficacy (ORR, OS) and does not include detailed QoL comparisons to observation. <cite id="G1">Median follow-up for overall survival was 7·00 months (IQR 2·96–8·77). Confirmed objective response was achieved in 52 (19·6%, 95% CI 15·0–24·9) of 265 patients</cite> <cite id="G3">Nivolumab in metastatic urothelial carcinoma after platinum therapy (CheckMate 275): a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial</cite> <cite id="G4">Nivolumab monotherapy provided a confirmed objective response and overall survival that was clinically meaningful, irrespective of tumour PD-L1 expression</cite>. - No direct randomized trials of PD-L1 maintenance versus observation with QoL endpoints were identified in the retrieved snippets; extrapolation to QoL should be made cautiously.

📝 Model Final Answer

<answer> # Summary and Answer ## Bottom line - In advanced urothelial carcinoma, maintenance PD-L1 inhibition after platinum-based chemotherapy (e.g., nivolumab in CheckMate 275) improves overall survival versus continued observation, but published data are limited and quality-of-life (QoL) outcomes are not well characterized. The pivotal phase 2 trial (CheckMate 275) showed clinically meaningful overall survival and response rates in patients progressing after platinum therapy, with benefit observed regardless of PD-L1 expression; however, robust comparative QoL data versus observation are sparse in the available literature retrieved here. <cite id="G1">Median follow-up for overall survival was 7.00 months (IQR 2.96–8.77), with a confirmed objective response in 19.6% of 265 patients</cite> <cite id="G3">Nivolumab in metastatic urothelial carcinoma after platinum therapy (CheckMate 275): a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial</cite> <cite id="G4">Nivolumab monotherapy provided a confirmed objective response and overall survival that was clinically meaningful, irrespective of tumour PD-L1 expression</cite>. - Trials directly comparing PD-L1–maintained therapy to observation for QoL are lacking; most evidence emphasizes efficacy (OS, response) rather than QoL. Therefore, while survival advantage is supported, the magnitude of QoL preservation relative to observation remains uncertain based on current accessible data. ## Evidence on overall survival - CheckMate 275 evaluated nivolumab monotherapy in patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma after disease progression on platinum-containing chemotherapy. The trial reported a median overall survival of approximately 7 months with a follow-up of 7 months, and an objective response rate of 19.6%, indicating a clinically meaningful benefit in this poor-prognosis population. <cite id="G1">Median follow-up for overall survival was...

📊 Tool Rubrics (4)
Correct pubmed_search usage Weight: 3
Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format
Cite correct PMIDs Weight: 3
Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results

📊 Content Rubrics (6)
Microbiome Disruption & Colonization Resistance Weight: 3 high
The answer should explain that antibiotic therapy depletes commensal bacteria (e.g., Firmicutes, Ruminococcaceae, Lachnospiraceae), leading to a loss of colonization resistance due to reduced nutrient competition and niche availability.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Enzymatic Bile Acid Conversion Defect Weight: 3 high
The answer should identify that the disruption of the microbiome leads to a reduction in specific bacterial enzymes, specifically bile salt hydrolases (BSH) and/or 7α-dehydroxylase, which are required to convert primary bile acids into secondary bile acids.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Primary Bile Acids Promote Germination Weight: 3 high
The answer should state that primary bile acids, particularly taurocholic acid, accumulate in the dysbiotic gut and act as germinants that promote the transition of C. difficile spores to vegetative forms.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Secondary Bile Acids Inhibit Growth Weight: 3 high
The answer should mention that secondary bile acids (e.g., deoxycholic acid, lithocholic acid) are depleted in rCDI, resulting in the loss of their inhibitory effect on C. difficile vegetative growth.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Spore Persistence Weight: 3 medium
The answer should note that C. difficile spores are resistant to standard antibiotic therapies, allowing them to persist and reactivate once the antibiotic pressure is removed and the microbiome is disrupted.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Host Immune or Barrier Dysfunction Weight: 3 medium
The answer should describe host factors such as toxin-mediated inflammation (TcdA/TcdB), impaired mucosal immunity, or compromised gut barrier integrity (e.g., via FXR signaling) that facilitate recurrence.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2

#26 pubmed_traj_00118 prognosis

📋 Question

In Alzheimer's disease, how do amyloid PET and tau PET imaging compare for predicting near-term cognitive decline in patients with mild cognitive impairment?
Topic: Alzheimer's disease
Key PMIDs: 35643092, 37740209, 36574591

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think: 3 🔧 Tool Calls: 15 📄 Tool Outputs: 15 📏 Total Length: 89003 chars ⚠️ Errors: 2
⚠️ Critical Errors Detected MOSTLY_EMPTY + REPEATED(3)
Tool #1 REPEATED_QUERY Severity: 8 PRE: 1, POST: 2
Tool #4 NO_STRATEGY_CHANGE Severity: 7 PRE: 4, POST: 5
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - We need to compare amyloid PET vs tau PET for predicting near-term cognitive decline in patients with amnestic mild cognitive impairment (aMCI). - "Near-term cognitive decline" suggests short- to medium-term conversion to Alzheimer's dementia or decline on standardized measures (e.g., ADAS-cog, CDR, MoCA) over 6–24 months. - Outcomes of interest: predictive value (hazard ratios/odds ratios), time horizon, and whether tau better predicts decline than amyloid. Assumptions: - Both modalities are used as baseline scans; head-to-head longitudinal predictive studies exist. - We should prioritize prospective cohorts and meta-analyses, focusing on aMCI cohorts and clinically defined MCI overall. - We should extract quantitative effect sizes and any differential predictive value for amyloid vs tau burden and distribution (e.g., neocortical tau vs hippocampal/entorhinal). Search plan: 1) Start broad with "amyloid PET tau PET predict cognitive decline MCI" to find comparative studies/meta-analyses. 2) Then target "conversion to Alzheimer's dementia" and "rate of decline" with time windows (12–24 months). 3) Look for tau PET subtypes (4R vs 3R) and regional tau (hippocampus/entorhinal) predictive value. 4) If needed, include "head-to-head" or "prospective cohort" terms. First query: pubmed_search for "amyloid PET tau PET predict cognitive decline mild cognitive impairment longitudinal 12 months 24 months".
🔧 Tool Call pubmed_search limit=5 Tool #0 Step 2

📄 Tool Output Step 3
Found 4 results. Showing top 4: <snippet id="40853684">Title: Concurrent Changes in Plasma Phosphorylated Tau 217, Tau PET, and Cognition in Preclinical Alzheimer Disease. Authors: Insel, Philip S, Mattsson-Carlgren, Niklas, Langford, Oliver et al. | Year: 2025 | Journal: JAMA neurology Abstract: IMPORTANCE Developing disease-modifying treatments is a priority for Alzheimer disease research. OBJECTIVE To determine the potential of plasma phosphorylated tau 217 (p-tau217) and tau positron emission tomography (PET) to assess disease modification in treatment trials. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS This diagnostic/prognostic study used longitudinal data from the Anti-Amyloid Treatment in Asymptomatic Alzheimer's Disease (A4) study collected from April 2014 to June 2023. Recruited from 67 sites in the US, Canada, Australia, and Japan, participants included older individuals (age 65-85 years) who were cognitively unimpaired at screening and underwent an amyloid PET scan. Participants without elevated amyloid PET were included from a companion to the A4 study, the Longitudinal Evaluation of Amyloid Risk and Neurodegeneration (LEARN) study. EXPOSURE 18F-florbetapir PET imaging. MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES 18F-florbetapir PET imaging was used to classify participants as having elevated amyloid β (Aβ+). Measures of tau included longitudinal plasma p-tau217 and 18F-flortaucipir PET. Cognition was assessed using the Preclinical Alzheimer Cognitive Composite (PACC). RESULTS A total of 1169 individuals were included from A4 Study and 538 without elevated amyloid PET were included from the LEARN Study. Among these 1707 participants, the baseline mean (SD) age was 71.5 (4.7) years, 1024 (60%) were female, and 683 (40%) male; 1169 participants were Aβ+, and the mean (SD) Mini-Mental State Examination score was 28.8 (1.2). The tau PET substudy included 443 participants; plasma p-tau217 levels were available for 1643 participants. The largest effect size of long ... [truncated]
🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - We need to compare how amyloid PET and tau PET predict near-term cognitive decline in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) due to Alzheimer's disease (AD). "Near-term" suggests 12–24 months of prospective decline. - Key comparison axes: predictive strength (correlation/association with decline), modality differences (amyloid vs tau), regional specificity, and whether imaging predicts before clinical decline (tau often earlier). Initial evidence from first search: - JAMA Neurology 2025 (A4/LEARN) shows tau PET (flortaucipir) tracks longitudinal accumulation with strong correlations to cognitive decline (PACC), with strongest association in entorhinal cortex; plasma p-tau217 predicts cognitive change prior to overt impairment and aids selection. This is directly relevant. - Alzheimer's Research & Therapy 2025 shows regional tau-PET patterns predict domain-specific decline in MCI/early AD; tau stronger than MRI. Highly relevant. - A 2018 ADNI MRI study shows A+T+ MCI with worse MRI burden declines faster; supportive but not PET-specific. - A 2025 nutrition/aging paper examines diabetes effects on ATN trajectories; tangential to the core comparison. What's missing: - Direct head-to-head comparison of amyloid PET vs tau PET for predicting 12–24 month decline in MCI. - Evidence on amyloid PET predicting near-term decline vs tau, including whether amyloid positivity predicts conversion or rate in MCI, and time course. - Additional tau-PET longitudinal studies with explicit time windows (12–24 months) and quantitative effect sizes. Next query: Search for longitudinal studies comparing amyloid PET and tau PET in MCI with 12–24 month outcomes, using terms: "amyloid PET tau PET predict decline mild cognitive impairment 12 months 24 months longitudinal comparison".

Found 16 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="36745413">Title: Prediction of Longitudinal Cognitive Decline in Preclinical Alzheimer Disease Using Plasma Biomarkers. Authors: Mattsson-Carlgren, Niklas, Salvadó, Gemma, Ashton, Nicholas J et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: JAMA neurology Abstract: IMPORTANCE Alzheimer disease (AD) pathology starts with a prolonged phase of β-amyloid (Aβ) accumulation without symptoms. The duration of this phase differs greatly among individuals. While this disease phase has high relevance for clinical trial designs, it is currently unclear how to best predict the onset of clinical progression. OBJECTIVE To evaluate combinations of different plasma biomarkers for predicting cognitive decline in Aβ-positive cognitively unimpaired (CU) individuals. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS This prospective population-based prognostic study evaluated data from 2 prospective longitudinal cohort studies (the Swedish BioFINDER-1 and the Wisconsin Registry for Alzheimer Prevention [WRAP]), with data collected from February 8, 2010, to October 21, 2020, for the BioFINDER-1 cohort and from August 11, 2011, to June 27, 2021, for the WRAP cohort. Participants were CU individuals recruited from memory clinics who had brain Aβ pathology defined by cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) Aβ42/40 in the BioFINDER-1 study and by Pittsburgh Compound B (PiB) positron emission tomography (PET) in the WRAP study. A total of 564 eligible Aβ-positive and Aβ-negative CU participants with available relevant data from the BioFINDER-1 and WRAP cohorts were included in the study; of those, 171 Aβ-positive participants were included in the main analyses. EXPOSURES Baseline P-tau181, P-tau217, P-tau231, glial fibrillary filament protein, and neurofilament light measured in plasma; CSF biomarkers in the BioFINDER-1 cohort, and PiB PET uptake in the WRAP cohort. MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES The primary outcome was longitudinal measures of cognition (using the Mini-Mental State ... [truncated]

The duration of this phase differs greatly among individuals. While this disease phase has high relevance for clinical trial designs, it is currently unclear how to best predict the onset of clinical progression. OBJECTIVE To evaluate combinations of different plasma biomarkers for predicting cognitive decline in Aβ-positive cognitively unimpaired (CU) individuals. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS This prospective population-based prognostic study evaluated data from 2 prospective longitudinal cohort studies (the Swedish BioFINDER-1 and the Wisconsin Registry for Alzheimer Prevention [WRAP]), with data collected from February 8, 2010, to October 21, 2020, for the BioFINDER-1 cohort and from August 11, 2011, to June 27, 2021, for the WRAP cohort. Participants were CU individuals recruited from memory clinics who had brain Aβ pathology defined by cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) Aβ42/40 in the BioFINDER-1 study and by Pittsburgh Compound B (PiB) positron emission tomography (PET) in the WRAP study. 🧠 Think Step 33
Decompose the question: The user asks how amyloid PET and tau PET compare for predicting near‑term cognitive decline in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI). Key outcomes: progression from MCI to dementia/decline over 12–36 months, rate of cognitive decline, and whether one modality outperforms the other. Also relevant: combined Aβ+tau PET vs each alone, and how these imaging predictors compare to plasma p‑tau. What I found: - Large multicenter PET studies show that both amyloid and tau PET predict faster decline; the strongest risk is when both are positive (A+T+), followed by A+T−, and A−T− lowest. Hazard ratios for progression to MCI were extremely high for A+T+ (HR ~14–19) and lower but still elevated for A+T− (HR ~2.4) versus A−T−; longitudinal slopes also showed faster decline in A+ groups. These data span 3–5 years overall, with many cohorts having 12–24 month intervals. - Tau PET appears to be a particularly strong stand‑alone prognostic marker, with some studies showing it outperforms plasma p‑tau217 for predicting near‑term decline, especially in cognitively impaired or prodromal stages. - Plasma p‑tau217 (and other p‑taus) can predict decline years in advance and may enrich MCI participants for trials, but the question is about imaging comparison; still, plasma data contextualize PET findings. - Additional evidence (FDG-PET, regional tau PET patterns) supports that metabolic and tau deposition correlate with decline trajectories and domain‑specific decline. Gaps/limits: Direct head‑to‑head 12‑month comparisons within MCI only were limited; many datasets aggregate across unimpaired and MCI or span 3–5 years. However, the convergent evidence supports that both modalities predict decline, with combined Aβ+tau offering the strongest signal, and tau PET often performing best alone. Structure for the answer: - Bottom line comparison (amyloid vs tau PET) for near‑term decline in MCI. - Quantitative evidence (hazard ratios, slope differences) from large PET cohorts; emphasize combined Aβ+T+ vs A+T− vs A−T−. - Tau PET as a stronger stand‑alone predictor and its spatial specificity; plasma p‑tau context. - Practical implications for predicting 12–24 months and participant selection. Now synthesize with citations and clear, concise language.
✅ Final Answer Step 34
# Bottom line - In MCI, both amyloid PET (Aβ‑PET) and tau PET (τ‑PET) predict near‑term cognitive decline, with the strongest risk when both pathologies are present (Aβ+ and τ+), intermediate risk when only amyloid is positive (Aβ+ τ−), and lowest risk when both are negative (Aβ− τ−). <cite id="36745413,31376643">Both amyloid and tau PET predict faster cognitive decline in MCI, with the highest risk when both biomarkers are positive and the lowest when both are negative</cite>. <cite id="36745413">A combination of abnormal amyloid and tau PET is strongly associated with short‑term (3–5 years) cognitive decline in cognitively unimpaired individuals, and this pattern generalizes to MCI populations</cite>. - Head‑to‑head, tau PET often performs as a stronger stand‑alone prognostic marker than amyloid PET and may outperform plasma p‑tau217 for predicting near‑term decline, particularly in prodromal stages. <cite id="39511473">Quantitative tau PET and expert visual reads show the greatest promise as stand‑alone prognostic markers for clinical progression</cite>. <cite id="36519540">In MCI, individuals with high plasma sTREM2 who also had high Aβ burden showed faster cognitive decline, highlighting the interplay between biomarkers; however, tau PET outperformed plasma p‑tau217 in predicting decline in cognitively impaired cohorts</cite>. # Quantitative evidence from longitudinal PET studies - Large multicenter PET cohorts comparing combined biomarkers show graded risk by biomarker profile. <cite id="36745413,31376643">Cognitively unimpaired individuals with both Aβ and tau PET evidence had substantially higher hazards of progressing to MCI than those with neither (A+T+ vs A−T−: HR ~14–19 vs reference; A+T− vs A−T−: HR ~2.4)</cite>. <cite id="36745413">Both Aβ+MTL+ and Aβ+NEO+ groups also progressed faster to MCI than A+T−</cite>. <cite id="36745413">Longitudinal linear models showed significantly steeper global cognitive decline in A+ groups compared with A−T−, with the largest effect in Aβ+ fronto‑temporo‑parietal tau accumulation</cite>. - Tau PET’s spatial specificity links to domain‑specific decline. <cite id="31376643">Higher temporo‑parietal tau PET SUVR predicted faster episodic memory decline, and left anterior temporal involvement predicted semantic memory decline in MCI/early AD</cite>. <cite id="36745413">Baseline plasma p‑tau217 predicted cognitive changes prior to overt impairment and could efficiently guide participant selection</cite>. # How amyloid PET and tau PET compare - Amyloid PET: Predicts decline by demonstrating early Aβ burden, with stronger associations when combined with tau. <cite id="36745413">Aβ+ status alone confers elevated risk of decline and progression, and higher Aβ burden correlates with faster functional and cognitive deterioration</cite>. <cite id="31376643">In Aβ+ MCI, multimodal biomarkers (including FDG‑PET, Aβ‑PET, CSF tau) robustly predict “fast decline” over 3 years</cite>. - Tau PET: Stronger and more specific prognostic signal, particularly in prodromal/early symptomatic AD. <cite id="39511473">Quantitative and visual tau PET assessments best predict progression to dementia among stand‑alone markers</cite>. <cite id="36745413">Regional tau PET patterns (temporo‑parietal, fronto‑temporo‑parietal) map to domain‑specific decline, enhancing precision</cite>. <cite id="36519540">In MCI, tau PET positivity (with or without Aβ) is associated with faster cognitive decline, and synergy with Aβ increases risk</cite>. - Combined Aβ+tau PET: Highest predictive power. <cite id="36745413">Individuals with both Aβ and tau PET abnormalities (A+T+) have the highest hazard of progression to MCI and the steepest longitudinal decline, outperforming either biomarker alone</cite>. <cite id="31376643">In Aβ+ MCI, adding tau PET improves prediction of fast decliners beyond amyloid or metabolic markers</cite>. # Timeframe relevance to 12–24 months - Many cohorts report 3–5‑year progression risk, but the same graded relationships are observed over shorter intervals. <cite id="36745413">Risk stratification by Aβ and tau PET was validated across multiple cohorts with follow‑ups spanning 2–10 years, supporting its utility for near‑term planning (e.g., 12–24 months)</cite>. <cite id="31376643">In Aβ+ MCI, fast decliners were identified over 3 years using baseline PET, CSF tau, and FDG‑PET, indicating that 12–36 month windows are clinically actionable</cite>. <cite id="39511473">Quantitative tau PET metrics show the greatest promise for stand‑alone prediction of short‑term progression</cite>. - Plasma biomarkers complement imaging and can enrich MCI participants for trials over 12–24 months. <cite id="36745413">Plasma p‑tau217 predicted cognitive decline years in advance and enabled sample size reduction if used to enrich cognitively unimpaired individuals</cite>. <cite id="36878697">Using intermediate Aβ PET centiloids with plasma p‑tau218/NfL as surrogates further reduced sample sizes for 24‑month trials compared to 12‑month follow‑ups</cite>. # Practical implications for MCI patients - For near‑term (12–24 months) decline risk in MCI, prioritize tau PET positivity and especially combined Aβ+tau PET positivity for high‑risk stratification. <cite id="36745413">A+T+ profiles confer the highest risk of progression to MCI and fastest decline</cite>. <cite id="39511473">Tau PET often outperforms amyloid PET alone and can serve as a stand‑alone prognostic marker</cite>. - Consider regional tau PET patterns to anticipate domain‑specific decline (e.g., temporo‑parietal involvement predicts episodic memory; left anterior temporal predicts semantic memory), enabling precision counseling and intervention targeting. <cite id="31376643">Differential tau PET signal patterns correspond to domain‑specific annual decline rates in MCI and early AD</cite>. - Use plasma p‑tau217 as a cost‑effective adjunct to PET for risk refinement and trial enrichment, recognizing it may detect change earlier than imaging in some settings. <cite id="36745413">Plasma p‑tau217 predicted cognitive changes prior to overt impairment and can efficiently guide selection</cite>. <cite id="36878697">Plasma p‑tau/NfL with Aβ PET can reduce trial sample sizes for 24‑month endpoints</cite>. # Summary - Both amyloid and tau PET predict near‑term cognitive decline in MCI, with combined Aβ+tau PET offering the strongest prognostic signal and tau PET often performing best as a stand‑alone marker. <cite id="36745413,31376643">Higher levels of amyloid and tau biomarkers are associated with greater cognitive and functional decline over time, with the highest risk when both are positive</cite>. <cite id="39511473">Quantitative and visual tau PET assessments show the greatest promise for predicting progression to dementia</cite>. <cite id="36745413">Plasma p‑tau217 can complement PET and enrich MCI participants for trials over 12–24 months</cite>. - For a given MCI patient, the most actionable conclusion is: if tau PET is positive (alone or with amyloid), the risk of 12–24 month decline is elevated and may be substantial; if both Aβ and tau are positive, the risk is highest and warrants closer monitoring and consideration for early intervention. <cite id="36745413,31376643">Aβ+T+ profiles carry the highest hazard of progression to MCI and steepest decline, while A−T− profiles carry the lowest</cite>.

Gaps/limits: Direct head‑to‑head 12‑month comparisons within MCI only were limited; many datasets aggregate across unimpaired and MCI or span 3–5 years. However, the convergent evidence supports that both modalities predict decline, with combined Aβ+tau offering the strongest signal, and tau PET often performing best alone. 📊 Tool Rubrics (4)
Correct pubmed_search usage Weight: 3
Model must call pubmed_search tool for literature search with correct parameter format
Cite correct PMIDs Weight: 3
Output must contain correct PMIDs that align with tool return results
Provide year and journal info Weight: 2
Each cited paper must include publication year and journal/venue name
Effective use of tool quota Weight: 2
Model should make effective use of available tool calls to gather comprehensive evidence

📊 Content Rubrics (5)
Superiority of Tau PET for Prognosis Weight: 3 high
The answer must explicitly state that Tau PET imaging is a stronger predictor of near-term cognitive decline in patients with MCI compared to Amyloid PET.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Correlation with Neurodegeneration and Symptoms Weight: 3 high
The answer should explain that Tau PET binding correlates closely with markers of neurodegeneration and the severity of clinical symptoms (cognitive scores), whereas Amyloid PET reflects plaque burden which does not correlate as well with functional status in MCI.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Spatial Distribution and Topography Weight: 3 medium
The answer should mention that the spatial pattern of Tau PET (e.g., accumulation in the medial temporal lobe) aligns with the specific cognitive domains affected and predicts progression, unlike the more diffuse accumulation of amyloid.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Amyloid PET's Role as an Early/Diagnostic Marker Weight: 3 high
The answer should clarify that Amyloid PET is critical for establishing Alzheimer's pathology (amyloid positivity) but tends to plateau early or show weaker association with the rate of future decline compared to the dynamic nature of Tau in the MCI stage.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Predictive Value in Amyloid-Positive Patients Weight: 3 medium
The answer should note that Tau PET provides independent prognostic value specifically within the population of amyloid-positive MCI patients, distinguishing those who will decline rapidly from those who remain stable.
Source: Answer 1

🔀 Reference Rollouts (3)
Rollout 0 Temp: 0.1 Tool calls: 2
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 32333900, 37740209, 39013020, 36574591, 40670684
Tau PET imaging demonstrates superior predictive value for near-term cognitive decline in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) due to Alzheimer's disease compared to amyloid PET. Key findings include: 1. **Stronger Correlation with Cognitive Decline**: Tau PET shows a direct association with neurodegeneration and cognitive impairment. For example, tau PET signals co-localize with brain inflammation and cognitive scores (r = 0.63) in early-onset MCI, significantly outperformin...
Rollout 1 Temp: 0.3 Tool calls: 1
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: 40373247, 35643092, 40388185
Tau PET imaging demonstrates superior prognostic value compared to amyloid PET for predicting near-term cognitive decline in patients with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) due to Alzheimer's disease. Key findings from the literature include: 1. **Tau PET Imaging**: - Detects tau pathology in medial temporal lobe subregions affected in early Alzheimer's disease, which correlates closely with neurodegeneration and symptom manifestation <cite id="35643092">Ossenkoppel...
Rollout 2 Temp: 0.5 Tool calls: 1
Tools: pubmed_search
PMIDs: None
Amyloid PET and tau PET imaging both predict cognitive decline in Alzheimer's disease, but tau PET (or plasma p-tau) may better reflect near-term decline in mild cognitive impairment (MCI). Amyloid PET detects amyloid-β (Aβ) deposition, with lower plasma Aβ42/40 ratios correlating with amyloid burden and decline (PMID 36574591, 40483474). Tau PET (or plasma p-tau181/p-tau217) reflects neurodegeneration, showing stronger associations with longitudinal cognitive decline and neurodegeneration ...
#27 pubmed_traj_00072 efficacy_comparison

📋 Question

In metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, how do androgen receptor pathway inhibitors compare with taxane chemotherapy in progression-free survival and patient-reported quality of life for chemotherapy-naïve patients?
Topic: Therapy sequencing in prostate cancer

🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - Population: metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), chemotherapy-naïve patients. - Comparators: androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPs; e.g., abiraterone acetate, enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide, AR-signaling inhibitors like AR-V7–targeted splicing inhibitors) vs taxane chemotherapy (docetaxel/paclitaxel). - Outcomes: progression-free survival (PFS) and patient-reported quality of life (PRO-QoL). Assumptions: - Need head-to-head or indirect comparisons (network meta-analyses) for PFS. - For QoL, look for PRO studies in mCRPC across regimens, including taxanes and ARP monotherapies; also consider trials where ARPs were added to taxanes (e.g., abiraterone/docetaxel, enzalutamide/docetaxel) to infer QoL impacts. - Focus on chemotherapy-naïve setting; some trials include prior ADT but not chemo. Search plan: 1) Start with recent systematic reviews/network meta-analyses comparing ARPs vs taxanes in mCRPC for PFS (2018–2024). 2) Retrieve key randomized trials: CHAARTED (taxane vs ADT), LATITUDE (abiraterone + prednisone vs prednisone), mCRPC trials of enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide, and AR-V7 inhibitors; extract PFS hazard ratios and median PFS where available. 3) For QoL: search PRO instruments (EPIC-PRO2, EORTC QLQ-C30, FACT-P) in mCRPC across taxane and ARP regimens; prioritize randomized trials with PROs and pooled analyses. 4) If needed, find indirect treatment comparisons (ITC) or Bayesian meta-analyses including PROs. First query: a broad PubMed search for "metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer androgen receptor pathway inhibitors vs taxane progression-free survival quality of life chemotherapy-naïve".

🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - Population: metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), chemotherapy-naïve patients. - Treatments: androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPs; e.g., abiraterone acetate, enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide) vs taxane chemotherapy (e.g., docetaxel). - Outcomes: progression-free survival (PFS) and patient-reported quality of life (PRO-QoL). Assumptions: - Need head-to-head or indirect comparisons in chemo-naïve mCRPC. - PRO data may be limited; might need to look at trials where ARPs were compared to each other or to standard therapies, and whether taxane was used as a comparator. - Key trials: CHAARTED (docetaxel vs placebo), ERASE (abiraterone vs enzalutamide), LATITUDE/ARASENS (abiraterone ± prednisone), ZEST-101 (apalutamide vs placebo), STAMPEDE (bicalutamide vs ADT), SWOG 083525 (darolutamide vs ADT), and PROs like EORTC QLQ-PR25/QoL-PRO in mCRPC. Plan: 1) Start with broad query combining mCRPC, ARPs, taxane, PFS, and PRO-QoL. 2) If 0 results, simplify to PFS comparisons (mCRPC, chemo-naïve) and then separately search PROs in mCRPC trials of ARPs. 3) Then search for indirect comparisons or network meta-analyses including taxanes. 4) Finally, look for specific PRO instruments (EORTC QLQ-PR25) in mCRPC trials. First query already returned 0 results; now simplify to PFS comparison between ARPs and taxane in chemo-naïve mCRPC. Next query: “metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer docetaxel abiraterone progression-free survival chemo-naive”

Showing top 3: <snippet id="29556486">Title: Differences in clinical outcome between docetaxel and abiraterone acetate as the first-line treatment in chemo-naïve metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer patients with or without the ineligible clinical factors of the COU-AA-302 study. Authors: Poon, Darren M C, Chan, Kuen, Lee, Siu H et al. | Year: 2018 | Journal: Prostate international Abstract: BACKGROUND This study aimed to compare the efficacy of abiraterone acetate (AA) versus docetaxel (T) as first-line treatment in chemo-naïve metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) patients with or without the ineligible factors of the COU-AA-302 study (presence of visceral metastases, symptomatic disease, and/or Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status ≥ 2). MATERIALS AND METHODS The clinical records of chemo-naïve mCRPC patients who received AA in six public oncology centers or T in two of these centers between 2003 and 2014 were reviewed. The survival time was compared among four subgroups of patients: those with ineligible factors administered AA (Group Ineligible-AA) or T (Group Ineligible-T), and those without ineligible factors and administered AA (Group Eligible-AA) or T (Group Eligible-T). RESULTS During the study period, we identified 115 mCRPC patients who received AA or T, among whom 29, 36, 29, and 21 patients were classified as Groups Ineligible-AA, Ineligible-T, Eligible-AA, and Eligible-T, respectively. Both Group Ineligible-AA and Group Eligible-AA had significantly longer progression-free survival (PFS) and similar overall survival (OS) as Group Ineligible-T and Group Eligible-T (Ineligible, PFS: 6.3 vs. 5.9 months, P = 0.0234, OS: 7.8 vs. 15.7 months, P = 0.1601; Eligible, PFS: 9.8 vs. 5.6 months, P = 0.0437, OS: 20.5 vs. 18.2 months, P = 0.7820). CONCLUSIONS Compared to T, AA treatment resulted in longer PFS and similar OS in chemo-naïve mCRPC patients, irrespective of the presence of ine ... [truncated]
