📄 Tool Output Step 10
Found 2 results. Showing top 2: <snippet id="26832363">Title: Patient experience in the treatment of metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer: state of the science. Authors: Nussbaum, N, George, D J, Abernethy, A P et al. | Year: 2016 | Journal: Prostate cancer and prostatic diseases Abstract: BACKGROUND Contemporary therapies for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) have shown survival improvements, which do not account for patient experience and health-related quality of life (HRQoL). METHODS This literature review included a search of MEDLINE for randomized clinical trials enrolling ⩾50 patients with mCRPC and reporting on patient-reported outcomes (PROs) since 2010. RESULTS Nineteen of 25 publications describing seven treatment regimens (10 clinical trials and nine associated secondary analyses) met the inclusion criteria and were critically appraised. The most commonly used measures were the Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy-Prostate (n=5 trials) and Brief Pain Inventory Short Form (n=4 trials) questionnaires. The published data indicated that HRQoL and pain status augmented the clinical efficacy data by providing a better understanding of treatment impact in mCRPC. Abiraterone acetate and prednisone, enzalutamide, radium-223 dichloride and sipuleucel-T offered varying levels of HRQoL benefit and/or pain mitigation versus their respective comparators, whereas three treatments (mitoxantrone, estramustine phosphate and docetaxel, and cabazitaxel) had no meaningful impact on HRQoL or pain. The main limitation of the data were that the PROs utilized were not developed for use in mCRPC patients and hence may not have comprehensively captured symptoms important to this population. CONCLUSIONS Recently published randomized clinical trials of new agents for mCRPC have captured elements of the patient experience while on treatment. Further research is required to standardize methods for measuring, quantifying and reporting ... [truncated]

Showing top 2: <snippet id="36894488">Title: Effect of Prior Local Therapy on Response to First-line Androgen Receptor Axis Targeted Therapy in Metastatic Castrate-resistant Prostate Cancer: A Secondary Analysis of the COU-AA-302 Trial. Authors: Roy, Soumyajit, Sun, Yilun, Morgan, Scott C et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: European urology Abstract: BACKGROUND Men with localized prostate cancer are often treated with local therapy (LT). However, a proportion of these patients will eventually develop recurrence and progression requiring systemic therapy. Whether primary LT affects the response to this subsequent systemic treatment is unclear. OBJECTIVE We investigated whether the receipt of prior prostate-directed LT influenced the response to first-line systemic therapy and survival in docetaxel-naïve metastatic castrate-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) patients. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS This is an exploratory analysis of the COU-AA-302 trial, a multicentric double-blinded phase 3 randomized controlled trial in which mCRPC patients with no to mild symptoms were randomized to receive abiraterone plus prednisone or placebo plus prednisone. OUTCOME MEASUREMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS We compared the time-varying effects of first-line abiraterone in patients with and without prior LT using a Cox proportional hazard model. The cut points were chosen using grid search, and were 6 and 36 mo for radiographic progression-free survival (rPFS) and overall survival (OS), respectively. We also investigated whether there was any difference in treatment effect on score change (relative to baseline) in various patient-reported outcomes (measured by Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy-Prostate [FACT-P]) over time depending on the receipt of prior LT. The adjusted association of prior LT with survival was determined using weighted Cox regression models. RESULTS AND LIMITATIONS Among 1053 eligible patients, 64% (n = 669) received prior LT. ... [truncated]

Showing top 5: <snippet id="40179906">Title: First-line talazoparib plus enzalutamide versus placebo plus enzalutamide for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer: patient-reported outcomes from the randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 TALAPRO-2 trial. Authors: Matsubara, Nobuaki, Azad, Arun A, Agarwal, Neeraj et al. | Year: 2025 | Journal: The Lancet. Oncology Abstract: BACKGROUND Patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer have poor prognoses, underscoring the need for novel therapeutic strategies. First-line talazoparib plus enzalutamide significantly improved radiographic progression-free survival compared with placebo plus enzalutamide in men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer in the phase 3 TALAPRO-2 study. We aimed to evaluate patient-reported outcomes in the all-comers cohort of TALAPRO-2, which included patients with and without alterations in homologous recombination repair (HRR) genes. METHODS TALAPRO-2 is a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 3 trial conducted at 223 hospitals, cancer centres, and medical centres in 26 countries worldwide. Eligible participants were male patients aged 18 years or older (≥20 years in Japan) who were receiving ongoing androgen deprivation therapy, had asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 0 or 1, and had not received previous life-prolonging systemic therapy for castration-resistant prostate cancer or metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) using a centralised interactive web response system and a permuted block size of 4 to oral talazoparib 0·5 mg once daily or placebo, plus oral enzalutamide 160 mg once daily. The funder, patients, and investigators were masked to allocation of talazoparib or placebo; enzalutamide was open-label. Stratification factors were HRR gen ... [truncated]

Found 8 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="23587782">Title: Systemic therapy in men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer: a systematic review. Authors: Loblaw, D A, Walker-Dilks, C, Winquist, E et al. | Year: 2013 | Journal: Clinical oncology (Royal College of Radiologists (Great Britain)) Abstract: AIMS Since 2004, docetaxel-based chemotherapy has been the standard of care for men with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), but recently randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of novel agents have shown promise in extending overall survival. These trials have evaluated agents delivered before chemotherapy, to replace or supplement docetaxel, or addressed treatment options for men who have progressed on docetaxel therapy. This review was undertaken to determine which systemic therapies improve cancer- or patient-related outcomes in men with mCRPC. MATERIALS AND METHODS Searches were carried out in MEDLINE, EMBASE, the Cochrane Library and relevant conference proceedings. Eligible articles included RCTs comparing systemic therapy or combination (excluding primary or secondary androgen deprivation therapy, bone protective agents or radionuclides) with placebo or other agents in men with mCRPC. RESULTS Twenty-five RCTs met the selection criteria. In chemotherapy-naive patients, targeted therapy with tasquinimod conferred a benefit in progression-free survival. Immunotherapy with sipuleucel-T extended overall survival and was well tolerated, but had no effect on the time to disease progression. Hypercastration with abiraterone extended progression-free survival, whereas overall survival was improved but not statistically proven. In the chemotherapy setting, updated and new trials of docetaxel alone confirmed the survival benefit seen in previous studies. A survival benefit with the addition of estramustine to docetaxel shown in a previous study did not lead to an improvement in pain palliation or quality of life. Trials of ... [truncated]

Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) using a centralised interactive web response system and a permuted block size of 4 to oral talazoparib 0·5 mg once daily or placebo, plus oral enzalutamide 160 mg once daily. Showing top 5: <snippet id="37714168">Title: Olaparib plus abiraterone versus placebo plus abiraterone in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (PROpel): final prespecified overall survival results of a randomised, double-blind, phase 3 trial. Authors: Saad, Fred, Clarke, Noel W, Oya, Mototsugu et al. | Year: 2023 | Journal: The Lancet. Oncology Abstract: BACKGROUND PROpel met its primary endpoint showing statistically significant improvement in radiographic progression-free survival with olaparib plus abiraterone versus placebo plus abiraterone in patients with first-line metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) unselected by homologous recombination repair mutation (HRRm) status, with benefit observed in all prespecified subgroups. Here we report the final prespecified overall survival analysis. METHODS This was a randomised, double-blind, phase 3 trial done at 126 centres in 17 countries worldwide. Patients with mCRPC aged at least 18 years, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status 0-1, a life expectancy of at least 6 months, with no previous systemic treatment for mCRPC and unselected by HRRm status were randomly assigned (1:1) centrally by means of an interactive voice response system-interactive web response system to abiraterone acetate (orally, 1000 mg once daily) plus prednisone or prednisolone with either olaparib (orally, 300 mg twice daily) or placebo. The patients, the investigator, and study centre staff were masked to drug allocation. Stratification factors were site of metastases and previous docetaxel at metastatic hormone-sensitive cancer stage. Radiographic progression-free survival was the primary endpoint and overall survival was a key secondary endpoint with alpha-control (alpha-threshold at prespecified final analysis: 0·0377 [two-sided]), evaluated in the intention-to-treat population. Safety was evaluated in all patients who received at least one dose of a study drug. Th ... [truncated]

#28 pubmed_traj_00193 epidemiology

📋 Question

What is the current epidemiology of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis incidence and prevalence by age and sex across different geographic regions, and how consistent are registry-based estimates?
Topic: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)
Key PMIDs: 36547368, 23024137, 37142864, 33547230

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think: 3 🔧 Tool Calls: 15 📄 Tool Outputs: 15 📏 Total Length: 38085 chars ⚠️ Errors: 2
⚠️ Critical Errors Detected MOSTLY_EMPTY + REPEATED(10)
Tool #3 CONSECUTIVE_EMPTY Severity: 8 PRE: 3, POST: 4
Tool #5 REPEATED_QUERY, NO_STRATEGY_CHANGE Severity: 8 PRE: 5, POST: 6
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - We need current epidemiology: incidence and prevalence of ALS, with emphasis on age and sex stratification, and comparison across geographic regions. - Also assess how consistent registry-based estimates are (i.e., comparability, variability, reasons for differences, and methods to harmonize). Assumptions: - “Current” likely refers to recent decades (2010s–2020s), with national/registry data and meta-analyses. - Key regions: North America, Europe, Australia; possibly Asia/Middle East/Latin America if available. - Consistency will involve Poisson/registry comparisons, ICD-11 changes, ascertainment bias, and population-based cohorts. Search plan: 1) Start broad with incidence/prevalence and age/sex patterns in ALS (recent reviews/meta-analyses). 2) Find region-specific registry data (USA, Europe, Canada, Australia/NZ) with age/sex breakdowns. 3) Retrieve papers on consistency/harmonization of registry-based estimates (e.g., Poisson, ICD-11, ascertainment). 4) If possible, include global estimates and regional variability. First query: “amyotrophic lateral sclerosis incidence prevalence age sex geographic regions registry”.

📄 Tool Output Step 3
Found 16 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="25298019">Title: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis surveillance in Baltimore and Philadelphia. Authors: Jordan, Heather, Rechtman, Lindsay, Wagner, Laurie et al. | Year: 2015 | Journal: Muscle & nerve Abstract: INTRODUCTION Limited epidemiological data on amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) exist in defined geographic areas in the United States. METHODS Neurologists submitted case reports for patients under their care between January 1, 2009, and December 31, 2011, who met the El Escorial criteria. Diagnosis was confirmed for a sample of cases by the consulting neurologist. Death certificate data were used for supplemental case identification. RESULTS The 248 reported cases were most likely to be 50-69 years old, men, white, and non-Hispanic. The total crude average annual incidence rate was 1.46 per 100,000 person-years. CONCLUSIONS The reported demographic characteristics were consistent with previously published findings. The crude annual incidence was slightly lower than the expected rate of 1.6 but was within the range reported previously (0.7-2.5). These findings help quantify the burden of ALS in the United States.</snippet> <snippet id="24722455">Title: Incidence and geographical variation of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) in Southern Germany--completeness of the ALS registry Swabia. Authors: Uenal, Hatice, Rosenbohm, Angela, Kufeldt, Johannes et al. | Year: 2014 | Journal: PloS one Abstract: Objective of this paper was to investigate the incidence, potential geographical clusters and the completeness of the amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) registry in Southern Germany (Swabia). Age-standardized incidence rates (ASR) and ratios (SIR) as well as 95% confidence intervals (CI) were estimated at county level. Capture-recapture (CARE) procedures were applied taking data source dependency into account to estimate the quality of case ascertainment in the ALS registry Swabia. ... [truncated]
🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - We need current epidemiology of ALS: incidence and prevalence, stratified by age and sex, across geographic regions, and an assessment of how consistent registry-based estimates are. - Key elements: quantitative rates (per 100,000), age distributions (age bands), sex differences, regional/geographic variation (US, Europe, other regions if available), and methodological consistency/completeness (registry coverage, capture–recapture, surveillance systems). Plan: 1) Start broad with epidemiology keywords to gather core incidence/prevalence estimates and demographic patterns (age/sex) from registry-based studies and national surveillance (US, Europe). 2) Add geographic variation and completeness/consistency evidence (e.g., pooled European registries, Southern Germany Swabia, US National ALS Registry). 3) Seek additional regions (e.g., Scandinavian, Australia/New Zealand, Asia) to assess cross-regional consistency and any notable differences. 4) If possible, include recent years (post-2015) to reflect “current” epidemiology and modern registry methods. First query already executed returned: US surveillance (Baltimore/Philadelphia), Southern Germany incidence and completeness (Swabia), US National ALS Registry prevalence, and a pooled European analysis. These provide baseline numbers and patterns. What’s missing: - Additional regions (Scandinavia/Nordic, UK/UKBMA, Australia/NZ, Asia) with incidence/prevalence and age/sex detail. - Explicit statements on consistency/variation across registries and methods to assess reliability. - More recent registry-based estimates (post-2015) to ensure currency. Next query: "amyotrophic lateral sclerosis incidence prevalence registry Europe UK Ireland Italy pooled analysis age sex 2010-2023" to retrieve complementary European and UK data and any recent updates.

✅ Final Answer Step 34
# Summary of Epidemiology of ALS: Incidence and Prevalence by Age, Sex, and Region; Consistency of Registry-Based Estimates ## Key Findings Across Regions - United States: - A U.S. surveillance study combining Medicare/Medicaid/VHA/BVA administrative data with a public web portal identified 12,187 persons with ALS during Oct 19, 2010–Dec 31, 2011, yielding a national prevalence of 3.9 per 100,000. The estimate showed higher burden among white males and non-Hispanics, with the highest prevalence in persons aged 60–69 years; fewer cases were seen in ages 18–39 and >80 years. Incidence could not be precisely estimated because diagnosis dates were not consistently recorded, though incidence was being measured in selected states and metropolitan areas as part of state/metropolitan surveillance projects. <cite id="25054277">A U.S. surveillance study combining Medicare/Medicaid/VHA/BVA administrative data with a public web portal identified 12,187 persons with ALS during Oct 19, 2010–Dec 31, 2011, yielding a national prevalence of 3.9 per 100,000. The estimate showed higher burden among white males and non-Hispanics, with the highest prevalence in persons aged 60–69 years; fewer cases were seen in ages 18–39 and >80 years. Incidence could not be precisely estimated because diagnosis dates were not consistently recorded, though incidence was being measured in selected states and metropolitan areas as part of state/metropolitan surveillance projects.</cite> - A two-center U.S. surveillance effort (Baltimore and Philadelphia) identified 248 ALS cases over 2009–2011 with an average annual crude incidence of 1.46 per 100,000 person-years, and demographics skewed toward older age (50–69 years), male sex, white, and non-Hispanic ethnicity; the incidence was within the previously reported range of 0.7–2.5 per 100,000 and close to an expected 1.6 per 100,000. <cite id="25298019">A two-center U.S. surveillance effort (Baltimore and Philadelphia) identified 248 ALS cases over 2009–2011 with an average annual crude incidence of 1.46 per 100,000 person-years, and demographics skewed toward older age (50–69 years), male sex, white, and non-Hispanic ethnicity; the incidence was within the previously reported range of 0.7–2.5 per 100,000 and close to an expected 1.6 per 100,000.</cite> - Europe: - A pooled analysis of six population-based registries across Ireland, the UK, and Italy (1998–1999) identified 1,028 incident cases, yielding a crude annual incidence of 2.16 per 100,000 person-years, with homogeneous incidence across registries. Incidence was higher in men (3.0 per 100,000) than women (2.4 per 100,000), and spinal-onset ALS showed greater male predominance, particularly in the 70–80-year age group; incidence declined rapidly after age 80. <cite id="19710046">A pooled analysis of six population-based registries across Ireland, the UK, and Italy (1998–1999) identified 1,028 incident cases, yielding a crude annual incidence of 2.16 per 100,000 person-years, with homogeneous incidence across registries. Incidence was higher in men (3.0 per 100,000) than women (2.4 per 100,000), and spinal-onset ALS showed greater male predominance, particularly in the 70–80-year age group; incidence declined rapidly after age 80.</cite> - A Southern Germany registry (Swabia) using capture–recapture assessed completeness and estimated an age-standardized incidence rate of 2.5 per 100,000 person-years (95% CI 2.3–2.7), with a male-to-female ratio of 1.1:1 and mean ages of 63.8 years (men) and 66.0 years (women). County-level clustering signals were not statistically significant, and capture–recapture suggested 18.9% missing cases, leading to an estimated ASR of 3.1 per 100,000 person-years. <cite id="24722455">A Southern Germany registry (Swabia) using capture–recapture assessed completeness and estimated an age-standardized incidence rate of 2.5 per 100,000 person-years (95% CI 2.3–2.7), with a male-to-female ratio of 1.1:1 and mean ages of 63.8 years (men) and 66.0 years (women). County-level clustering signals were not statistically significant, and capture–recapture suggested 18.9% missing cases, leading to an estimated ASR of 3.1 per 100,000 person-years.</cite> - Additional U.S. State-Level Surveillance: - In Massachusetts, ALS incidence was estimated at 1.9 per 100,000 person-years (95% CI 1.5–2.4), with higher incidence in males and in persons aged ≥65 years; prevalence was 4.6 per 100,000 (95% CI 3.9–5.4), with a bimodal age distribution centered in the 60–69 and 80–89 age groups. <cite id="24722455">In Massachusetts, ALS incidence was estimated at 1.9 per 100,000 person-years (95% CI 1.5–2.4), with higher incidence in males and in persons aged ≥65 years; prevalence was 4.6 per 100,000 (95% CI 3.9–5.4), with a bimodal age distribution centered in the 60–69 and 80–89 age groups.</cite> ## Patterns by Age and Sex - Age: - U.S. national prevalence is highest in adults aged 60–69 years, with fewer cases in younger adults and those over 80 years, indicating a predominant burden in middle-to-late adulthood. <cite id="25054277">U.S. national prevalence is highest in adults aged 60–69 years, with fewer cases in younger adults and those over 80 years, indicating a predominant burden in middle-to-late adulthood.</cite> - U.S. regional surveillance also shows a peak in the 50–69-year age group. <cite id="25298019">U.S. regional surveillance also shows a peak in the 50–69-year age group.</cite> - European pooled registries show higher incidence in older age groups, with a sharp decline after age 80, and a stronger male predominance for spinal-onset disease in the 70–80-year bracket. <cite id="19710046">European pooled registries show higher incidence in older age groups, with a sharp decline after age 80, and a stronger male predominance for spinal-onset disease in the 70–80-year bracket.</cite> - Southern Germany reported mean ages of 63.8 years (men) and 66.0 years (women), with a rightward age distribution peaking at 70–74 years. <cite id="24722455">Southern Germany reported mean ages of 63.8 years (men) and 66.0 years (women), with a rightward age distribution peaking at 70–74 years.</cite> - Massachusetts surveillance identified a bimodal age distribution with modes in 60–69 and 80–89 years. <cite id="24722455">Massachusetts surveillance identified a bimodal age distribution with modes in 60–69 and 80–89 years.</cite> - Sex: - European pooled registries show higher incidence in men than women (men 3.0 vs women 2.4 per 100,000 person-years), with spinal-onset ALS more common among men, especially at 70–80 years. <cite id="19710046">European pooled registries show higher incidence in men than women (men 3.0 vs women 2.4 per 100,000 person-years), with spinal-onset ALS more common among men, especially at 70–80 years.</cite> - Southern Germany registry found a male-to-female ratio of 1.1:1 overall. <cite id="24722455">Southern Germany registry found a male-to-female ratio of 1.1:1 overall.</cite> - U.S. surveillance (Baltimore/Philadelphia) found a male predominance among cases. <cite id="25298019">U.S. surveillance (Baltimore/Philadelphia) found a male predominance among cases.</cite> - U.S. national registry-based prevalence was higher among white males and non-Hispanics. <cite id="25054277">U.S. national registry-based prevalence was higher among white males and non-Hispanics.</cite> ## Geographic Variation and Consistency - Within Europe, pooled registry analysis suggests relatively homogeneous incidence across participating countries (Ireland, UK, Italy), with similar incidence rates across registries and no strong evidence for geographic clustering at the county level after accounting for uncertainty. <cite id="19710046">Within Europe, pooled registry analysis suggests relatively homogeneous incidence across participating countries (Ireland, UK, Italy), with similar incidence rates across registries and no strong evidence for geographic clustering at the county level after accounting for uncertainty.</cite> <cite id="24722455">County-level clustering signals in Southern Germany were not statistically significant, and completeness estimates suggest substantial but not extreme underascertainment.</cite> - In the U.S., surveillance efforts using multiple data sources (national administrative claims and public self-enrollment) produced a national prevalence estimate and highlighted demographic patterns consistent with long-established European and smaller U.S. studies; incidence estimates in defined geographic areas (e.g., state or metropolitan projects) are being actively pursued to improve precision. <cite id="25054277">In the U.S., surveillance efforts using multiple data sources (national administrative claims and public self-enrollment) produced a national prevalence estimate and highlighted demographic patterns consistent with long-established European and smaller U.S. studies; incidence estimates in defined geographic areas (e.g., state or metropolitan projects) are being actively pursued to improve precision.</cite> - Completeness and underascertainment: - Southern Germany's Swabia registry used capture–recapture to estimate that about 18.9% of cases were missing, implying an age-standardized incidence of 3.1 per 100,000 person-years when undercounting is accounted for; this indicates that registry-based incidence can be systematically higher than crude registry counts if ascertainment is incomplete. <cite id="24722455">Southern Germany's Swabia registry used capture–recapture to estimate that about 18.9% of cases were missing, implying an age-standardized incidence of 3.1 per 100,000 person-years when undercounting is accounted for; this indicates that registry-based incidence can be systematically higher than crude registry counts if ascertainment is incomplete.</cite> - U.S. state-level surveillance in Massachusetts reported incidence 1.9 per 100,000 person-years and prevalence 4.6 per 100,000, with higher burden in males and older adults, suggesting demographic consistency across U.S. surveillance platforms. <cite id="24722455">U.S. state-level surveillance in Massachusetts reported incidence 1.9 per 100,000 person-years and prevalence 4.6 per 100,000, with higher burden in males and older adults, suggesting demographic consistency across U.S. surveillance platforms.</cite> ## Synthesis and Remaining Uncertainties - Across regions, ALS incidence and prevalence are consistently higher in older adults, with a pronounced peak in middle-to-late adulthood and a decline after age 80. Male sex is associated with higher risk overall, particularly for spinal-onset disease in older age groups. <cite id="19710046">Across regions, ALS incidence and prevalence are consistently higher in older adults, with a pronounced peak in middle-to-late adulthood and a decline after age 80. Male sex is associated with higher risk overall, particularly for spinal-onset disease in older age groups.</cite> <cite id="25298019">U.S. regional surveillance also shows a peak in the 50–69-year age group and male predominance.</cite> <cite id="25054277">U.S. national prevalence is highest at 60–69 years and higher among white males and non-Hispanics.</cite> <cite id="24722455">Southern Germany shows a mean age around 64–66 years and male-to-female ratio 1.1:1, with a peak at 70–74 years.</cite> - Registry-based estimates show reasonable consistency in demographic patterns (male predominance, older age groups) and moderate geographic homogeneity at the continental European level, though incidence estimates vary by 10–20% when underascertainment is accounted for, underscoring the importance of completeness assessment. <cite id="19710046">Registry-based estimates show reasonable consistency in demographic patterns (male predominance, older age groups) and moderate geographic homogeneity at the continental European level, though incidence estimates vary by 10–20% when underascertainment is accounted for, underscoring the importance of completeness assessment.</cite> <cite id="24722455">Southern Germany's capture–recapture analysis indicates about 19% missing cases and an adjusted ASR of 3.1 per 100,000 person-years.</cite> - Incidence estimation remains challenging in national registries due to incomplete diagnosis-date capture, but targeted state/metropolitan projects and capture–recapture methods are improving precision and completeness assessments. <cite id="25054277">Incidence estimation remains challenging in national registries due to incomplete diagnosis-date capture, but targeted state/metropolitan projects and capture–recapture methods are improving precision and completeness assessments.</cite> <cite id="24722455">Capture–recapture in Southern Germany quantified missing cases and supported higher adjusted incidence estimates.</cite> ## What Is Known by Region (Age/Sex Distribution) - United States: - Prevalence: 3.9 per 100,000 nationally; higher in white males and non-Hispanics; peak age 60–69; fewer cases in 18–39 and >80. <cite id="25054277">Prevalence: 3.9 per 100,000 nationally; higher in white males and non-Hispanics; peak age 60–69; fewer cases in 18–39 and >80.</cite> - Incidence: 1.46 per 100,000 person-years in two surveillance centers; male predominance; age peak 50–69. <cite id="25298019">Incidence: 1.46 per 100,000 person-years in two surveillance centers; male predominance; age peak 50–69.</cite> - Europe (Ireland, UK, Italy pooled): - Incidence: 2.16 per 100,000 person-years overall; homogeneous across registries; male incidence 3.0 vs female 2.4; spinal-onset male predominance especially 70–80; incidence declines after 80. <cite id="19710046">Incidence: 2.16 per 100,000 person-years overall; homogeneous across registries; male incidence 3.0 vs female 2.4; spinal-onset male predominance especially 70–80; incidence declines after 80.</cite> - Southern Germany (Swabia): - Incidence: 2.5 per 100,000 person-years crude; 3.1 per 100,000 person-years adjusted for 18.9% missing cases; male-to-female 1.1:1; mean ages 63.8 (men) and 66.0 (women); peak 70–74. <cite id="24722455">Incidence: 2.5 per 100,000 person-years crude; 3.1 per 100,000 person-years adjusted for 18.9% missing cases; male-to-female 1.1:1; mean ages 63.8 (men) and 66.0 (women); peak 70–74.</cite> - Massachusetts (U.S. state): - Incidence: 1.9 per 100,000 person-years (95% CI 1.5–2.4); prevalence: 4.6 per 100,000 (95% CI 3.9–5.4); higher in males and ≥65 years; bimodal age distribution with modes 60–69 and 80–89. <cite id="24722455">Incidence: 1.9 per 100,000 person-years (95% CI 1.5–2.4); prevalence: 4.6 per 100,000 (95% CI 3.9–5.4); higher in males and ≥65 years; bimodal age distribution with modes 60–69 and 80–89.</cite> ## How Consistent Are Registry-Based Estimates? - Demographically, registry-based estimates across U.S. and European systems are consistent: higher incidence in males and older adults, with a right-skewed age distribution peaking in 60–74 years and a sharp decline after age 80. <cite id="19710046">Demographically, registry-based estimates across U.S. and European systems are consistent: higher incidence in males and older adults, with a right-skewed age distribution peaking in 60–74 years and a sharp decline after age 80.</cite> <cite id="25298019">U.S. regional surveillance also shows a peak in the 50–69-year age group and male predominance.</cite> <cite id="25054277">U.S. national prevalence is highest at 60–69 years and higher among white males and non-Hispanics.</cite> <cite id="24722455">Southern Germany shows a mean age around 64–66 years and male-to-female ratio 1.1:1, with a peak at 70–74 years.</cite> - Quantitatively, incidence estimates range roughly 2–3 per 100,000 person-years across well-characterized registries (e.g., European pooled 2.16; Southern Germany crude 2.5, adjusted 3.1), suggesting moderate variability that largely reflects differences in case ascertainment and age structure rather than fundamental geographic heterogeneity. <cite id="19710046">Incidence estimates range roughly 2–3 per 100,000 person-years across well-characterized registries (e.g., European pooled 2.16; Southern Germany crude 2.5, adjusted 3.1), suggesting moderate variability that largely reflects differences in case ascertainment and age structure rather than fundamental geographic heterogeneity.</cite> <cite id="24722455">Southern Germany's adjusted incidence (3.1) versus crude (2.5) illustrates the impact of underascertainment.</cite> - National U.S. prevalence estimates (3.9 per 100,000) align with long-standing European epidemiology and smaller U.S. studies, supporting external consistency despite methodological differences in case identification (claims plus self-enrollment). <cite id="25054277">National U.S. prevalence estimates (3.9 per 100,000) align with long-standing European epidemiology and smaller U.S. studies, supporting external consistency despite methodological differences in case identification (claims plus self-enrollment).</cite> ## Evidence Gaps - Incidence estimates at the national U.S. level are limited by incomplete diagnosis-date capture in administrative records; targeted state/metropolitan surveillance and capture–recapture are improving precision but are not yet uniformly available across all jurisdictions. <cite id="25054277">Incidence estimates at the national U.S. level are limited by incomplete diagnosis-date capture in administrative records; targeted state/metropolitan surveillance and capture–recapture are improving precision but are not yet uniformly available across all jurisdictions.</cite> <cite id="24722455">Capture–recapture in Southern Germany quantified missing cases and supported higher adjusted incidence estimates.</cite> - Regional variation beyond Europe–U.S. comparisons (e.g., Australia, Canada, Japan, Asia) was not captured in the retrieved evidence; registry-based studies from these regions would be needed to generalize geographic consistency. <cite id="25054277">Regional variation beyond Europe–U.S. comparisons (e.g., Australia, Canada, Japan, Asia) was not captured in the retrieved evidence; registry-based studies from these regions would be needed to generalize geographic consistency.</cite>

📊 Content Rubrics (6)
Magnitude of Increased Risk Weight: 3 high
The answer should state that SLE patients have a significantly higher incidence of cardiovascular events, quantifying the risk as approximately 2- to 4-fold (or 200-300%) higher than age- and sex-matched general populations.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Traditional Risk Factors Weight: 3 high
The answer must list traditional cardiovascular risk factors that contribute to the excess risk, specifically mentioning hypertension, dyslipidemia (or hyperlipidemia), and smoking.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Role of Corticosteroids Weight: 3 high
The answer should identify the use of corticosteroids (or glucocorticoids) as a significant factor contributing to cardiovascular risk in SLE patients.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Antiphospholipid Antibodies Weight: 3 high
The answer must mention antiphospholipid antibodies (aPL) or the presence of Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) as a specific immune-mediated driver of thrombotic or cardiovascular risk.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Chronic Inflammation and Disease Activity Weight: 3 high
The answer should explain that chronic systemic inflammation or high disease activity drives accelerated atherosclerosis, endothelial dysfunction, or vascular damage.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Lupus Nephritis or Organ Damage Weight: 3 medium
The answer should mention specific clinical manifestations such as lupus nephritis or accumulated organ damage (e.g., SLICC Damage Index) as factors associated with increased cardiovascular events.
Source: Answer 2, Answer 3

🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - Outcomes: high-level skin clearance (typically defined as PASI 90/100 or body surface area [BSA] 90) in phase III moderate-to-severe psoriasis. - Safety: rates of candidiasis and inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) events. - Comparators: IL-17 inhibitors vs IL-23 inhibitors (including biologics like secukinumab, ixekizumab, brodalumab vs. nemolizumab, guselkumab, tildrakizumab; also note oral small-molecule IL-23/17 inhibitors like upadacitinib/abrocitinib may be included but focus on IL-23 class). Assumptions: - Phase III includes pivotal trials (e.g., NORPAS for nemolizumab vs IL-17; psoriasis PEARL program for guselkumab; VOYAGE 1/2 for tildrakizumab; and comparative analyses). - Need head-to-head or indirect comparisons on PASI90/100 and safety (candidiasis, IBD). Search plan: 1) Start with PubMed for comparative phase III evidence on PASI90/100 between IL-17 and IL-23 inhibitors (secukinumab/ixekizumab/brodalumab vs guselkumab/nemolizumab/tildrakizumab), prioritizing RCTs and meta-analyses. 2) Then search specifically for safety outcomes: candidiasis and IBD events in these phase III trials. 3) If needed, use Google to find regulatory documents or trial registry pages for detailed adverse event rates. 4) Browse key trial pages (e.g., NORPAS, PEARL, VOYAGE) for exact numbers. First query: "psoriasis phase III IL-17 vs IL-23 PASI 90 randomized trial"

Showing top 2: <snippet id="31402114">Title: Guselkumab versus secukinumab for the treatment of moderate-to-severe psoriasis (ECLIPSE): results from a phase 3, randomised controlled trial. Authors: Reich, Kristian, Armstrong, April W, Langley, Richard G et al. | Year: 2019 | Journal: Lancet (London, England) Abstract: BACKGROUND Antibodies targeting interleukin (IL)-23 and IL-17A effectively treat moderate-to-severe psoriasis. ECLIPSE is the first comparator study of an IL-23p19 inhibitor, guselkumab, versus an IL-17A inhibitor, secukinumab. The primary objective of this study was to show superiority of clinical response at week 48 for guselkumab versus secukinumab. METHODS In this phase 3, multicentre, double-blind, randomised, comparator-controlled trial at 142 outpatient clinical sites in nine countries (Australia, Canada, Czech Republic, France, Germany, Hungary, Poland, Spain, and the USA), eligible patients were aged 18 years or older, had moderate-to-severe plaque-type psoriasis, and were candidates for phototherapy or systemic therapy. Eligible patients were randomly assigned with permuted block randomisation using an interactive web response system to receive either guselkumab (100 mg at weeks 0 and 4 then every 8 weeks) or secukinumab (300 mg at weeks 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4, and then every 4 weeks). The primary endpoint, the proportion of patients in the intention-to-treat population who achieved 90% reduction or more from baseline of Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI 90 response) at week 48, and major secondary endpoints (the proportions of patients in the guselkumab group and in the secukinumab group who achieved a PASI 75 response at both weeks 12 and 48, a PASI 90 response at week 12, a PASI 75 response at week 12, a PASI 100 response at week 48, an Investigator's Global Assessment [IGA] score of 0 [cleared] at week 48, and an IGA score of 0 or 1 [minimal] at week 48) were to be tested in a fixed sequence to contr ... 🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - The user asks for head-to-head comparisons in phase III moderate-to-severe psoriasis between IL-17 inhibitors and IL-23 inhibitors on two outcomes: high-level skin clearance (PASI 90/100, IGA 0) and adverse events (candidiasis, inflammatory bowel disease). - Key phase III head-to-head trials include ECLIPSE (guselkumab vs secukinumab) and IXORA (ixekizumab vs ustekinumab). Additional IL-23p19 trials (biologics congress/NEJM) compare guselkumab or tildrakizumab vs IL-17 agents (secukinumab/ixekizumab), but we need to ensure they are phase III and report the requested AE signals. Plan: 1) Retrieve ECLIPSE (Lancet 2019) for efficacy (PASI 90/100, IGA 0) and safety including candidiasis/IBD. 2) Retrieve IXORA-S (BJD 2017) for efficacy and safety, focusing on candidiasis and GI/IBD events. 3) Find a phase III trial of another IL-23p19 (tildrakizumab) vs IL-17 (secukinumab/ixekizumab) to corroborate patterns (e.g., biologics congress/NEJM) and extract AE data on candidiasis/IBD. 4) If needed, search for post-hoc or safety analyses detailing fungal infections (candidiasis) and inflammatory bowel disease events. First query already run returned ECLIPSE and IXORA-S. Next, I will search for tildrakizumab phase III vs secukinumab/ixekizumab with safety terms. Next query: “tildrakizumab phase 3 secukinumab randomized psoriasis PASI 90 safety candidiasis inflammatory bowel disease”

Showing top 1: <snippet id="32600737">Title: Short-term effectiveness of biologics in patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis: A systematic review and network meta-analysis. Authors: Tada, Yayoi, Watanabe, Rei, Noma, Hisashi et al. | Year: 2020 | Journal: Journal of dermatological science Abstract: BACKGROUND Complete lesion clearance is important to patients with psoriasis. OBJECTIVE To conduct a network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials of biologic agents available for psoriasis in Japan, using mixed-treatment comparisons. METHODS MEDLINE and EMBASE were searched to identify randomized clinical trials (placebo-controlled or head-to-head) of infliximab, adalimumab, ustekinumab, secukinumab, ixekizumab, brodalumab, risankizumab or guselkumab in adult patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis published in English between 01 January 2000 and 31 August 2019. We assessed the proportion of patients who achieved a 100 %, 90 % and 75 % reduction in their Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) score (PASI100, PASI90 and PASI75) at 10, 12 or 16 weeks after starting biologic treatment, using contrast-based network meta-analysis methods and risk difference (RD). Probabilities of rank and surface under the cumulative ranking (SUCRA) were also estimated. RESULTS Data were pooled from 41 trials in 19,248 patients. All biologics were significantly more effective than placebo for PASI100, PASI90 and PASI75. The RD for PASI100 for brodalumab vs ixekizumab was 0.05 (95 % Confidence intervals [CI] -0.02, 0.11), brodalumab vs risankizumab was 0.04 (95 %CI -0.03, 0.11), and risankizumab vs ixekizumab was -0.01 (95 %CI -0.08, 0.06). The SUCRA for PASI100 and PASI90 achievement was 96.8 % and 86.8 %, respectively, for brodalumab, 82.6 % and 90.3 %, respectively for risankizumab, and 78.3 %, 80.9 %, respectively, for ixekizumab. CONCLUSION Of the biologics assessed, brodalumab, ixekizumab and risankizumab were the greatest ra ... [truncated]

✅ Final Answer Step 33
# Summary of Evidence and Key Findings - High-level skin clearance (PASI 90/100, IGA 0/1) in phase III moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis shows that IL-23–targeted therapy (guselkumab) achieved superior week-48 PASI 90 rates versus the IL-17A inhibitor secukinumab (84% vs 70%; p<0.0001), with non-inferiority but not superiority for PASI 75 sustained response (p=0.0616) and broadly consistent safety profiles across major endpoints in ECLIPSE, a randomized, phase 3, multicenter, double-blind, comparator-controlled trial across nine countries (N=1048) <cite id="31402114">Guselkumab achieved a higher proportion of PASI 90 responders at week 48 than secukinumab (84% vs 70%), with non-inferiority but not superiority for sustained PASI 75 and similar overall adverse event rates and safety findings consistent with registrational trials</cite>. This indicates that in head-to-head phase 3 settings, IL-23 inhibition can outperform IL-17A inhibition for maximal skin clearance endpoints at 1–2 years. - Additional phase 3 head-to-head data (IXORA-S) comparing an IL-17A inhibitor (ixekizumab) to an IL-12/23 inhibitor (ustekinumab) showed ixekizumab's superiority for PASI 90 at week 12 (72.8% vs 42.2%; difference 32.1%, 95% CI 19.8–44.5%) and persistence of higher response rates up to week 24 across PASI, sPGA, and DLQI, with no difference in overall adverse event incidence, underscoring that IL-17A blockade can also deliver rapid and robust skin clearance depending on the comparator and context <cite id="28542874">Ixekizumab was superior to ustekinumab for PASI 90 at week 12 (72.8% vs 42.2%) and maintained higher response rates at week 24, with no significant difference in overall adverse events</cite>. - A systematic review and network meta-analysis pooling 41 randomized trials (n=19,248) across biologics (including IL-17 and IL-23/IL-23p19 agents) concluded that all agents exceeded placebo for PASI90/100, and that brodalumab, ixekizumab, and risankizumab had the greatest probabilities of achieving PASI90/100 in the induction phase, suggesting class-level efficacy differences and speed of onset that may favor IL-17A inhibitors for early maximal response in some regimens <cite id="32600737">All biologics outperformed placebo for PASI90/100; brodalumab, risankizumab, and ixekizumab had the highest probabilities of achieving PASI90/100 in induction-phase analyses</cite>. - Safety signals specific to the user's question (candidiasis and inflammatory bowel disease) were not directly quantified in the retrieved phase 3 head-to-head trials; however, ECLIPSE reported similar overall adverse event, infection, and serious adverse event rates between guselkumab and secukinumab, consistent with prior registrational observations, and IXORA-S found no difference in overall adverse event incidence between ixekizumab and ustekinumab up to 24 weeks <cite id="31402114">ECLIPSE found similar proportions of adverse events, infections, and serious adverse events between guselkumab and secukinumab, consistent with registrational safety</cite> <cite id="28542874">IXORA-S showed no difference in overall adverse event incidence between ixekizumab and ustekinumab up to 24 weeks</cite>. The network meta-analysis did not isolate candidiasis or inflammatory bowel disease specifically, and no retrieved phase 3 IL-23 vs IL-17 comparisons provided explicit rates for these events, indicating a gap in directly comparable, event-specific safety data across classes in phase 3 trials <cite id="32600737">The network meta-analysis focused on PASI endpoints and did not report class-specific candidiasis or inflammatory bowel disease event rates</cite>. # Direct Comparison: IL-17 vs IL-23 Inhibitors - For high-level skin clearance, IL-23–targeted therapy (guselkumab, IL-23p19) achieved significantly higher PASI 90 rates at 48 weeks than IL-17A (secukinumab), suggesting that IL-23 inhibition may provide superior long-term clearance in this phase 3 head-to-head context <cite id="31402114">Guselkumab achieved higher PASI 90 at week 48 than secukinumab (84% vs 70%)</cite>. In contrast, another phase 3 head-to-head (IXORA-S) favored an IL-17A inhibitor (ixekizumab) over an IL-12/23 inhibitor (ustekinumab) for PASI 90 at week 12 and maintained higher response rates through week 24, showing that IL-17A blockade can also deliver rapid and durable clearance depending on the comparator and dosing schedule <cite id="28542874">Ixekizumab outperformed ustekinumab on PASI 90 at week 12 and across multiple endpoints at week 24</cite>. A broader network meta-analysis supports that several IL-17A agents (brodalumab, risankizumab, ixekizumab) have high probabilities of achieving PASI90/100 early, reinforcing that IL-17A blockade can be highly effective for rapid clearance in some regimens <cite id="32600737">Brodalumab, risankizumab, and ixekizumab had the greatest probabilities of PASI90/100 achievement in induction phase analyses</cite>. - Head-to-head phase 3 data are limited and class-specific; guselkumab (IL-23p19) vs secukinumab (IL-17A) showed IL-23p19 superiority for PASI 90 at 48 weeks, whereas ixekizumab (IL-17A) vs ustekinumab (IL-12/23) showed IL-17A superiority for PASI 90 at 12 weeks. This suggests that neither class is uniformly superior for maximal clearance; efficacy may depend on agent-specific pharmacology, dosing kinetics, and patient population, with IL-23p19 potentially favoring sustained long-term clearance and some IL-17A agents favoring rapid onset of maximal response <cite id="31402114">Guselkumab vs secukinumab: higher PASI 90 at week 48 with guselkumab</cite> <cite id="28542874">Ixekizumab vs ustekinumab: higher PASI 90 at week 12 with ixekizumab and sustained advantages through week 24</cite> <cite id="32600737">Network meta-analysis indicates top IL-17A agents for induction-phase PASI90/100</cite>. # Candidiasis and Inflammatory Bowel Disease Events - The ECLIPSE trial reported similar overall adverse event, infection, and serious adverse event rates between IL-23p19 (guselkumab) and IL-17A (secukinumab), consistent with prior registrational safety findings, but did not provide specific candidiasis event rates or detailed inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) event monitoring in its abstract <cite id="31402114">ECLIPSE reported similar proportions of adverse events, infections, and serious adverse events between guselkumab and secukinumab, consistent with registrational safety</cite>. The IXORA-S trial likewise reported no difference in overall adverse event incidence between ixekizumab and ustekinumab up to 24 weeks, without providing class-specific candidiasis or IBD event rates <cite id="28542874">No difference in overall adverse events between ixekizumab and ustekinumab up to week 24</cite>. - A broader network meta-analysis did not report candidiasis or IBD event rates; it focused on PASI endpoints and comparative efficacy versus placebo, leaving a gap in directly comparable phase 3 IL-23 vs IL-17 data for fungal infections (e.g., candidiasis) or IBD events <cite id="32600737">The network meta-analysis focused on PASI75/90/100 and did not report candidiasis or IBD event rates</cite>. # Practical Interpretation - For high-level skin clearance, IL-23–targeted therapy (e.g., guselkumab) demonstrated superior PASI 90 achievement at 48 weeks compared with an IL-17A inhibitor (secukinumab) in a phase 3 head-to-head trial, whereas another phase 3 trial favored an IL-17A inhibitor (ixekizumab) over an IL-12/23 inhibitor (ustekinumab) for early and sustained response, indicating that both classes can achieve high-level clearance and that superiority may depend on agent and timing <cite id="31402114">Guselkumab outperformed secukinumab for PASI 90 at week 48</cite> <cite id="28542874">Ixekizumab outperformed ustekinumab for PASI 90 at week 12 and maintained higher response rates through week 24</cite> <cite id="32600737">Top IL-17A agents showed high induction-phase probabilities for PASI90/100</cite>. - Regarding safety, available phase 3 head-to-head trials did not show a consistent class-wide increase in candidiasis or IBD events for either IL-23 or IL-17 inhibitors, and overall adverse event rates were similar between agents in ECLIPSE and IXORA-S; however, explicit candidiasis or IBD event rates were not reported in these summaries, highlighting a need for postmarketing or pooled safety analyses focused on these adverse events across IL-23 and IL-17 biologics in psoriasis <cite id="31402114">Similar overall adverse events and infections between guselkumab and secukinumab</cite> <cite id="28542874">No difference in overall adverse events between ixekizumab and ustekinumab up to 24 weeks</cite> <cite id="32600737">No candidiasis or IBD event-specific data in the network meta-analysis</cite>. # Bottom Line - High-level skin clearance: In phase 3 head-to-head trials, IL-23–targeted therapy (guselkumab) achieved higher PASI 90 at 48 weeks than IL-17A (secukinumab), while another IL-17A agent (ixekizumab) outperformed an IL-12/23 inhibitor (ustekinumab) for PASI 90 at 12 weeks and maintained advantages through week 24; thus, both classes can reach high-level clearance, with IL-23p19 showing sustained superiority in one trial and rapid/high efficacy for some IL-17A agents in another <cite id="31402114">Guselkumab vs secukinumab: higher PASI 90 at week 48</cite> <cite id="28542874">Ixekizumab vs ustekinumab: higher PASI 90 at week 12 and sustained benefits through week 24</cite> <cite id="32600737">Induction-phase analyses favor top IL-17A agents for PASI90/100</cite>. - Candidiasis/IBD events: Available phase 3 head-to-head trials did not report specific candidiasis or IBD event rates and found similar overall adverse event profiles between IL-23 and IL-17 inhibitors in the cited trials; therefore, within the retrieved evidence, no consistent difference in candidiasis or IBD event rates between IL-23 and IL-17 inhibitors can be concluded, and targeted postmarketing safety comparisons would be needed to answer this more precisely <cite id="31402114">ECLIPSE: similar overall adverse events and infections between guselkumab and secukinumab</cite> <cite id="28542874">IXORA-S: no difference in overall adverse events between ixekizumab and ustekinumab up to 24 weeks</cite> <cite id="32600737">Network meta-analysis did not report candidiasis or IBD event rates</cite>. # Evidence Gaps and What Would Resolve Them - Direct, pooled phase 3 comparisons of IL-23 vs IL-17 inhibitors reporting candidiasis and IBD event rates (including serious fungal infections and hospitalization for IBD) would resolve uncertainty. Post hoc analyses from ECLIPSE and other phase 3 trials, or a network/meta-analysis focused on safety endpoints, would be informative. Until then, conclusions on candidiasis/IBD comparative risk must be cautious and agent-specific rather than class-wide <cite id="31402114">ECLIPSE did not report candidiasis or IBD event rates</cite> <cite id="28542874">IXORA-S did not report candidiasis or IBD event rates</cite> <cite id="32600737">Network meta-analysis focused on efficacy endpoints</cite>.

This indicates that in head-to-head phase 3 settings, IL-23 inhibition can outperform IL-17A inhibition for maximal skin clearance endpoints at 1–2 years. - Additional phase 3 head-to-head data (IXORA-S) comparing an IL-17A inhibitor (ixekizumab) to an IL-12/23 inhibitor (ustekinumab) showed ixekizumab's superiority for PASI 90 at week 12 (72.8% vs 42.2%; difference 32.1%, 95% CI 19.8–44.5%) and persistence of higher response rates up to week 24 across PASI, sPGA, and DLQI, with no difference in overall adverse event incidence, underscoring that IL-17A blockade can also deliver rapid and robust skin clearance depending on the comparator and context <cite id="28542874">Ixekizumab was superior to ustekinumab for PASI 90 at week 12 (72.8% vs 42.2%) and maintained higher response rates at week 24, with no significant difference in overall adverse events</cite>. - A systematic review and network meta-analysis pooling 41 randomized trials (n=19,248) across biologics (including IL-17 and IL-23/IL-23p19 agents) concluded that all agents exceeded placebo for PASI90/100, and...

📊 Content Rubrics (6)
General Prevalence of CMD Weight: 3 high
The answer should state that the prevalence of coronary microvascular dysfunction (or endothelial dysfunction) in patients with ANOCA/INOCA is high, typically estimated between 50% and two-thirds (66%).
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Association with Adverse Outcomes Weight: 3 high
The answer must mention that CMD is independently associated with adverse long-term cardiovascular prognosis, including increased risks of myocardial infarction, mortality, or cardiovascular death.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Impact on Morbidity and Quality of Life Weight: 3 high
The answer should note that CMD is linked to increased morbidity, such as recurrent angina, repeat hospitalizations, or reduced quality of life.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Specific Prevalence of Endothelium-Independent CMD Weight: 3 medium
The answer should include specific data indicating that endothelium-independent CMD affects approximately 24% to 32% of these patients (referencing recent studies like Nogami et al., 2025).
Source: Answer 1, Answer 3
Prognostic Significance of Symptom Type Weight: 3 medium
The answer should highlight that patients with exertional angina/chest pain tend to have worse microvascular function or a higher risk of adverse events compared to those with resting pain.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 3
Progression to Obstructive Disease Weight: 3 medium
The answer should mention the risk of progression from non-obstructive to obstructive coronary artery disease as part of the long-term prognosis.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3

#30 pubmed_traj_00086 mechanism

📋 Question

What cellular and extracellular matrix processes drive atrial fibrosis and how do these processes contribute to atrial fibrillation recurrence after catheter ablation?
Topic: Atrial fibrillation
Key PMIDs: 21489023, 38643140

🔄 Interleaved Text (Model Reasoning Trajectory)

🧠 Think: 3 🔧 Tool Calls: 15 📄 Tool Outputs: 15 📏 Total Length: 26001 chars ⚠️ Errors: 2
⚠️ Critical Errors Detected MOSTLY_EMPTY + REPEATED(2)
Tool #2 REPEATED_QUERY, CONSECUTIVE_EMPTY Severity: 8 PRE: 2, POST: 3
Tool #3 NO_STRATEGY_CHANGE Severity: 7 PRE: 3, POST: 4
🧠 Think Step 1
Decompose the question: - Cellular processes: atrial myocyte injury and Ca2+-overload triggering injury response; fibroblast activation into myofibroblasts (CAFs); recruitment (bone marrow–derived cells, EMT); epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) from resident cells; inflammatory macrophage polarization and cytokine signaling (TGF-β, Wnt/β-catenin, Notch, BMP); mast cells; endothelial dysfunction and neovascularization. - Extracellular matrix (ECM) processes: collagen overproduction/deposition, crosslinking; matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and TIMPs imbalance; fibronectin, tenascin-C, thrombospondin-1; integrins and mechanotransduction; increased tissue stiffness and altered ECM mechanics; adhesion changes. - Link to AF recurrence after catheter ablation: scar formation vs. healing/remodeling; substrate heterogeneity and conduction anomalies; reentry and ectopic foci; epicardial/recurrent pulmonary vein PVs; fibroinflammatory barriers; neointimal fibrosis at ablation lesions; stretch/flow/ionic remodeling interactions. Search plan (broad → specific): 1) Broad reviews on atrial fibrosis mechanisms and AF (2018–2025) to frame cellular/ECM processes. 2) Specific evidence on myocyte injury/Ca2+-overload and injury response in atrium. 3) Fibroblast activation/CAF recruitment and EMT in human atria and AF; TGF-β, Wnt/β-catenin signaling. 4) ECM remodeling: MMP/TIMP, collagen/crosslinking, stiffness, fibronectin/tenascin-C. 5) AF recurrence after ablation: fibrosis/scar remodeling, PV reconnection, substrate drivers of recurrence; neointimal fibrosis at ablation lines. First query: broad review terms.

🧠 Think Step 4
Decompose the question: - We need mechanistic cellular and extracellular matrix (ECM) processes that drive atrial fibrosis, and how these processes promote atrial fibrillation (AF) recurrence after catheter ablation. - Likely mechanisms: fibroblast activation and myofibroblast differentiation; epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) of cardiac cells; inflammatory signaling (TGF-β, Wnt/β-catenin, NF-κB, Notch, BMP/Smad); oxidative stress; Ca2+/PKC signaling; ECM remodeling (collagen I/III, fibronectin, tenascin-C), matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), integrins, and mechanical stretch/trophic remodeling. - Post-ablation recurrence mechanisms: electro-anatomic scar healing (fibrosis quality), substrate heterogeneity and conduction anisotropy, re-entrant circuits, autonomic remodeling, and inflammatory/EMT reactivation near ablation lesions. Plan: 1) Start broad with recent reviews on atrial fibrosis mechanisms and AF (2018–2025) to anchor core pathways. 2) Retrieve evidence on cellular drivers (fibroblasts, EMT, inflammatory signaling) in human atria and AF. 3) Retrieve ECM remodeling specifics (collagen cross-linking, MMPs, integrins, mechanotransduction). 4) Retrieve ablation-specific literature on scar/fibrosis quality, predictors of recurrence, and mechanistic links (EMT/reactivation, stretch-induced fibrogenesis, oxidative stress). 5) Add evidence on autonomic/neurohormonal remodeling and epicardial vs endocardial differences. First query returned 0 results, likely due to too many terms. I will simplify to core terms: "atrial fibrosis mechanisms review fibroblast TGF-beta 2018..2025".

Decompose the question: - We need mechanistic cellular and extracellular matrix (ECM) processes that drive atrial fibrosis, and how these processes promote atrial fibrillation (AF) recurrence after catheter ablation. - Likely mechanisms: fibroblast activation/epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), inflammatory signaling (TGF-β, Wnt/β-catenin, Notch, NF-κB), oxidative stress, autophagy, calcium handling, endothelial dysfunction, and ECM remodeling (collagen I/III, fibronectin, tenascin-C, LOX/LOXL crosslinking, MMPs/TIMPs). Post-ablation: inflammatory response, myelination, neointimal fibrosis, scar maturation, substrate heterogeneity, conduction slowing, reentry. Plan: 1) Find recent reviews on atrial fibrosis mechanisms (fibroblasts, EMT, ECM composition, signaling pathways) 2018–2025. 2) Retrieve mechanistic/quantitative evidence linking TGF-β/EMT/fibronectin/collagen remodeling to conduction/remodeling in atria. 3) Find studies on AF substrate and ablation outcomes: fibrosis, neointimal scar, myelinated fibrosis, macrophage/immune remodeling, and recurrence risk. 4) Look for interventional evidence: anti-fibrotic strategies (e.g., pirfenidone/nintedanib, ROCK inhibitors, MMP modulation) affecting AF recurrence. First query returned 0 results; likely too broad with multiple terms. I will narrow to core terms: "atrial fibrosis mechanisms review fibroblast TGF-beta 2018..2025".

Found 45 results. Showing top 5: <snippet id="35006336">Title: Late arrhythmia recurrence after atrial fibrillation ablation: incidence, mechanisms and clinical implications. Authors: Erhard, Nico, Metzner, Andreas, Fink, Thomas | Year: 2022 | Journal: Herzschrittmachertherapie & Elektrophysiologie Abstract: BACKGROUND AND OBJECTIVES Catheter ablation of atrial fibrillation (AF) has become a well-established and widely used therapy, with pulmonary vein isolation (PVI) being the key modality of ablation. However, arrhythmia recurrences after PVI are common, with a relevant number of patients undergoing repeat ablation. Arrhythmia recurrence after PVI may vary regarding time point and mode of recurrence. While early arrhythmia recurrences of AF after PVI are mostly found to be the product of electrical reconnection of the pulmonary veins, the exact mechanisms of very late arrhythmia recurrence, occurring later than 12 months after successful PVI, remain unclear. This review provides an overview on the current evidence on time point and mechanisms of arrhythmia recurrence after PVI focussing on late arrhythmia recurrence. RECENT FINDINGS The incidence of late arrhythmia recurrence after PVI can lie at a rate of up to 30% according to long-term follow-up studies. Mechanisms of recurrence include electrical reconnection of previously isolated pulmonary veins and development of atrial fibrosis. The use of cryoballoon ablation is likely to be more effective in reducing late arrhythmia recurrences compared to radiofrequency ablation. Novel scores such as the MB-LATER score or the APPLE score may become useful tools in predicting arrhythmia recurrence after PVI. RESULTS AND CONCLUSION Late arrhythmia recurrence after PVI is common and leads to a relevant impairment of long-term success. Relevant data are currently limited and exact mechanisms of arrhythmia recurrence remain unclear. Further studies are needed to elucidate pathogenetic mechanisms of late a ... [truncated]

✅ Final Answer Step 34
# Summary of cellular and extracellular matrix processes driving atrial fibrosis and their impact on AF recurrence after catheter ablation ## Cellular processes that drive atrial fibrosis Atrial fibrosis is driven by activation of cardiac fibroblasts and their differentiation into myofibroblasts, which increases collagen synthesis and secretion of extracellular matrix proteins. Key pro-fibrotic signaling pathways include transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β), particularly TGF-β1, which upregulates collagen types I and III and inhibits matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) while stimulating their tissue inhibitors (TIMPs), favoring net collagen accumulation. Crosstalk with inflammatory cytokines, oxidative stress, calcium overload, and autonomic nervous system imbalance further amplifies fibroblast activation and myofibroblast transition. MicroRNAs also modulate these processes by repressing anti-fibrotic targets such as connective tissue growth factor (CTGF) and collagen-degrading enzymes. These pathways are intertwined with atrial remodeling that sustains atrial fibrillation (AF) by creating a stiffened, electrically heterogeneous substrate conducive to reentry. <cite id="25661032">Oxidative stress, atrial dilatation, calcium overload, inflammation, microRNAs, and myofibroblast activation are all thought to be involved in AF-induced atrial remodeling</cite> <cite id="25661032">Some pathways have fast kinetics (e.g., oxidative stress-mediated shortening of refractory period), while others depend on slower molecular processes (e.g., transcriptional changes in ion channels and fibroblast activation)</cite> <cite id="41511320">Key molecular pathways identified include structural remodeling mediated by fibrosis involving TGF-β1 and matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), inflammatory pathways, autonomic imbalance, and genetic/epigenetic alterations</cite>. ## Extracellular matrix remodeling in atrial fibrosis Extracellular matrix remodeling features excessive deposition of interstitial collagen, increased fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transition, and altered turnover regulated by MMP/TIMP imbalance. TGF-β1 signaling promotes fibronectin and collagen deposition while suppressing MMPs, leading to a fibrotic scar with disorganized matrix. Over time, this creates a stiffened atrial substrate with electrical and structural heterogeneity that supports reentrant arrhythmias. Reviews emphasize that structural remodeling—represented by atrial fibrosis—is a central mechanism sustaining AF, with reentries occurring endocardially, epicardially, or intramurally, complicating substrate-based ablation. <cite id="25661032">Myofibroblast activation and related signaling contribute to atrial remodeling that perpetuates AF</cite> <cite id="30459630">Evidence for structural remodeling, mainly represented by atrial fibrosis, suggests reentrant activities using anatomical substrate are key mechanisms sustaining AF, with endocardial, epicardial, and intramural reentries</cite> <cite id="41511320">Structural remodeling mediated by fibrosis involving TGF-β1 and MMPs is a key pathway underlying AF recurrence post-ablation</cite>. ## Mechanisms of AF recurrence after catheter ablation AF recurs after ablation through a combination of electrical reconnection and substrate-driven reentry. Early recurrences are often due to pulmonary vein (PV) reconnection, whereas late recurrences (beyond 12 months) are increasingly attributed to progressive atrial substrate remodeling, particularly fibrosis, leading to macro-reentry in a structurally altered atrium. Reviews report late recurrence incidence up to 30% in long-term follow-up, with mechanisms including PV reconnection and development of atrial fibrosis; cryoballoon ablation may reduce late recurrences compared with radiofrequency, and predictive scores (e.g., MB-LATER, APPLE) are under evaluation. Persistent AF is less responsive to ablation because multiple reentrant foci and diffuse fibrotic substrate complicate identification and elimination of all drivers. <cite id="35006336">Late arrhythmia recurrence after pulmonary vein isolation can reach up to 30%; mechanisms include electrical reconnection of previously isolated PVs and development of atrial fibrosis, with cryoballoon ablation possibly more effective in reducing late recurrences</cite> <cite id="35006336">Repeat ablation is often required for recurrent AF, and exact mechanisms of very late recurrence remain unclear</cite> <cite id="30459630">Ablation of persistent AF remains less effective, mainly due to difficulty identifying sustaining sources; theories range from localized foci to diffuse bi-atrial wavelets, and structural remodeling (fibrosis) suggests reentrant activities using anatomical substrate are key mechanisms sustaining AF</cite>. ## How fibrosis contributes to ablation failure and recurrence Fibrosis alters atrial architecture and electrical properties by increasing collagen content and fibroblast density, which promotes conduction slowing, macro-reentry circuits, and electrical isolation of PVs, making reconnection less likely but also creating alternative reentrant pathways that are harder to map and ablate. As fibrosis progresses, PV isolation may become less effective at preventing reentrant circuits that utilize epicardial or intramural scar, leading to both early and late recurrences. Reviews highlight that early interventions to reverse remodeling and prevent fibrosis progression are critical to improve long-term ablation success. <cite id="30459630">Reentries can be endocardial, epicardial, and intramural, making them less accessible for mapping and ablation</cite> <cite id="35006336">Incidence of late arrhythmia recurrence after PVI can be up to 30%, with atrial fibrosis as a mechanism of recurrence</cite> <cite id="25661032">AF-induced remodeling involves fibroblast activation and inflammation, with slower kinetic pathways contributing to sustained substrate changes</cite>. ## Modifiable drivers and therapeutic implications Obesity and metabolic inflammation exacerbate atrial remodeling, including fibrosis, oxidative stress, and ion-channel alterations, and weight loss can reverse some changes and reduce AF recurrence after ablation. Anti-fibrotic strategies targeting TGF-β signaling, MMP/TIMP imbalance, inflammatory pathways, and autonomic dysfunction are under investigation, with biomarkers such as TGF-β1, MMPs, connexins, and novel markers (e.g., GDF-15, relaxin) proposed for risk stratification and personalized treatment. However, evidence remains limited by small, heterogeneous studies with insufficient long-term follow-up, underscoring the need for integrated mechanistic and imaging approaches to characterize fibrosis and guide precision ablation strategies. <cite id="38156451">Obesity-driven structural and electrical remodeling contribute to AF via adiposity, inflammation, fibrosis, oxidative stress, ion-channel alterations, and autonomic dysfunction; weight loss reverses these changes and reduces AF risk and recurrence after ablation</cite> <cite id="41511320">Critical gaps persist due to small, heterogeneous studies and insufficient long-term follow-up; future work should validate biomarkers such as TGF-β1, MMPs, connexins, and novel markers like GDF-15 and relaxin to improve patient selection and ablation strategies</cite>. ## Key takeaways - Cellular drivers: TGF-β1-mediated myofibroblast activation, inflammatory signaling, oxidative stress, calcium overload, autonomic imbalance, and microRNA regulation converge on increased collagen synthesis and disorganized extracellular matrix. <cite id="25661032">Oxidative stress, inflammation, microRNAs, and myofibroblast activation are implicated in AF-induced remodeling</cite> <cite id="41511320">TGF-β1 and MMPs are central to structural remodeling via fibrosis</cite>. - ECM changes: Excess collagen deposition, fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transition, MMP/TIMP imbalance, and fibronectin accumulation remodel the atrial substrate to support reentry and conduction disturbances. <cite id="25661032">Myofibroblast activation contributes to remodeling that perpetuates AF</cite> <cite id="30459630">Structural remodeling (fibrosis) underlies sustained AF via reentrant activity</cite>. - AF recurrence post-ablation: Early recurrences often reflect PV reconnection; late recurrences increasingly reflect progressive fibrosis-driven substrate remodeling, with reentries involving epicardial/intramural scar that are difficult to eradicate. <cite id="35006336">Late recurrence incidence up to 30% with mechanisms including PV reconnection and atrial fibrosis</cite> <cite id="30459630">Diffuse fibrotic substrate and multiple wavelets complicate ablation in persistent AF</cite>. - Clinical implications: Early intervention to mitigate fibrosis (e.g., addressing obesity, anti-inflammatory and anti-remodeling strategies, and biomarker-guided precision ablation) may reduce recurrence risk. <cite id="38156451">Weight loss reduces AF recurrence after ablation by reversing obesity-driven remodeling</cite> <cite id="41511320">Precision diagnostics and personalized interventions targeting fibrosis pathways are needed to reduce recurrence</cite>.

📊 Content Rubrics (5)
Role of MMPs and ECM Turnover Weight: 3 high
The answer should mention the role of elevated Matrix Metalloproteinases (specifically MMP-2 and MMP-9) and collagen turnover markers (specifically ICTP) in degrading ECM and driving fibrotic remodeling.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Inflammatory Biomarkers and Immune Response Weight: 3 high
The answer should identify inflammatory markers such as hs-CRP and IL-6 as key drivers that activate fibroblasts and promote ECM deposition, contributing to recurrence.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Genetic and lncRNA Regulation Weight: 3 medium
The answer should mention the involvement of specific genetic factors, particularly the lncRNA TMEM51-AS1-201 and its interaction with fibrosis-related genes like COL6A1 or HSPG2.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2
Structural Mechanism of Recurrence Weight: 3 high
The answer should explain that fibrosis and ECM remodeling create a structural substrate that disrupts atrial continuity, leading to electrical heterogeneity and re-entrant circuits.
Source: Answer 1, Answer 2, Answer 3
Signaling Pathways Involved Weight: 3 medium
The answer should reference signaling pathways enriched in recurrent AF, such as PI3K-Akt, MAPK (from Answer 2), or general fibrotic pathways like TGF-β (from Answer 3).
Source: Answer 2, Answer 3

Atrial fibrosis and extracellular matrix (ECM) remodeling are critical drivers of atrial fibrillation (AF) recurrence after catheter ablation. Key mechanisms include: 1. **ECM Turnover and Fibrosis**: - Elevated matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), particularly MMP-2 and MMP-9, degrade ECM components, while increased collagen synthesis (e.g., collagen type I and VI) disrupts atrial architecture. Replacement fibrosis disrupts atrial muscle continuity, creating arrhythmogenic substrates.
